[38;2;248;248;242m              ify how many components of the hostname are desired.  With a negative  in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              teger, trailing components of the hostname are shown.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %n     $USERNAME.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %y     The  line  (tty)  the  user is logged in on, without `/dev/' prefix.  This[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              does not treat `/dev/tty' names specially.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Shell state[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       %#     A `#' if the shell is running with privileges, a `%' if  not.   Equivalent[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to  `%(!.#.%%)'.   The  definition of `privileged', for these purposes, is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that either the effective user ID is zero, or,  if  POSIX.1e  capabilities[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              are supported, that at least one capability is raised in either the Effec‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tive or Inheritable capability vectors.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %?     The return status of the last command executed just before the prompt.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %_     The  status of the parser, i.e. the shell constructs (like `if' and `for')[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that have been started on the command line. If  given  an  integer  number[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that  many  strings  will be printed; zero or negative or no integer means[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              print as many as there are.  This is most useful in prompts PS2  for  con‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tinuation  lines and PS4 for debugging with the XTRACE option; in the lat‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ter case it will also work non-interactively.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %^     The status of the parser in reverse. This is the same as `%_'  other  than[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the order of strings.  It is often used in RPS2.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %d[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       %/     Current  working directory.  If an integer follows the `%', it specifies a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              number of trailing components of the current working  directory  to  show;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zero  means  the  whole path.  A negative integer specifies leading compo‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              nents, i.e. %-1d specifies the first component.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %~     As %d and %/, but if the current working directory starts with $HOME, that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              part is replaced by a `~'. Furthermore, if it has a named directory as its[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              prefix, that part is replaced by a `~' followed by the name of the  direc‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tory,  but  only  if the result is shorter than the full path; see Dynamic[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and Static named directories in zshexpn(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %e     Evaluation depth of the current sourced file,  shell  function,  or  eval.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  is  incremented  or decremented every time the value of %N is set or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              reverted to a previous value, respectively.  This is most useful  for  de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              bugging as part of $PS4.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %h[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       %!     Current history event number.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %i     The  line  number currently being executed in the script, sourced file, or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              shell function given by %N.  This is most useful for debugging as part  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              $PS4.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %I     The  line number currently being executed in the file %x.  This is similar[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to %i, but the line number is always a line number in the file  where  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              code was defined, even if the code is a shell function.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %j     The number of jobs.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %L     The current value of $SHLVL.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %N     The  name  of the script, sourced file, or shell function that zsh is cur‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rently executing, whichever was started most recently.  If there is  none,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              this  is equivalent to the parameter $0.  An integer may follow the `%' to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              specify a number of trailing path components to show; zero means the  full[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              path.  A negative integer specifies leading components.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %x     The  name of the file containing the source code currently being executed.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This behaves as %N except that function and eval  command  names  are  not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              shown, instead the file where they were defined.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %c[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       %.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       %C     Trailing  component of the current working directory.  An integer may fol‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              low the `%' to get more than one component.  Unless `%C'  is  used,  tilde[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              contraction  is  performed  first.   These are deprecated as %c and %C are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              equivalent to %1~ and %1/, respectively, while explicit positive  integers[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              have the same effect as for the latter two sequences.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Date and time[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       %D     The date in yy-mm-dd format.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %T     Current time of day, in 24-hour format.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %t[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       %@     Current time of day, in 12-hour, am/pm format.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %*     Current time of day in 24-hour format, with seconds.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %w     The date in day-dd format.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %W     The date in mm/dd/yy format.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %D{string}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              string is formatted using the strftime function.  See strftime(3) for more[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              details.   Various  zsh extensions provide numbers with no leading zero or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              space if the number is a single digit:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              %f     a day of the month[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %K     the hour of the day on the 24-hour clock[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %L     the hour of the day on the 12-hour clock[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              In addition, if the system supports the POSIX gettimeofday system call, %.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              provides decimal fractions of a second since the epoch  with  leading  ze‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              roes.   By default three decimal places are provided, but a number of dig‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              its up to 9 may be given following the %; hence %6.  outputs microseconds,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and %9. outputs nanoseconds.  (The latter requires a  nanosecond-precision[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              clock_gettime;  systems lacking this will return a value multiplied by the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              appropriate power of 10.)   A  typical  example  of  this  is  the  format[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `%D{%H:%M:%S.%.}'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The GNU extension %N is handled as a synonym for %9..[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Additionally,  the  GNU  extension that a `-' between the % and the format[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              character causes a leading zero or space to be  stripped  is  handled  di‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rectly  by  the shell for the format characters d, f, H, k, l, m, M, S and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              y; any other format characters are provided to  the  system's  strftime(3)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              with  any  leading `-' present, so the handling is system dependent.  Fur‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ther GNU (or other) extensions are also passed to strftime(3) and may work[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              if the system supports them.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Visual effects[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       %B (%b)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Start (stop) boldface mode.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %E     Clear to end of line.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %U (%u)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Start (stop) underline mode.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %S (%s)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Start (stop) standout mode.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %F (%f)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Start (stop) using a different foreground colour, if supported by the ter‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              minal.  The colour may be specified two ways: either as  a  numeric  argu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ment,  as normal, or by a sequence in braces following the %F, for example[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %F{red}.  In the latter case the values allowed are as described  for  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fg zle_highlight attribute; see Character Highlighting in zshzle(1).  This[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              means that numeric colours are allowed in the second format also.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %K (%k)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Start (stop) using a different bacKground colour.  The syntax is identical[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to that for %F and %f.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %{...%}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Include  a  string  as  a  literal escape sequence.  The string within the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              braces should not change the cursor position.  Brace pairs can nest.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              A positive numeric argument between the % and the  {  is  treated  as  de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              scribed for %G below.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %G     Within a %{...%} sequence, include a `glitch': that is, assume that a sin‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              gle  character width will be output.  This is useful when outputting char‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              acters that otherwise cannot be correctly handled by the  shell,  such  as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the alternate character set on some terminals.  The characters in question[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              can  be  included  within a %{...%} sequence together with the appropriate[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              number of %G sequences to indicate the correct width.  An integer  between[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  `%'  and  `G'  indicates  a  character  width  other than one.  Hence[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %{seq%2G%} outputs seq and assumes it takes up the width of  two  standard[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              characters.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Multiple uses of %G accumulate in the obvious fashion; the position of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %G is unimportant.  Negative integers are not handled.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note  that  when  prompt truncation is in use it is advisable to divide up[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              output into single characters within each %{...%} group so that  the  cor‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rect truncation point can be found.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mCONDITIONAL SUBSTRINGS IN PROMPTS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       %v     The  value  of  the first element of the psvar array parameter.  Following[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the `%' with an integer gives that element of the array.   Negative  inte‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              gers count from the end of the array.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %(x.true-text.false-text)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Specifies  a  ternary  expression.  The character following the x is arbi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              trary; the same character is used to separate the text for the `true'  re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sult  from  that for the `false' result.  This separator may not appear in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the true-text, except as part of a %-escape sequence.  A `)' may appear in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the false-text as `%)'.  true-text and false-text may both  contain  arbi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              trarily-nested escape sequences, including further ternary expressions.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The  left parenthesis may be preceded or followed by a positive integer n,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              which defaults to zero.  A negative integer will be multiplied by -1,  ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cept  as noted below for `l'.  The test character x may be any of the fol‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              lowing:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              !      True if the shell is running with privileges.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              #      True if the effective uid of the current process is n.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ?      True if the exit status of the last command was n.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              _      True if at least n shell constructs were started.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              C[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              /      True if the current absolute path has at least n elements  relative[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     to the root directory, hence / is counted as 0 elements.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              c[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              .[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ~      True  if  the current path, with prefix replacement, has at least n[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     elements relative to the root directory, hence / is  counted  as  0[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     elements.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              D      True if the month is equal to n (January = 0).[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              d      True if the day of the month is equal to n.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              e      True if the evaluation depth is at least n.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              g      True if the effective gid of the current process is n.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              j      True if the number of jobs is at least n.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              L      True if the SHLVL parameter is at least n.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              l      True if at least n characters have already been printed on the cur‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     rent  line.  When n is negative, true if at least abs(n) characters[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     remain before  the  opposite  margin  (thus  the  left  margin  for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     RPROMPT).[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              S      True if the SECONDS parameter is at least n.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              T      True if the time in hours is equal to n.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              t      True if the time in minutes is equal to n.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              v      True if the array psvar has at least n elements.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              V      True if element n of the array psvar is set and non-empty.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              w      True if the day of the week is equal to n (Sunday = 0).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %<string<[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       %>string>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       %[xstring][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Specifies  truncation  behaviour  for  the remainder of the prompt string.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The third, deprecated, form is equivalent to `%xstringx', i.e.  x  may  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `<'  or  `>'.  The string will be displayed in place of the truncated por‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion of any string; note this does not undergo prompt expansion.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The numeric argument, which in the third form may appear immediately after[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the `[', specifies the maximum permitted length  of  the  various  strings[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that can be displayed in the prompt.  In the first two forms, this numeric[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              argument  may  be  negative, in which case the truncation length is deter‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mined by subtracting the absolute value of the numeric argument  from  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              number  of  character  positions remaining on the current prompt line.  If[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              this results in a zero or negative length, a length  of  1  is  used.   In[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              other words, a negative argument arranges that after truncation at least n[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              characters remain before the right margin (left margin for RPROMPT).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The  forms with `<' truncate at the left of the string, and the forms with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `>' truncate at the right of the string.  For example, if the current  di‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rectory  is `/home/pike', the prompt `%8<..<%/' will expand to `..e/pike'.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              In this string, the terminating character (`<', `>' or `]'),  or  in  fact[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              any character, may be quoted by a preceding `\'; note when using print -P,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              however,  that this must be doubled as the string is also subject to stan‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              dard print processing, in addition to any backslashes removed by a  double[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              quoted string:  the worst case is therefore `print -P "%<\\\\<<..."'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  the string is longer than the specified truncation length, it will ap‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pear in full, completely replacing the truncated string.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The part of the prompt string to be truncated  runs  to  the  end  of  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              string,  or  to the end of the next enclosing group of the `%(' construct,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              or to the next truncation encountered at the  same  grouping  level  (i.e.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              truncations  inside a `%(' are separate), which ever comes first.  In par‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ticular, a truncation with argument zero (e.g., `%<<') marks  the  end  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  range of the string to be truncated while turning off truncation from[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              there on. For example, the prompt `%10<...<%~%<<%# ' will  print  a  trun‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cated  representation  of the current directory, followed by a `%' or `#',[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              followed by a space.  Without the `%<<', those two characters would be in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cluded in the string to be truncated.  Note that `%-0<<' is not equivalent[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to `%<<' but specifies that the prompt is truncated at the right margin.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Truncation applies only within each individual line of the prompt, as  de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              limited by embedded newlines (if any).  If the total length of any line of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  prompt after truncation is greater than the terminal width, or if the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              part to be truncated contains embedded newlines,  truncation  behavior  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              undefined  and  may  change  in  a  future  version  of  the  shell.   Use[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `%-n(l.true-text.false-text)' to remove  parts  of  the  prompt  when  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              available space is less than n.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mzsh 5.9                               May 14, 2022                            ZSHMISC(1)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────[0m
[38;2;248;248;242mZSHEXPN(1)                       General Commands Manual                      ZSHEXPN(1)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mNAME[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zshexpn - zsh expansion and substitution[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mDESCRIPTION[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The  following  types  of expansions are performed in the indicated order in five[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       steps:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       History Expansion[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This is performed only in interactive shells.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Alias Expansion[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Aliases are expanded immediately before the command line is parsed as  ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              plained under Aliasing in zshmisc(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Process Substitution[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Parameter Expansion[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Command Substitution[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Arithmetic Expansion[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Brace Expansion[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              These  five are performed in left-to-right fashion.  On each argument, any[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of the five steps that are needed  are  performed  one  after  the  other.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Hence, for example, all the parts of parameter expansion are completed be‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fore  command  substitution  is  started.  After these expansions, all un‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              quoted occurrences of the characters `\',`'' and `"' are removed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Filename Expansion[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If the SH_FILE_EXPANSION option is set, the order of expansion is modified[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for compatibility with sh and ksh.  In that  case  filename  expansion  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              performed immediately after alias expansion, preceding the set of five ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pansions mentioned above.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Filename Generation[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This expansion, commonly referred to as globbing, is always done last.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The following sections explain the types of expansion in detail.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mHISTORY EXPANSION[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       History expansion allows you to use words from previous command lines in the com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       mand  line  you are typing.  This simplifies spelling corrections and the repeti‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tion of complicated commands or arguments.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Immediately before execution, each command is saved in the history list, the size[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       of which is controlled by the HISTSIZE parameter.  The one most recent command is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       always retained in any case.  Each saved command in the history list is called  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       history  event  and  is  assigned a number, beginning with 1 (one) when the shell[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       starts up.  The history number that you may see in your prompt (see EXPANSION  OF[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       PROMPT  SEQUENCES in zshmisc(1)) is the number that is to be assigned to the next[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       command.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Overview[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       A history expansion begins with the first character of the  histchars  parameter,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       which  is  `!'  by default, and may occur anywhere on the command line, including[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       inside double quotes (but not inside single quotes '...' or C-style quotes $'...'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       nor when escaped with a backslash).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The first character is followed by an optional event designator (see the  section[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       `Event Designators') and then an optional word designator (the section `Word Des‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ignators');  if neither of these designators is present, no history expansion oc‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       curs.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Input lines containing history expansions are echoed after  being  expanded,  but[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       before any other expansions take place and before the command is executed.  It is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       this expanded form that is recorded as the history event for later references.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       History expansions do not nest.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       By default, a history reference with no event designator refers to the same event[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       as  any  preceding history reference on that command line; if it is the only his‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tory reference in a command, it refers to the previous command.  However, if  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       option  CSH_JUNKIE_HISTORY  is  set,  then  every history reference with no event[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       specification always refers to the previous command.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       For example, `!' is the event designator for the previous command, so `!!:1'  al‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ways refers to the first word of the previous command, and `!!$' always refers to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the  last  word of the previous command.  With CSH_JUNKIE_HISTORY set, then `!:1'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       and `!$' function in the same manner as `!!:1'  and  `!!$',  respectively.   Con‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       versely,  if  CSH_JUNKIE_HISTORY is unset, then `!:1' and `!$' refer to the first[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       and last words, respectively, of the same event referenced by the  nearest  other[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       history  reference preceding them on the current command line, or to the previous[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       command if there is no preceding reference.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The character sequence `^foo^bar' (where `^' is actually the second character  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the  histchars parameter) repeats the last command, replacing the string foo with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       bar.    More   precisely,   the   sequence   `^foo^bar^'   is   synonymous   with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       `!!:s^foo^bar^',  hence  other modifiers (see the section `Modifiers') may follow[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the final `^'.  In particular, `^foo^bar^:G' performs a global substitution.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       If the shell encounters the character sequence `!"' in  the  input,  the  history[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       mechanism  is  temporarily  disabled  until  the current list (see zshmisc(1)) is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       fully parsed.  The `!"' is removed from the input, and any subsequent `!' charac‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ters have no special significance.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       A less convenient but more comprehensible form of command history support is pro‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       vided by the fc builtin.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Event Designators[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       An event designator is a reference to a command-line entry in the  history  list.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       In  the  list below, remember that the initial `!' in each item may be changed to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       another character by setting the histchars parameter.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       !      Start a history expansion, except when followed by a blank,  newline,  `='[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              or  `('.   If  followed  immediately by a word designator (see the section[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `Word Designators'), this forms a history reference with no event designa‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tor (see the section `Overview').[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       !!     Refer to the previous command.  By itself, this expansion repeats the pre‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              vious command.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       !n     Refer to command-line n.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       !-n    Refer to the current command-line minus n.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       !str   Refer to the most recent command starting with str.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       !?str[?][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Refer to the most recent command containing str.  The trailing `?' is nec‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              essary if this reference is to be followed by a modifier  or  followed  by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              any text that is not to be considered part of str.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       !#     Refer to the current command line typed in so far.  The line is treated as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              if  it  were complete up to and including the word before the one with the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `!#' reference.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       !{...} Insulate a history reference from adjacent characters (if necessary).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Word Designators[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       A word designator indicates which word or words of a given command line are to be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       included in a history reference.  A `:' usually separates the event specification[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       from the word designator.  It may be omitted only if the word  designator  begins[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       with a `^', `$', `*', `-' or `%'.  Word designators include:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       0      The first input word (command).[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       n      The nth argument.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ^      The first argument.  That is, 1.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       $      The last argument.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       %      The word matched by (the most recent) ?str search.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       x-y    A range of words; x defaults to 0.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       *      All the arguments, or a null value if there are none.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       x*     Abbreviates `x-$'.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       x-     Like `x*' but omitting word $.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Note  that  a  `%'  word designator works only when used in one of `!%', `!:%' or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       `!?str?:%', and only when used after a !? expansion (possibly in an earlier  com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       mand).  Anything else results in an error, although the error may not be the most[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       obvious one.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Modifiers[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       After  the optional word designator, you can add a sequence of one or more of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       following modifiers, each preceded by a `:'.  These modifiers also  work  on  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       result of filename generation and parameter expansion, except where noted.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       a      Turn  a  file name into an absolute path:  prepends the current directory,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              if necessary; remove `.' path segments; and remove `..' path segments  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the segments that immediately precede them.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              This  transformation  is agnostic about what is in the filesystem, i.e. is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              on the logical, not the physical directory.  It takes place  in  the  same[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              manner as when changing directories when neither of the options CHASE_DOTS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              or  CHASE_LINKS  is  set.   For example, `/before/here/../after' is always[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              transformed to `/before/after', regardless of whether  `/before/here'  ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ists or what kind of object (dir, file, symlink, etc.) it is.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       A      Turn  a file name into an absolute path as the `a' modifier does, and then[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pass the result through the realpath(3) library function to  resolve  sym‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              bolic links.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note: on systems that do not have a realpath(3) library function, symbolic[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              links are not resolved, so on those systems `a' and `A' are equivalent.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note:  foo:A  and  realpath(foo)  are different on some inputs.  For real‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              path(foo) semantics, see the `P` modifier.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       c      Resolve a command name into an absolute path by searching the command path[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              given by the PATH variable.  This does not work  for  commands  containing[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              directory  parts.   Note  also  that  this does not usually work as a glob[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              qualifier unless a file of the same name is found in  the  current  direc‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tory.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       e      Remove  all  but the part of the filename extension following the `.'; see[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the definition of the filename extension in the description of the r modi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fier below.  Note that according to that definition  the  result  will  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              empty if the string ends with a `.'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       h [ digits ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Remove a trailing pathname component, shortening the path by one directory[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              level: this is the `head' of the pathname.  This works like `dirname'.  If[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  h  is followed immediately (with no spaces or other separator) by any[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              number of decimal digits,  and  the  value  of  the  resulting  number  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              non-zero,  that  number  of leading components is preserved instead of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              final component being removed.  In an absolute path the leading `/' is the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              first component,  so,  for  example,  if  var=/my/path/to/something,  then[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ${var:h3}  substitutes /my/path.  Consecutive `/'s are treated the same as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              a single `/'.  In parameter substitution, digits may only be used  if  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              expression  is  in  braces,  so  for  example  the short form substitution[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              $var:h2 is treated as ${var:h}2, not as ${var:h2}.  No restriction applies[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to the use of digits in history substitution or globbing  qualifiers.   If[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              more components are requested than are present, the entire path is substi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tuted  (so  this does not trigger a `failed modifier' error in history ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pansion).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       l      Convert the words to all lowercase.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       p      Print the new command but do not execute it.  Only works with history  ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pansion.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       P      Turn  a  file name into an absolute path, like realpath(3).  The resulting[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              path will be absolute, will refer to the same directory entry as the input[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              filename, and none of its components will be symbolic links  or  equal  to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `.' or `..'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Unlike  realpath(3),  non-existent  trailing  components are permitted and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              preserved.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       q      Quote the substituted words, escaping further substitutions.   Works  with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              history  expansion  and  parameter  expansion, though for parameters it is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              only useful if the resulting text is to be re-evaluated such as by eval.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Q      Remove one level of quotes from the substituted words.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       r      Remove a filename extension leaving the root name.  Strings with no  file‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              name extension are not altered.  A filename extension is a `.' followed by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              any number of characters (including zero) that are neither `.' nor `/' and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that  continue  to  the  end of the string.  For example, the extension of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `foo.orig.c' is `.c', and `dir.c/foo' has no extension.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       s/l/r[/][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Substitute r for l as described below.  The substitution is done only  for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  first string that matches l.  For arrays and for filename generation,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              this applies to each word of the expanded text.   See  below  for  further[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              notes on substitutions.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The  forms  `gs/l/r' and `s/l/r/:G' perform global substitution, i.e. sub‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              stitute every occurrence of r for l.  Note that the g or :G must appear in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              exactly the position shown.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              See further notes on this form of substitution below.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       &      Repeat the previous s substitution.  Like s, may be  preceded  immediately[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              by  a  g.   In parameter expansion the & must appear inside braces, and in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              filename generation it must be quoted with a backslash.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       t [ digits ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Remove all  leading  pathname  components,  leaving  the  final  component[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (tail).   This  works like `basename'.  Any trailing slashes are first re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              moved.  Decimal digits are handled as described above for (h), but in this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              case that number of trailing components is preserved instead  of  the  de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fault 1; 0 is treated the same as 1.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       u      Convert the words to all uppercase.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       x      Like  q, but break into words at whitespace.  Does not work with parameter[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              expansion.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The s/l/r/ substitution works as follows.  By default the left-hand side of  sub‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       stitutions are not patterns, but character strings.  Any character can be used as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the  delimiter in place of `/'.  A backslash quotes the delimiter character.  The[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       character `&', in the right-hand-side  r,  is  replaced  by  the  text  from  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       left-hand-side  l.   The  `&'  can be quoted with a backslash.  A null l uses the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       previous string either from the previous l or from the contextual scan  string  s[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       from  `!?s'.   You can omit the rightmost delimiter if a newline immediately fol‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       lows r; the rightmost `?' in a context scan can similarly be omitted.   Note  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       same record of the last l and r is maintained across all forms of expansion.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Note that if a `&' is used within glob qualifiers an extra backslash is needed as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       a & is a special character in this case.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Also  note  that  the  order of expansions affects the interpretation of l and r.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       When used in a history expansion, which occurs before any other expansions, l and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       r are treated as literal strings (except as explained for HIST_SUBST_PATTERN  be‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       low).   When  used  in parameter expansion, the replacement of r into the parame‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ter's value is done first, and then any additional process,  parameter,  command,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       arithmetic,  or  brace references are applied, which may evaluate those substitu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tions and expansions more than once if l appears more than once in  the  starting[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       value.   When  used in a glob qualifier, any substitutions or expansions are per‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       formed once at the time the qualifier is parsed, even before the `:s'  expression[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       itself is divided into l and r sides.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       If  the  option HIST_SUBST_PATTERN is set, l is treated as a pattern of the usual[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       form described in the section FILENAME GENERATION below.  This can be used in all[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the places where modifiers are available; note, however, that in globbing  quali‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       fiers  parameter  substitution  has already taken place, so parameters in the re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       placement string should be quoted to ensure they  are  replaced  at  the  correct[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       time.   Note  also that complicated patterns used in globbing qualifiers may need[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the extended glob qualifier notation (#q:s/.../.../) in order for  the  shell  to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       recognize the expression as a glob qualifier.  Further, note that bad patterns in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the  substitution  are  not subject to the NO_BAD_PATTERN option so will cause an[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       error.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       When HIST_SUBST_PATTERN is set, l may start with a # to indicate that the pattern[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       must match at the start of the string to be substituted, and a %  may  appear  at[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the start or after an # to indicate that the pattern must match at the end of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       string to be substituted.  The % or # may be quoted with two backslashes.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       For  example,  the  following  piece  of  filename  generation  code with the EX‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       TENDED_GLOB option:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              print -r -- *.c(#q:s/#%(#b)s(*).c/'S${match[1]}.C'/)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       takes the expansion of *.c and applies the glob qualifiers in the (#q...) expres‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       sion, which consists of a substitution modifier anchored to the start and end  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       each  word  (#%).  This turns on backreferences ((#b)), so that the parenthesised[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       subexpression is available in the replacement string  as  ${match[1]}.   The  re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       placement  string  is  quoted so that the parameter is not substituted before the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       start of filename generation.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The following f, F, w and W modifiers work  only  with  parameter  expansion  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       filename generation.  They are listed here to provide a single point of reference[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       for all modifiers.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       f      Repeats the immediately (without a colon) following modifier until the re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sulting word doesn't change any more.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       F:expr:[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Like  f,  but  repeats only n times if the expression expr evaluates to n.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Any character can be used instead of the `:'; if `(', `[', or `{' is  used[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              as  the  opening  delimiter,  the closing delimiter should be ')', `]', or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `}', respectively.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       w      Makes the immediately following modifier work on each word in the string.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       W:sep: Like w but words are considered to be the parts of  the  string  that  are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              separated  by  sep.  Any character can be used instead of the `:'; opening[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              parentheses are handled specially, see above.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mPROCESS SUBSTITUTION[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Each part of a command argument that  takes  the  form  `<(list)',  `>(list)'  or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       `=(list)'  is subject to process substitution.  The expression may be preceded or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       followed by other strings except that, to prevent clashes with commonly occurring[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       strings and patterns, the last form must occur at the start of  a  command  argu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ment,  and  the  forms are only expanded when first parsing command or assignment[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       arguments.  Process substitutions may be used following redirection operators; in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       this case, the substitution must appear with no trailing string.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Note that `<<(list)' is not a special syntax; it is equivalent  to  `<  <(list)',[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       redirecting  standard  input  from the result of process substitution.  Hence all[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the following documentation applies.  The second form (with the space) is  recom‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       mended for clarity.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       In  the  case  of the < or > forms, the shell runs the commands in list as a sub‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       process of the job executing the shell command line.  If the system supports  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       /dev/fd  mechanism,  the  command  argument is the name of the device file corre‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       sponding to a file descriptor; otherwise, if the system supports named pipes (FI‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       FOs), the command argument will be a named pipe.  If the form with > is  selected[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       then  writing  on  this  special file will provide input for list.  If < is used,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       then the file passed as an argument will be connected to the output of  the  list[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       process.  For example,[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              paste <(cut -f1 file1) <(cut -f3 file2) |[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tee >(process1) >(process2) >/dev/null[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       cuts  fields  1 and 3 from the files file1 and file2 respectively, pastes the re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       sults together, and sends it to the processes process1 and process2.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       If =(...) is used instead of <(...), then the file passed as an argument will  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the name of a temporary file containing the output of the list process.  This may[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       be  used instead of the < form for a program that expects to lseek (see lseek(2))[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       on the input file.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       There is an optimisation for substitutions of the form =(<<<arg), where arg is  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       single-word  argument  to  the here-string redirection <<<.  This form produces a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       file name containing the value of arg after  any  substitutions  have  been  per‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       formed.   This is handled entirely within the current shell.  This is effectively[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the reverse of the special form $(<arg) which treats arg as a file name  and  re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       places it with the file's contents.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The  =  form  is  useful as both the /dev/fd and the named pipe implementation of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       <(...) have drawbacks.  In the former case,  some  programmes  may  automatically[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       close  the  file  descriptor in question before examining the file on the command[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       line, particularly if this is necessary for security reasons  such  as  when  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       programme is running setuid.  In the second case, if the programme does not actu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ally  open  the  file,  the subshell attempting to read from or write to the pipe[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       will (in a typical implementation, different operating systems may have different[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       behaviour) block for ever and have to be killed explicitly.  In both  cases,  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       shell actually supplies the information using a pipe, so that programmes that ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       pect to lseek (see lseek(2)) on the file will not work.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Also note that the previous example can be more compactly and efficiently written[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       (provided the MULTIOS option is set) as:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              paste <(cut -f1 file1) <(cut -f3 file2) > >(process1) > >(process2)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The shell uses pipes instead of FIFOs to implement the latter two process substi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tutions in the above example.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       There  is  an additional problem with >(process); when this is attached to an ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ternal command, the parent shell does not wait for process to finish and hence an[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       immediately following command cannot rely on the  results  being  complete.   The[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       problem  and  solution  are  the same as described in the section MULTIOS in zsh‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       misc(1).  Hence in a simplified version of the example above:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              paste <(cut -f1 file1) <(cut -f3 file2) > >(process)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       (note that no MULTIOS are involved), process will be run asynchronously as far as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the parent shell is concerned.  The workaround is:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              { paste <(cut -f1 file1) <(cut -f3 file2) } > >(process)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The extra processes here are spawned from the parent shell which  will  wait  for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       their completion.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Another  problem arises any time a job with a substitution that requires a tempo‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       rary file is disowned by the shell, including the case where `&!' or `&|' appears[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       at the end of a command containing a substitution.  In that  case  the  temporary[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       file  will not be cleaned up as the shell no longer has any memory of the job.  A[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       workaround is to use a subshell, for example,[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              (mycmd =(myoutput)) &![0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       as the forked subshell will wait for the command to finish then remove the tempo‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       rary file.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       A general workaround to ensure a process substitution endures for an  appropriate[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       length  of  time  is  to pass it as a parameter to an anonymous shell function (a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       piece of shell code that is run immediately with function scope).   For  example,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       this code:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              () {[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                 print File $1:[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                 cat $1[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              } =(print This be the verse)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       outputs something resembling the following[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              File /tmp/zsh6nU0kS:[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This be the verse[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The  temporary  file created by the process substitution will be deleted when the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       function exits.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mPARAMETER EXPANSION[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The character `$' is used to introduce parameter expansions.  See zshparam(1) for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       a description of parameters, including arrays, associative arrays, and  subscript[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       notation to access individual array elements.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Note  in  particular the fact that words of unquoted parameters are not automati‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       cally split on whitespace unless the option SH_WORD_SPLIT is set; see  references[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       to  this  option  below  for  more details.  This is an important difference from[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       other shells.  However, as in other shells, null words are elided  from  unquoted[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       parameters' expansions.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       With default options, after the assignments:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              array=("first word" "" "third word")[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              scalar="only word"[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       then  $array  substitutes  two  words, `first word' and `third word', and $scalar[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       substitutes a single word `only word'.  Note that second  element  of  array  was[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       elided.   Scalar parameters can be elided too if their value is null (empty).  To[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       avoid elision, use quoting as follows: "$scalar" for scalars and "${array[@]}" or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       "${(@)array}" for arrays.  (The last two forms are equivalent.)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Parameter expansions can involve flags, as in `${(@kv)aliases}', and other opera‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tors, such  as  `${PREFIX:-"/usr/local"}'.   Parameter  expansions  can  also  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       nested.   These  topics will be introduced below.  The full rules are complicated[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       and are noted at the end.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       In the expansions discussed below that require a pattern, the form of the pattern[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       is the same as that used for filename generation; see the section `Filename  Gen‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       eration'.   Note that these patterns, along with the replacement text of any sub‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       stitutions, are themselves subject to parameter expansion, command  substitution,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       and  arithmetic  expansion.   In  addition to the following operations, the colon[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       modifiers described in the section `Modifiers' in the section `History Expansion'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       can be applied:  for example, ${i:s/foo/bar/} performs string substitution on the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       expansion of parameter $i.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       In the following descriptions, `word' refers to a single word substituted on  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       command line, not necessarily a space delimited word.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The  value,  if any, of the parameter name is substituted.  The braces are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              required if the expansion is to be followed by a letter, digit, or  under‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              score  that  is  not to be interpreted as part of name.  In addition, more[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              complicated forms  of  substitution  usually  require  the  braces  to  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              present; exceptions, which only apply if the option KSH_ARRAYS is not set,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              are a single subscript or any colon modifiers appearing after the name, or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              any of the characters `^', `=', `~', `#' or `+' appearing before the name,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              all of which work with or without braces.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  name is an array parameter, and the KSH_ARRAYS option is not set, then[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the value of each element of name is substituted, one  element  per  word.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Otherwise,  the  expansion results in one word only; with KSH_ARRAYS, this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is the first element of an array.  No field splitting is done on  the  re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sult  unless  the  SH_WORD_SPLIT  option is set.  See also the flags = and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              s:string:.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ${+name}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If name is the name of a set parameter `1' is substituted,  otherwise  `0'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is substituted.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name-word}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name:-word}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  name  is  set,  or in the second form is non-null, then substitute its[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              value; otherwise substitute word.  In the second form name may be omitted,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              in which case word is always substituted.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name+word}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name:+word}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If name is set, or in the second form is non-null, then  substitute  word;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              otherwise substitute nothing.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name=word}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name:=word}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name::=word}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              In  the  first  form,  if name is unset then set it to word; in the second[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              form, if name is unset or null then set it to word; and in the third form,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              unconditionally set name to word.  In all forms, the value of the  parame‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ter is then substituted.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name?word}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name:?word}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              In  the  first form, if name is set, or in the second form if name is both[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              set and non-null, then substitute its value;  otherwise,  print  word  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              exit from the shell.  Interactive shells instead return to the prompt.  If[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              word is omitted, then a standard message is printed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       In  any of the above expressions that test a variable and substitute an alternate[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       word, note that you can use standard shell quoting in the word  value  to  selec‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tively  override  the  splitting done by the SH_WORD_SPLIT option and the = flag,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       but not splitting by the s:string: flag.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       In the following expressions, when name is an array and the substitution  is  not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       quoted, or if the `(@)' flag or the name[@] syntax is used, matching and replace‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ment is performed on each array element separately.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name#pattern}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name##pattern}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If the pattern matches the beginning of the value of name, then substitute[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  value  of name with the matched portion deleted; otherwise, just sub‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              stitute the value of name.  In the first form, the smallest matching  pat‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tern  is  preferred;  in  the second form, the largest matching pattern is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              preferred.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name%pattern}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name%%pattern}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If the pattern matches the end of the value of name, then  substitute  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              value of name with the matched portion deleted; otherwise, just substitute[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  value  of  name.  In the first form, the smallest matching pattern is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              preferred; in the second form, the largest matching pattern is preferred.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name:#pattern}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If the pattern matches the  value  of  name,  then  substitute  the  empty[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              string; otherwise, just substitute the value of name.  If name is an array[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  matching array elements are removed (use the `(M)' flag to remove the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              non-matched elements).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name:|arrayname}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If arrayname is the name (N.B., not contents) of an array  variable,  then[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              any  elements  contained in arrayname are removed from the substitution of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              name.  If the substitution is scalar, either  because  name  is  a  scalar[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              variable  or  the  expression is quoted, the elements of arrayname are in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              stead tested against the entire expression.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name:*arrayname}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Similar to the preceding substitution, but in the opposite sense, so  that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              entries  present  in both the original substitution and as elements of ar‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rayname are retained and others removed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name:^arrayname}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name:^^arrayname}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Zips two arrays, such that the output array is twice as long as the short‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              est (longest for `:^^') of name and arrayname, with the elements alternat‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ingly being picked from them. For `:^', if one  of  the  input  arrays  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              longer, the output will stop when the end of the shorter array is reached.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Thus,[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     a=(1 2 3 4); b=(a b); print ${a:^b}[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              will output `1 a 2 b'.  For `:^^', then the input is repeated until all of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the longer array has been used up and the above will output `1 a 2 b 3 a 4[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              b'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Either or both inputs may be a scalar, they will be treated as an array of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              length  1  with  the scalar as the only element. If either array is empty,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the other array is output with no extra elements inserted.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Currently the following code will output `a b' and `1' as two separate el‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ements, which can be unexpected. The second print  provides  a  workaround[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              which should continue to work if this is changed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     a=(a b); b=(1 2); print -l "${a:^b}"; print -l "${${a:^b}}"[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name:offset}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name:offset:length}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  syntax  gives  effects similar to parameter subscripting in the form[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              $name[start,end], but is compatible with other shells; note that both off‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              set and length are interpreted differently from the components of  a  sub‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              script.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  offset  is non-negative, then if the variable name is a scalar substi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tute the contents starting offset characters from the first  character  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  string,  and  if name is an array substitute elements starting offset[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              elements from the first element.  If length is given, substitute that many[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              characters or elements, otherwise the entire rest of the scalar or array.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              A positive offset is always treated as the offset of a character  or  ele‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ment  in  name  from  the first character or element of the array (this is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              different from native zsh subscript notation).   Hence  0  refers  to  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              first character or element regardless of the setting of the option KSH_AR‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              RAYS.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              A negative offset counts backwards from the end of the scalar or array, so[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that -1 corresponds to the last character or element, and so on.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              When  positive,  length  counts from the offset position toward the end of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the scalar or array.  When negative, length counts back from the end.   If[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              this  results  in  a position smaller than offset, a diagnostic is printed[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and nothing is substituted.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The option MULTIBYTE is obeyed, i.e. the offset and length count multibyte[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              characters where appropriate.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              offset and length undergo the same  set  of  shell  substitutions  as  for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              scalar assignment; in addition, they are then subject to arithmetic evalu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ation.  Hence, for example[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     print ${foo:3}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     print ${foo: 1 + 2}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     print ${foo:$(( 1 + 2))}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     print ${foo:$(echo 1 + 2)}[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              all  have  the  same  effect, extracting the string starting at the fourth[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              character of $foo if the substitution would otherwise return a scalar,  or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  array  starting  at the fourth element if $foo would return an array.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that with the option KSH_ARRAYS $foo always returns a scalar (regard‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              less of the use of the offset syntax) and a form such  as  ${foo[*]:3}  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              required to extract elements of an array named foo.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  offset  is  negative,  the - may not appear immediately after the : as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              this indicates the ${name:-word} form of substitution.  Instead,  a  space[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              may  be inserted before the -.  Furthermore, neither offset nor length may[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              begin with an alphabetic character or & as these are used to indicate his‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tory-style modifiers.  To substitute a value from a variable,  the  recom‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mended  approach is to precede it with a $ as this signifies the intention[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (parameter substitution can easily be rendered  unreadable);  however,  as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              arithmetic  substitution  is  performed,  the expression ${var: offs} does[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              work, retrieving the offset from $offs.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              For further compatibility with other shells there is a  special  case  for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              array  offset  0.   This  usually accesses the first element of the array.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              However, if the substitution refers to  the  positional  parameter  array,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              e.g.  $@ or $*, then offset 0 instead refers to $0, offset 1 refers to $1,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and so on.  In other words, the positional parameter array is  effectively[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              extended  by  prepending  $0.   Hence ${*:0:1} substitutes $0 and ${*:1:1}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              substitutes $1.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name/pattern/repl}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name//pattern/repl}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ${name:/pattern/repl}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Replace the longest possible match of pattern in the expansion of  parame‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ter  name  by  string repl.  The first form replaces just the first occur‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rence, the second form all occurrences, and the third form  replaces  only[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              if  pattern  matches the entire string.  Both pattern and repl are subject[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to    double-quoted    substitution,    so    that    expressions     like[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ${name/$opat/$npat}  will work, but obey the usual rule that pattern char‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              acters in $opat  are  not  treated  specially  unless  either  the  option[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              GLOB_SUBST is set, or $opat is instead substituted as ${~opat}.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The  pattern may begin with a `#', in which case the pattern must match at[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the start of the string, or `%', in which case it must match at the end of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the string, or `#%' in which  case  the  pattern  must  match  the  entire[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              string.   The repl may be an empty string, in which case the final `/' may[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              also be omitted.  To quote the final `/' in other cases it should be  pre‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ceded  by  a single backslash; this is not necessary if the `/' occurs in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              side a substituted parameter.  Note also that the `#', `%' and `#% are not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              active if they occur inside a substituted parameter, even at the start.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If, after quoting rules apply, ${name} expands to an array,  the  replace‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ments act on each element individually.  Note also the effect of the I and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              S parameter expansion flags below; however, the flags M, R, B, E and N are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              not useful.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              For example,[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     foo="twinkle twinkle little star" sub="t*e" rep="spy"[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     print ${foo//${~sub}/$rep}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     print ${(S)foo//${~sub}/$rep}[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Here, the `~' ensures that the text of $sub is treated as a pattern rather[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              than a plain string.  In the first case, the longest match for t*e is sub‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              stituted  and  the  result  is  `spy  star', while in the second case, the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              shortest matches are taken and the result is `spy spy lispy star'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ${#spec}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If spec is one of the above substitutions, substitute the length in  char‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              acters  of  the  result instead of the result itself.  If spec is an array[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              expression, substitute the number of elements of the result.  This has the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              side-effect that joining is skipped even in quoted forms, which may affect[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              other sub-expressions in spec.  Note that `^', `=', and `~',  below,  must[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              appear to the left of `#' when these forms are combined.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  the  option  POSIX_IDENTIFIERS  is not set, and spec is a simple name,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              then the braces are optional; this is true even for special parameters  so[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              e.g. $#- and $#* take the length of the string $- and the array $* respec‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tively.   If  POSIX_IDENTIFIERS is set, then braces are required for the #[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to be treated in this fashion.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ${^spec}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ${^^spec}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Turn on the RC_EXPAND_PARAM option for the evaluation of spec; if the  `^'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is doubled, turn it off.  When this option is set, array expansions of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              form  foo${xx}bar,  where  the parameter xx is set to (a b c), are substi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tuted with `fooabar foobbar foocbar' instead of the default `fooa b cbar'.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that an empty array will therefore cause all arguments to be removed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Internally, each such expansion is converted into the equivalent list  for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              brace  expansion.   E.g.,  ${^var}  becomes  {$var[1],$var[2],...}, and is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              processed as described in the section `Brace Expansion' below: note,  how‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ever, the expansion happens immediately, with any explicit brace expansion[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              happening  later.   If  word  splitting  is also in effect the $var[N] may[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              themselves be split into different list elements.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ${=spec}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ${==spec}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Perform word splitting using the rules for SH_WORD_SPLIT during the evalu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ation of spec, but regardless of whether the parameter appears  in  double[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              quotes;  if the `=' is doubled, turn it off.  This forces parameter expan‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sions to be split into separate words before substitution, using IFS as  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              delimiter.  This is done by default in most other shells.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that splitting is applied to word in the assignment forms of spec be‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fore  the assignment to name is performed.  This affects the result of ar‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ray assignments with the A flag.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ${~spec}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ${~~spec}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Turn on the GLOB_SUBST option for the evaluation of spec; if  the  `~'  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              doubled,  turn it off.  When this option is set, the string resulting from[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the expansion will be interpreted as a pattern anywhere that is  possible,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              such as in filename expansion and filename generation and pattern-matching[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              contexts  like the right hand side of the `=' and `!=' operators in condi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tions.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              In nested substitutions, note that the effect of the ~ applies to the  re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sult  of  the current level of substitution.  A surrounding pattern opera‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion on the result may cancel it.  Hence, for example,  if  the  parameter[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              foo  is set to *, ${~foo//\*/*.c} is substituted by the pattern *.c, which[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              may be expanded by filename generation, but ${${~foo}//\*/*.c} substitutes[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to the string *.c, which will not be further expanded.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       If a ${...} type parameter expression or a $(...) type  command  substitution  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       used in place of name above, it is expanded first and the result is used as if it[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       were  the  value  of  name.   Thus  it  is possible to perform nested operations:[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ${${foo#head}%tail} substitutes the value of $foo with  both  `head'  and  `tail'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       deleted.   The form with $(...) is often useful in combination with the flags de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       scribed next; see the examples below.  Each name or nested ${...} in a  parameter[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       expansion  may  also  be followed by a subscript expression as described in Array[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Parameters in zshparam(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Note that double quotes may appear around nested expressions, in which case  only[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the  part inside is treated as quoted; for example, ${(f)"$(foo)"} quotes the re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       sult of $(foo), but the flag `(f)' (see below) is applied using the rules for un‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       quoted expansions.  Note further that quotes are themselves nested in  this  con‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       text;  for  example, in "${(@f)"$(foo)"}", there are two sets of quotes, one sur‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       rounding the whole expression, the other (redundant) surrounding  the  $(foo)  as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       before.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Parameter Expansion Flags[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       If  the  opening brace is directly followed by an opening parenthesis, the string[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       up to the matching closing parenthesis will be taken as  a  list  of  flags.   In[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       cases  where repeating a flag is meaningful, the repetitions need not be consecu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tive; for example, `(q%q%q)' means the same thing as the more readable `(%%qqq)'.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The following flags are supported:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       #      Evaluate the resulting words as numeric expressions and interpret these as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              character codes.  Output the corresponding  characters.   Note  that  this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              form is entirely distinct from use of the # without parentheses.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  the  MULTIBYTE  option is set and the number is greater than 127 (i.e.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              not an ASCII character) it is treated as a Unicode character.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %      Expand all % escapes in the resulting words in the same way as in  prompts[0m
