[38;2;248;248;242m       l      Lower case characters in the pattern match upper or lower case characters;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              upper  case  characters in the pattern still only match upper case charac‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ters.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       I      Case sensitive:  locally negates the effect of i or l from that point on.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       b      Activate backreferences for parenthesised groups in the pattern; this does[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              not work in filename generation.  When a pattern  with  a  set  of  active[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              parentheses  is  matched,  the strings matched by the groups are stored in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the array $match, the indices of the beginning of the matched  parentheses[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              in  the array $mbegin, and the indices of the end in the array $mend, with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the first element of each array corresponding to the  first  parenthesised[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              group,  and  so  on.  These arrays are not otherwise special to the shell.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The indices use the same convention as  does  parameter  substitution,  so[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that  elements of $mend and $mbegin may be used in subscripts; the KSH_AR‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              RAYS option is respected.  Sets  of  globbing  flags  are  not  considered[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              parenthesised groups; only the first nine active parentheses can be refer‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              enced.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              For example,[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     foo="a_string_with_a_message"[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     if [[ $foo = (a|an)_(#b)(*) ]]; then[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                       print ${foo[$mbegin[1],$mend[1]]}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     fi[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              prints `string_with_a_message'.  Note that the first set of parentheses is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              before the (#b) and does not create a backreference.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Backreferences work with all forms of pattern matching other than filename[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              generation, but note that when performing matches on an entire array, such[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              as ${array#pattern}, or a global substitution, such as ${param//pat/repl},[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              only the data for the last match remains available.  In the case of global[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              replacements this may still be useful.  See the example for the m flag be‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              low.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The  numbering of backreferences strictly follows the order of the opening[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              parentheses from left to right in the pattern  string,  although  sets  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              parentheses  may  be nested.  There are special rules for parentheses fol‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              lowed by `#' or `##'.  Only the last match of the  parenthesis  is  remem‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              bered:  for  example, in `[[ abab = (#b)([ab])# ]]', only the final `b' is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              stored in match[1].  Thus extra parentheses may be necessary to match  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              complete  segment: for example, use `X((ab|cd)#)Y' to match a whole string[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of either `ab' or `cd' between `X' and `Y', using the value  of  $match[1][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rather than $match[2].[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  the match fails none of the parameters is altered, so in some cases it[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              may be necessary to initialise them beforehand.  If some of the backrefer‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ences fail to match -- which happens if they are in  an  alternate  branch[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              which  fails to match, or if they are followed by # and matched zero times[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -- then the matched string is set to the empty string, and the  start  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              end indices are set to -1.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Pattern matching with backreferences is slightly slower than without.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       B      Deactivate  backreferences,  negating  the  effect of the b flag from that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              point on.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       cN,M   The flag (#cN,M) can be used anywhere that the # or ##  operators  can  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              used  except  in  the expressions `(*/)#' and `(*/)##' in filename genera‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion, where `/' has special meaning; it  cannot  be  combined  with  other[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              globbing  flags  and a bad pattern error occurs if it is misplaced.  It is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              equivalent to the form {N,M} in regular expressions.  The previous charac‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ter or group is required to match between N and M times,  inclusive.   The[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              form  (#cN) requires exactly N matches; (#c,M) is equivalent to specifying[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              N as 0; (#cN,) specifies that there is no maximum limit on the  number  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              matches.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       m      Set  references  to  the match data for the entire string matched; this is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              similar to backreferencing and does not work in filename generation.   The[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              flag  must  be  in  effect  at the end of the pattern, i.e. not local to a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              group. The parameters $MATCH,  $MBEGIN and $MEND will be set to the string[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              matched and to the indices of the beginning and end of the string, respec‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tively.  This is most useful in parameter substitutions, as otherwise  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              string matched is obvious.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              For example,[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     arr=(veldt jynx grimps waqf zho buck)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     print ${arr//(#m)[aeiou]/${(U)MATCH}}[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              forces  all  the matches (i.e. all vowels) into uppercase, printing `vEldt[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              jynx grImps wAqf zhO bUck'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Unlike backreferences, there is no speed penalty for  using  match  refer‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ences,  other  than  the  extra substitutions required for the replacement[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              strings in cases such as the example shown.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       M      Deactivate the m flag, hence no references to match data will be created.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       anum   Approximate matching: num errors are allowed in the string matched by  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pattern.  The rules for this are described in the next subsection.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       s, e   Unlike  the other flags, these have only a local effect, and each must ap‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pear on its own:  `(#s)' and `(#e)' are the only valid forms.  The  `(#s)'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              flag  succeeds  only  at the start of the test string, and the `(#e)' flag[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              succeeds only at the end of the test string; they correspond  to  `^'  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `$'  in  standard  regular expressions.  They are useful for matching path[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              segments in patterns other than those in filename generation  (where  path[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              segments   are   in   any   case   treated   separately).    For  example,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `*((#s)|/)test((#e)|/)*' matches a path segment `test' in any of the  fol‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              lowing strings: test, test/at/start, at/end/test, in/test/middle.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Another   use   is   in   parameter   substitution;   for  example  `${ar‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ray/(#s)A*Z(#e)}' will remove only elements of an array  which  match  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              complete  pattern  `A*Z'.   There are other ways of performing many opera‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tions of this type, however the combination of the substitution operations[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `/' and `//' with the `(#s)' and `(#e)' flags provides a single simple and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              memorable method.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that assertions of the form `(^(#s))' also work, i.e. match  anywhere[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              except  at the start of the string, although this actually means `anything[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              except a zero-length portion at the start of the string'; you need to  use[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `(""~(#s))' to match a zero-length portion of the string not at the start.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       q      A  `q'  and everything up to the closing parenthesis of the globbing flags[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              are ignored by the pattern matching code.  This is intended to support the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              use of glob qualifiers,  see  below.   The  result  is  that  the  pattern[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `(#b)(*).c(#q.)'  can be used both for globbing and for matching against a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              string.  In the former case, the `(#q.)' will be treated as a glob  quali‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fier  and  the  `(#b)'  will  not  be useful, while in the latter case the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `(#b)' is useful for backreferences and the `(#q.)' will be ignored.  Note[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that colon modifiers in the glob qualifiers are also not applied in  ordi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              nary pattern matching.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       u      Respect  the current locale in determining the presence of multibyte char‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              acters in a pattern, provided the shell was compiled  with  MULTIBYTE_SUP‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              PORT.  This overrides the MULTIBYTE option; the default behaviour is taken[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              from  the  option.   Compare U.  (Mnemonic: typically multibyte characters[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              are from Unicode in the UTF-8 encoding, although any  extension  of  ASCII[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              supported by the system library may be used.)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       U      All  characters  are considered to be a single byte long.  The opposite of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              u.  This overrides the MULTIBYTE option.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       For example, the test string fooxx can be matched by the pattern  (#i)FOOXX,  but[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       not  by  (#l)FOOXX, (#i)FOO(#I)XX or ((#i)FOOX)X.  The string (#ia2)readme speci‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       fies case-insensitive matching of readme with up to two errors.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       When using the ksh syntax for grouping both KSH_GLOB and  EXTENDED_GLOB  must  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       set  and  the left parenthesis should be preceded by @.  Note also that the flags[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       do not affect letters inside [...] groups, in other words (#i)[a-z] still matches[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       only lowercase letters.  Finally, note that when examining whole  paths  case-in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       sensitively every directory must be searched for all files which match, so that a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       pattern of the form (#i)/foo/bar/... is potentially slow.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Approximate Matching[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       When  matching  approximately, the shell keeps a count of the errors found, which[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       cannot exceed the number specified in the (#anum) flags.  Four types of error are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       recognised:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       1.     Different characters, as in fooxbar and fooybar.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       2.     Transposition of characters, as in banana and abnana.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       3.     A character missing in the target string, as with  the  pattern  road  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              target string rod.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       4.     An  extra  character  appearing  in  the  target string, as with stove and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              strove.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Thus, the pattern (#a3)abcd matches dcba, with the errors occurring by using  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       first  rule  twice  and  the  second  once, grouping the string as [d][cb][a] and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       [a][bc][d].[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Non-literal parts of the pattern must  match  exactly,  including  characters  in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       character  ranges:  hence  (#a1)???   matches strings of length four, by applying[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       rule 4 to an empty part of the pattern, but not strings of length two, since  all[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the  ? must match.  Other characters which must match exactly are initial dots in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       filenames (unless the GLOB_DOTS option is set), and all slashes in filenames,  so[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       that  a/bc  is  two errors from ab/c (the slash cannot be transposed with another[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       character).  Similarly, errors are counted separately for non-contiguous  strings[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       in the pattern, so that (ab|cd)ef is two errors from aebf.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       When using exclusion via the ~ operator, approximate matching is treated entirely[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       separately  for  the  excluded  part  and  must  be  activated separately.  Thus,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       (#a1)README~READ_ME matches READ.ME but not READ_ME, as the trailing  READ_ME  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       matched  without approximation.  However, (#a1)README~(#a1)READ_ME does not match[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       any pattern of the form READ?ME as all such forms are now excluded.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Apart from exclusions, there is only one overall error count; however, the  maxi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       mum errors allowed may be altered locally, and this can be delimited by grouping.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       For example, (#a1)cat((#a0)dog)fox allows one error in total, which may not occur[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       in the dog section, and the pattern (#a1)cat(#a0)dog(#a1)fox is equivalent.  Note[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       that the point at which an error is first found is the crucial one for establish‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ing  whether  to  use approximation; for example, (#a1)abc(#a0)xyz will not match[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       abcdxyz, because the error occurs at the `x', where approximation is turned off.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Entire path segments may be matched approximately, so that `(#a1)/foo/d/is/avail‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       able/at/the/bar' allows one error in any path segment.  This is much  less  effi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       cient  than without the (#a1), however, since every directory in the path must be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       scanned for a possible approximate match.  It is best to place  the  (#a1)  after[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       any path segments which are known to be correct.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Recursive Globbing[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       A  pathname  component of the form `(foo/)#' matches a path consisting of zero or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       more directories matching the pattern foo.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       As a shorthand, `**/' is equivalent to `(*/)#'; note that this therefore  matches[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       files in the current directory as well as subdirectories.  Thus:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              ls -ld -- (*/)#bar[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       or[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              ls -ld -- **/bar[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       does  a  recursive  directory search for files named `bar' (potentially including[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the file `bar' in the current directory).  This form  does  not  follow  symbolic[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       links;  the alternative form `***/' does, but is otherwise identical.  Neither of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       these can be combined with other forms of globbing within the same path  segment;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       in that case, the `*' operators revert to their usual effect.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Even shorter forms are available when the option GLOB_STAR_SHORT is set.  In that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       case if no / immediately follows a ** or *** they are treated as if both a / plus[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       a further * are present.  Hence:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              setopt GLOBSTARSHORT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ls -ld -- **.c[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       is equivalent to[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              ls -ld -- **/*.c[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Glob Qualifiers[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Patterns used for filename generation may end in a list of qualifiers enclosed in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       parentheses.   The  qualifiers  specify  which filenames that otherwise match the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       given pattern will be inserted in the argument list.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       If the option BARE_GLOB_QUAL is set, then a trailing set of parentheses  contain‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ing  no `|' or `(' characters (or `~' if it is special) is taken as a set of glob[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       qualifiers.  A glob subexpression that would normally be  taken  as  glob  quali‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       fiers,  for  example `(^x)', can be forced to be treated as part of the glob pat‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tern by doubling the parentheses, in this case producing `((^x))'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       If the option EXTENDED_GLOB is set, a different syntax  for  glob  qualifiers  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       available,  namely `(#qx)' where x is any of the same glob qualifiers used in the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       other format.  The qualifiers must still appear at the end of the pattern.   How‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ever,  with  this  syntax multiple glob qualifiers may be chained together.  They[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       are treated as a logical AND of the individual sets of flags.  Also, as the  syn‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tax  is  unambiguous,  the  expression will be treated as glob qualifiers just as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       long any parentheses contained within it are balanced; appearance of `|', `('  or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       `~'  does not negate the effect.  Note that qualifiers will be recognised in this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       form even if a bare glob qualifier exists at the end of the pattern, for  example[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       `*(#q*)(.)' will recognise executable regular files if both options are set; how‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ever, mixed syntax should probably be avoided for the sake of clarity.  Note that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       within  conditions using the `[[' form the presence of a parenthesised expression[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       (#q...) at the end of a string indicates that globbing should be  performed;  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       expression  may  include  glob  qualifiers,  but it is also valid if it is simply[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       (#q).  This does not apply to the right hand side of pattern match  operators  as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the syntax already has special significance.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       A qualifier may be any one of the following:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       /      directories[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       F      `full'  (i.e.  non-empty)  directories.  Note that the opposite sense (^F)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              expands to empty directories and all non-directories.  Use (/^F) for empty[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              directories.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       .      plain files[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       @      symbolic links[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       =      sockets[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       p      named pipes (FIFOs)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       *      executable plain files (0100 or 0010 or 0001)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %      device files (character or block special)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %b     block special files[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       %c     character special files[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       r      owner-readable files (0400)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       w      owner-writable files (0200)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       x      owner-executable files (0100)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       A      group-readable files (0040)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       I      group-writable files (0020)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       E      group-executable files (0010)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       R      world-readable files (0004)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       W      world-writable files (0002)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       X      world-executable files (0001)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       s      setuid files (04000)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       S      setgid files (02000)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       t      files with the sticky bit (01000)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       fspec  files with access rights matching spec. This spec may be  a  octal  number[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              optionally preceded by a `=', a `+', or a `-'. If none of these characters[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is  given, the behavior is the same as for `='. The octal number describes[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the mode bits to be expected, if combined with a `=', the value given must[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              match the file-modes exactly, with a `+', at least the bits in  the  given[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              number must be set in the file-modes, and with a `-', the bits in the num‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ber must not be set. Giving a `?' instead of a octal digit anywhere in the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              number  ensures  that  the  corresponding  bits  in the file-modes are not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              checked, this is only useful in combination with `='.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If the qualifier `f' is followed by any other character anything up to the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              next matching character (`[', `{', and `<' match `]', `}', and `>' respec‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tively, any other  character  matches  itself)  is  taken  as  a  list  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              comma-separated  sub-specs. Each sub-spec may be either an octal number as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              described above or a list of any of the characters `u', `g', `o', and `a',[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              followed by a `=', a `+', or a `-', followed by a list of any of the char‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              acters `r', `w', `x', `s', and `t', or an octal digit. The first  list  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              characters  specify  which  access  rights  are to be checked. If a `u' is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              given, those for the owner of the file are used, if a `g' is given,  those[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of  the  group  are checked, a `o' means to test those of other users, and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the `a' says to test all three groups. The `=', `+', and  `-'  again  says[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              how the modes are to be checked and have the same meaning as described for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the first form above. The second list of characters finally says which ac‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cess rights are to be expected: `r' for read access, `w' for write access,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `x'  for the right to execute the file (or to search a directory), `s' for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the setuid and setgid bits, and `t' for the sticky bit.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Thus, `*(f70?)' gives the files for which the owner has read,  write,  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              execute  permission, and for which other group members have no rights, in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              dependent of the permissions for other users. The pattern `*(f-100)' gives[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              all files for which the  owner  does  not  have  execute  permission,  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `*(f:gu+w,o-rx:)'  gives  the files for which the owner and the other mem‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              bers of the group have at least write  permission,  and  for  which  other[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              users don't have read or execute permission.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       estring[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       +cmd   The  string will be executed as shell code.  The filename will be included[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              in the list if and only if the code returns a  zero  status  (usually  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              status of the last command).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              In  the  first  form,  the first character after the `e' will be used as a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              separator and anything up to the next matching separator will be taken  as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the string; `[', `{', and `<' match `]', `}', and `>', respectively, while[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              any other character matches itself. Note that expansions must be quoted in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the string to prevent them from being expanded before  globbing  is  done.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              string is then executed as shell code.  The string globqual is appended to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the array zsh_eval_context the duration of execution.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              During  the  execution  of  string  the filename currently being tested is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              available in the parameter REPLY; the parameter may be altered to a string[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to be inserted into the list instead of the original filename.   In  addi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion,  the parameter reply may be set to an array or a string, which over‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rides the value of REPLY.  If set to an array, the latter is inserted into[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the command line word by word.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              For example, suppose a directory contains a single  file  `lonely'.   Then[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the   expression  `*(e:'reply=(${REPLY}{1,2})':)'  will  cause  the  words[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `lonely1' and `lonely2' to be inserted into the command  line.   Note  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              quoting of string.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The  form  +cmd  has the same effect, but no delimiters appear around cmd.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Instead, cmd is taken as the longest sequence of characters following  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              +  that are alphanumeric or underscore.  Typically cmd will be the name of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              a shell function that contains the appropriate test.  For example,[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     nt() { [[ $REPLY -nt $NTREF ]] }[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     NTREF=reffile[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ls -ld -- *(+nt)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              lists all files in the directory that have  been  modified  more  recently[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              than reffile.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ddev   files on the device dev[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       l[-|+]ct[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              files  having a link count less than ct (-), greater than ct (+), or equal[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to ct[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       U      files owned by the effective user ID[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       G      files owned by the effective group ID[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       uid    files owned by user ID id if that is a number.  Otherwise, id specifies  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              user  name:  the  character after the `u' will be taken as a separator and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the string between it and the next matching separator will be taken  as  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              user name.  The starting separators `[', `{', and `<' match the final sep‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              arators  `]',  `}', and `>', respectively; any other character matches it‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              self.  The selected files are those owned  by  this  user.   For  example,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `u:foo:' or `u[foo]' selects files owned by user `foo'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       gid    like uid but with group IDs or names[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       a[Mwhms][-|+]n[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              files  accessed exactly n days ago.  Files accessed within the last n days[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              are selected using a negative value for n (-n).  Files accessed more  than[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              n  days ago are selected by a positive n value (+n).  Optional unit speci‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fiers `M', `w', `h', `m' or `s' (e.g. `ah5') cause the check  to  be  per‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              formed  with months (of 30 days), weeks, hours, minutes or seconds instead[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of days, respectively.  An explicit `d' for days is also allowed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Any fractional part of the difference between the access time and the cur‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rent part in the appropriate units is ignored in the comparison.  For  in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              stance,  `echo  *(ah-5)'  would  echo  files accessed within the last five[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              hours, while `echo *(ah+5)' would echo files accessed at least  six  hours[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ago,  as  times  strictly  between  five and six hours are treated as five[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              hours.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       m[Mwhms][-|+]n[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              like the file access qualifier, except that it uses the file  modification[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              time.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       c[Mwhms][-|+]n[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              like  the file access qualifier, except that it uses the file inode change[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              time.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       L[+|-]n[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              files less than n bytes (-), more than n bytes (+), or exactly n bytes  in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              length.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  this  flag  is  directly  followed  by a size specifier `k' (`K'), `m'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (`M'), or `p' (`P') (e.g. `Lk-50') the check is performed with  kilobytes,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              megabytes,  or blocks (of 512 bytes) instead.  (On some systems additional[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              specifiers are available for gigabytes, `g' or `G', and terabytes, `t'  or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `T'.) If a size specifier is used a file is regarded as "exactly" the size[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              if  the  file  size rounded up to the next unit is equal to the test size.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Hence `*(Lm1)' matches files from 1 byte up to 1 Megabyte inclusive.  Note[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              also that the set of files "less than" the test size only  includes  files[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that would not match the equality test; hence `*(Lm-1)' only matches files[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of zero size.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ^      negates all qualifiers following it[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -      toggles between making the qualifiers work on symbolic links (the default)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and  the  files  they point to, if any; any symbolic link for whose target[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the `stat' system call fails  (whatever  the  cause  of  the  failure)  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              treated as a file in its own right[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       M      sets the MARK_DIRS option for the current pattern[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       T      appends  a  trailing  qualifier  mark  to  the filenames, analogous to the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              LIST_TYPES option, for the current pattern (overrides M)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       N      sets the NULL_GLOB option for the current pattern[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       D      sets the GLOB_DOTS option for the current pattern[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       n      sets the NUMERIC_GLOB_SORT option for the current pattern[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Yn     enables short-circuit mode: the pattern will expand to  at  most  n  file‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              names.   If  more than n matches exist, only the first n matches in direc‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tory traversal order will be considered.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Implies oN when no oc qualifier is used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       oc     specifies how the names of the files should be sorted. The following  val‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ues of c sort in the following ways:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              n      By name.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              L      By the size (length) of the files.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              l      By number of links.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              a      By time of last access, youngest first.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              m      By time of last modification, youngest first.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              c      By time of last inode change, youngest first.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              d      By  directories: files in subdirectories appear before those in the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     current directory at each level of the search -- this is best  com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     bined  with other criteria, for example `odon' to sort on names for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     files within the same directory.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              N      No sorting is performed.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              estring[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              +cmd   Sort by shell code (see below).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that the modifiers ^ and - are used, so `*(^-oL)' gives a list of all[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              files sorted by file size in  descending  order,  following  any  symbolic[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              links.   Unless oN is used, multiple order specifiers may occur to resolve[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ties.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The default sorting is n (by name) unless the Y glob qualifier is used, in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              which case it is N (unsorted).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              oe and o+ are special cases; they are each followed by shell code,  delim‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ited  as  for  the  e glob qualifier and the + glob qualifier respectively[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (see above).  The code is executed for each matched file with the  parame‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ter  REPLY  set  to the name of the file on entry and globsort appended to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zsh_eval_context.  The code should modify  the  parameter  REPLY  in  some[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fashion.   On  return,  the  value of the parameter is used instead of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              file name as the string on which to sort.  Unlike other sort operators, oe[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and o+ may be repeated, but note that the maximum number of sort operators[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of any kind that may appear in any glob expression is 12.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Oc     like `o', but sorts in descending order; i.e.  `*(^oc)'  is  the  same  as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `*(Oc)'  and  `*(^Oc)' is the same as `*(oc)'; `Od' puts files in the cur‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rent directory before those in subdirectories at each level of the search.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       [beg[,end]][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              specifies which of the matched filenames should be  included  in  the  re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              turned  list.  The syntax is the same as for array subscripts. beg and the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              optional end may be mathematical expressions. As in parameter subscripting[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              they may be negative to make them count  from  the  last  match  backward.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              E.g.: `*(-OL[1,3])' gives a list of the names of the three largest files.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Pstring[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The  string  will  be  prepended  to  each  glob match as a separate word.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              string is delimited in the same way as arguments to the e  glob  qualifier[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              described  above.   The qualifier can be repeated; the words are prepended[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              separately so that the resulting command line contains the  words  in  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              same order they were given in the list of glob qualifiers.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              A typical use for this is to prepend an option before all occurrences of a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              file  name;  for example, the pattern `*(P:-f:)' produces the command line[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              arguments `-f file1 -f file2 ...'[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If the modifier ^ is active, then  string  will  be  appended  instead  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              prepended.   Prepending and appending is done independently so both can be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              used   on   the   same   glob   expression;   for   example   by   writing[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `*(P:foo:^P:bar:^P:baz:)'  which  produces the command line arguments `foo[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              baz file1 bar ...'[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       More than one of these lists can be combined, separated by commas. The whole list[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       matches if at least one of the sublists matches (they are `or'ed, the  qualifiers[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       in  the sublists are `and'ed).  Some qualifiers, however, affect all matches gen‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       erated, independent of the sublist in which they are given.  These are the quali‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       fiers `M', `T', `N', `D', `n', `o', `O' and  the  subscripts  given  in  brackets[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       (`[...]').[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       If  a  `:' appears in a qualifier list, the remainder of the expression in paren‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       thesis is interpreted as a modifier (see the section `Modifiers' in  the  section[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       `History  Expansion').  Each modifier must be introduced by a separate `:'.  Note[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       also that the result after modification does not have to  be  an  existing  file.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The  name of any existing file can be followed by a modifier of the form `(:...)'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       even if no actual filename generation is performed, although note that the  pres‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ence  of  the  parentheses  causes  the  entire expression to be subjected to any[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       global pattern matching options such as NULL_GLOB. Thus:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              ls -ld -- *(-/)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       lists all directories and symbolic links that point to directories, and[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              ls -ld -- *(-@)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       lists all broken symbolic links, and[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              ls -ld -- *(%W)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       lists all world-writable device files in the current directory, and[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              ls -ld -- *(W,X)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       lists all files in the current directory that are  world-writable  or  world-exe‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       cutable, and[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              print -rC1 /tmp/foo*(u0^@:t)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       outputs  the  basename of all root-owned files beginning with the string `foo' in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       /tmp, ignoring symlinks, and[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              ls -ld -- *.*~(lex|parse).[ch](^D^l1)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       lists all files having a link count of one whose names contain  a  dot  (but  not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       those starting with a dot, since GLOB_DOTS is explicitly switched off) except for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       lex.c, lex.h, parse.c and parse.h.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              print -rC1 b*.pro(#q:s/pro/shmo/)(#q.:s/builtin/shmiltin/)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       demonstrates  how  colon  modifiers and other qualifiers may be chained together.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The ordinary qualifier `.' is applied first, then the colon  modifiers  in  order[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       from  left to right.  So if EXTENDED_GLOB is set and the base pattern matches the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       regular file builtin.pro, the shell will print `shmiltin.shmo'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mzsh 5.9                               May 14, 2022                            ZSHEXPN(1)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────[0m
[38;2;248;248;242mZSHPARAM(1)                      General Commands Manual                     ZSHPARAM(1)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mNAME[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zshparam - zsh parameters[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mDESCRIPTION[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       A parameter has a name, a value, and a number of attributes.  A name may  be  any[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       sequence  of  alphanumeric  characters  and underscores, or the single characters[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       `*', `@', `#', `?', `-', `$', or `!'.  A parameter whose name begins with an  al‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       phanumeric or underscore is also referred to as a variable.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The  attributes of a parameter determine the type of its value, often referred to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       as the parameter type or variable type, and also control  other  processing  that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       may  be  applied  to  the  value  when it is referenced.  The value type may be a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       scalar (a string, an integer, or a floating point number), an array (indexed  nu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       merically),  or  an  associative array (an unordered set of name-value pairs, in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       dexed by name, also referred to as a hash).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Named scalar parameters may have the exported, -x, attribute, to copy  them  into[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the process environment, which is then passed from the shell to any new processes[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       that  it starts.  Exported parameters are called environment variables. The shell[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       also imports environment variables at startup time and  automatically  marks  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       corresponding  parameters  as  exported.   Some environment variables are not im‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ported for reasons of security or because they would interfere with  the  correct[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       operation of other shell features.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Parameters may also be special, that is, they have a predetermined meaning to the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       shell.   Special  parameters cannot have their type changed or their readonly at‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tribute turned off, and if a special parameter is unset,  then  later  recreated,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the special properties will be retained.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       To  declare  the type of a parameter, or to assign a string or numeric value to a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       scalar parameter, use the typeset builtin.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The value of a scalar parameter may also be assigned by writing:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              name=value[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       In scalar assignment, value is expanded as a single string, in which the elements[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       of arrays are joined together; filename expansion is not performed unless the op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tion GLOB_ASSIGN is set.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       When the integer attribute, -i, or a floating point attribute, -E or -F,  is  set[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       for name, the value is subject to arithmetic evaluation.  Furthermore, by replac‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ing  `='  with `+=', a parameter can be incremented or appended to.  See the sec‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tion `Array Parameters' and Arithmetic Evaluation (in zshmisc(1)) for  additional[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       forms of assignment.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Note  that  assignment  may implicitly change the attributes of a parameter.  For[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       example, assigning a number to a variable in arithmetic evaluation may change its[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       type to integer or float, and with GLOB_ASSIGN assigning a pattern to a  variable[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       may change its type to an array.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       To reference the value of a parameter, write `$name' or `${name}'.  See Parameter[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Expansion in zshexpn(1) for complete details.  That section also explains the ef‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       fect  of  the  difference between scalar and array assignment on parameter expan‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       sion.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mARRAY PARAMETERS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       To assign an array value, write one of:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              set -A name value ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              name=(value ...)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              name=([key]=value ...)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       If no parameter name exists, an ordinary array parameter is created.  If the  pa‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       rameter name exists and is a scalar, it is replaced by a new array.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       In the third form, key is an expression that will be evaluated in arithmetic con‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       text (in its simplest form, an integer) that gives the index of the element to be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       assigned  with  value.   In  this form any elements not explicitly mentioned that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       come before the largest index to which a value is assigned are assigned an  empty[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       string.  The indices may be in any order.  Note that this syntax is strict: [ and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ]=  must not be quoted, and key may not consist of the unquoted string ]=, but is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       otherwise treated as a simple string.  The enhanced forms of subscript expression[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       that may be used when directly subscripting a variable  name,  described  in  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       section `Array Subscripts' below, are not available.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The  syntaxes with and without the explicit key may be mixed.  An implicit key is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       deduced by incrementing the index from the  previously  assigned  element.   Note[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       that  it  is not treated as an error if latter assignments in this form overwrite[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       earlier assignments.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       For example, assuming the option KSH_ARRAYS is not set, the following:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              array=(one [3]=three four)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       causes the array variable array to contain four elements one,  an  empty  string,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       three and four, in that order.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       In  the  forms where only value is specified, full command line expansion is per‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       formed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       In the [key]=value form, both key and value undergo all forms  of  expansion  al‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       lowed  for  single  word shell expansions (this does not include filename genera‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tion); these are as performed by the parameter expansion flag (e) as described in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zshexpn(1).  Nested parentheses may surround value and are included  as  part  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the  value,  which is joined into a plain string; this differs from ksh which al‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       lows the values themselves to be arrays.  A future version  of  zsh  may  support[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       that.   To cause the brackets to be interpreted as a character class for filename[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       generation, and therefore to treat the resulting list of files as a set  of  val‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ues, quote the equal sign using any form of quoting.  Example:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              name=([a-z]'='*)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       To  append  to an array without changing the existing values, use one of the fol‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       lowing:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              name+=(value ...)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              name+=([key]=value ...)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       In the second form key may specify an existing index as well as an index off  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       end  of  the  old array; any existing value is overwritten by value.  Also, it is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       possible to use [key]+=value to append to the existing value at that index.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Within the parentheses on the right hand side of either form of  the  assignment,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       newlines  and semicolons are treated the same as white space, separating individ‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ual values.  Any consecutive sequence of such characters has the same effect.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Ordinary array parameters may also be explicitly declared with:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              typeset -a name[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Associative arrays must be declared before assignment, by using:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              typeset -A name[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       When name refers to an associative array, the list in  an  assignment  is  inter‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       preted as alternating keys and values:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              set -A name key value ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              name=(key value ...)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              name=([key]=value ...)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Note that only one of the two syntaxes above may be used in any given assignment;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the  forms  may not be mixed.  This is unlike the case of numerically indexed ar‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       rays.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Every key must have a value in this case.  Note that this assigns to  the  entire[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       array,  deleting  any elements that do not appear in the list.  The append syntax[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       may also be used with an associative array:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              name+=(key value ...)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              name+=([key]=value ...)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       This adds a new key/value pair if the key is not already  present,  and  replaces[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the  value for the existing key if it is.  In the second form it is also possible[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       to use [key]+=value to append to the existing value at that  key.   Expansion  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       performed  identically to the corresponding forms for normal arrays, as described[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       above.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       To create an empty array (including associative arrays), use one of:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              set -A name[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              name=()[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Array Subscripts[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Individual elements of an array may be selected using a subscript.   A  subscript[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       of  the  form  `[exp]' selects the single element exp, where exp is an arithmetic[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       expression which will be subject to arithmetic expansion as if it were surrounded[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       by `$((...))'.  The elements are numbered beginning with 1, unless the KSH_ARRAYS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       option is set in which case they are numbered from zero.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Subscripts may be used inside braces used  to  delimit  a  parameter  name,  thus[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       `${foo[2]}'  is  equivalent  to  `$foo[2]'.  If the KSH_ARRAYS option is set, the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       braced form is the only one that works, as bracketed  expressions  otherwise  are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       not treated as subscripts.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       If the KSH_ARRAYS option is not set, then by default accesses to an array element[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       with  a subscript that evaluates to zero return an empty string, while an attempt[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       to write such an element is treated as an error.  For backward compatibility  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       KSH_ZERO_SUBSCRIPT  option  can  be  set  to cause subscript values 0 and 1 to be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       equivalent; see the description of the option in zshoptions(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The same subscripting syntax is used  for  associative  arrays,  except  that  no[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       arithmetic  expansion  is  applied to exp.  However, the parsing rules for arith‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       metic expressions still apply, which affects the way that certain special charac‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ters must be protected from interpretation.  See Subscript Parsing below for  de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tails.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       A  subscript  of  the  form `[*]' or `[@]' evaluates to all elements of an array;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       there is no difference between the two except  when  they  appear  within  double[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       quotes.   `"$foo[*]"'  evaluates  to `"$foo[1] $foo[2] ..."', whereas `"$foo[@]"'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       evaluates to `"$foo[1]" "$foo[2]" ...'.  For associative arrays, `[*]'  or  `[@]'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       evaluate to all the values, in no particular order.  Note that this does not sub‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       stitute  the  keys; see the documentation for the `k' flag under Parameter Expan‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       sion Flags in zshexpn(1) for complete details.  When an array parameter is refer‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       enced as `$name' (with no subscript)  it  evaluates  to  `$name[*]',  unless  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       KSH_ARRAYS option is set in which case it evaluates to `${name[0]}' (for an asso‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ciative  array,  this means the value of the key `0', which may not exist even if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       there are values for other keys).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       A subscript of the form `[exp1,exp2]' selects all elements in the range  exp1  to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       exp2,  inclusive.  (Associative  arrays  are  unordered,  and  so  do not support[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ranges.) If one of the subscripts evaluates to a negative number,  say  -n,  then[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the  nth element from the end of the array is used.  Thus `$foo[-3]' is the third[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       element from the end of the array foo, and `$foo[1,-1]' is the same as `$foo[*]'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Subscripting may also be performed on non-array values, in which  case  the  sub‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       scripts specify a substring to be extracted.  For example, if FOO is set to `foo‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       bar',  then `echo $FOO[2,5]' prints `ooba'.  Note that some forms of subscripting[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       described below perform pattern matching, and in that case the substring  extends[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       from the start of the match of the first subscript to the end of the match of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       second subscript.  For example,[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              string="abcdefghijklm"[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              print ${string[(r)d?,(r)h?]}[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       prints  `defghi'.  This is an obvious generalisation of the rule for single-char‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       acter matches.  For a single subscript, only a  single  character  is  referenced[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       (not the range of characters covered by the match).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Note  that in substring operations the second subscript is handled differently by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the r and R subscript flags: the former takes the shortest match  as  the  length[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       and the latter the longest match.  Hence in the former case a * at the end is re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       dundant  while  in  the latter case it matches the whole remainder of the string.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       This does not affect the result of the single subscript case as here  the  length[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       of the match is irrelevant.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Array Element Assignment[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       A subscript may be used on the left side of an assignment like so:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              name[exp]=value[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       In  this  form of assignment the element or range specified by exp is replaced by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the expression on the right side.  An array (but not an associative array) may be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       created by assignment to a range or element.  Arrays do not nest, so assigning  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       parenthesized  list  of  values to an element or range changes the number of ele‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ments in the array, shifting the other elements to accommodate  the  new  values.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       (This is not supported for associative arrays.)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       This syntax also works as an argument to the typeset command:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              typeset "name[exp]"=value[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The  value  may not be a parenthesized list in this case; only single-element as‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       signments may be made with typeset.  Note that quotes are necessary in this  case[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       to  prevent the brackets from being interpreted as filename generation operators.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The noglob precommand modifier could be used instead.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       To delete an element of an ordinary array,  assign  `()'  to  that  element.   To[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       delete an element of an associative array, use the unset command:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              unset "name[exp]"[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Subscript Flags[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       If  the  opening bracket, or the comma in a range, in any subscript expression is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       directly followed by an opening parenthesis, the string up to the matching  clos‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ing one is considered to be a list of flags, as in `name[(flags)exp]'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The  flags  s, n and b take an argument; the delimiter is shown below as `:', but[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       any character, or the matching pairs `(...)', `{...}', `[...]', or  `<...>',  may[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       be used, but note that `<...>' can only be used if the subscript is inside a dou‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ble quoted expression or a parameter substitution enclosed in braces as otherwise[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the expression is interpreted as a redirection.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The flags currently understood are:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       w      If the parameter subscripted is a scalar then this flag makes subscripting[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              work on words instead of characters.  The default word separator is white‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              space.   When  combined with the i or I flag, the effect is to produce the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              index of the first character of the  first/last  word  which  matches  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              given pattern; note that a failed match in this case always yields 0.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       s:string:[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This gives the string that separates words (for use with the w flag).  The[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              delimiter character : is arbitrary; see above.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       p      Recognize the same escape sequences as the print builtin in the string ar‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              gument of a subsequent `s' flag.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       f      If the parameter subscripted is a scalar then this flag makes subscripting[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              work  on lines instead of characters, i.e. with elements separated by new‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              lines.  This is a shorthand for `pws:\n:'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       r      Reverse subscripting: if this flag is given, the exp is taken as a pattern[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and the result is the first matching array element, substring or word  (if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the parameter is an array, if it is a scalar, or if it is a scalar and the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `w' flag is given, respectively).  The subscript used is the number of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              matching  element, so that pairs of subscripts such as `$foo[(r)??,3]' and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `$foo[(r)??,(r)f*]' are possible if the parameter is  not  an  associative[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              array.   If  the parameter is an associative array, only the value part of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              each pair is compared to the pattern, and the result is that value.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If a search through an ordinary array failed, the  search  sets  the  sub‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              script  to  one  past the end of the array, and hence ${array[(r)pattern]}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              will substitute the empty string.  Thus the success of  a  search  can  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tested  by using the (i) flag, for example (assuming the option KSH_ARRAYS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is not in effect):[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     [[ ${array[(i)pattern]} -le ${#array} ]][0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If KSH_ARRAYS is in effect, the -le should be replaced by -lt.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       R      Like `r', but gives the last match.  For  associative  arrays,  gives  all[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              possible  matches.  May  be used for assigning to ordinary array elements,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              but not for assigning to associative arrays.  On failure, for  normal  ar‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rays  this  has  the effect of returning the element corresponding to sub‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              script  0;  this  is  empty  unless  one  of  the  options  KSH_ARRAYS  or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              KSH_ZERO_SUBSCRIPT is in effect.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note  that  in subscripts with both `r' and `R' pattern characters are ac‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tive even if they were substituted for a parameter (regardless of the set‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ting of GLOB_SUBST which controls this feature in  normal  pattern  match‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ing).   The  flag  `e'  can be added to inhibit pattern matching.  As this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              flag does not inhibit other forms of substitution, care is still required;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              using a parameter to hold the key has the desired effect:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     key2='original key'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     print ${array[(Re)$key2]}[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       i      Like `r', but gives the index of the match instead; this may not  be  com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              bined  with a second argument.  On the left side of an assignment, behaves[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              like `r'.  For associative arrays, the key part of each pair  is  compared[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to  the pattern, and the first matching key found is the result.  On fail‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ure substitutes the length of the array plus one, as discussed  under  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              description of `r', or the empty string for an associative array.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note:  Although  `i'  may  be applied to a scalar substitution to find the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              offset of a substring, the  results  are  likely  to  be  misleading  when[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              searching within substitutions that yield an empty string, or when search‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ing for the empty substring.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       I      Like  `i', but gives the index of the last match, or all possible matching[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              keys in an associative array.  On failure  substitutes  0,  or  the  empty[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              string  for an associative array.  This flag is best when testing for val‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ues or keys that do not exist.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note: If the option KSH_ARRAYS is in effect and no match is found, the re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sult is indistinguishable from the case when the first element of the  ar‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ray matches.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       k      If  used in a subscript on an associative array, this flag causes the keys[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to be interpreted as patterns, and returns the value  for  the  first  key[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              found where exp is matched by the key.  Note this could be any such key as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              no  ordering of associative arrays is defined.  This flag does not work on[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the left side of an assignment to an associative array element.   If  used[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              on another type of parameter, this behaves like `r'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       K      On  an associative array this is like `k' but returns all values where exp[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is matched by the keys.  On other types of parameters this  has  the  same[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              effect as `R'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       n:expr:[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If combined with `r', `R', `i' or `I', makes them give the nth or nth last[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              match  (if  expr  evaluates to n).  This flag is ignored when the array is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              associative.  The delimiter character : is arbitrary; see above.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       b:expr:[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If combined with `r', `R', `i' or `I', makes them begin at the nth or  nth[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              last  element,  word, or character (if expr evaluates to n).  This flag is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ignored when the array is associative.  The delimiter character : is arbi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              trary; see above.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       e      This flag causes any pattern matching that would be performed on the  sub‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              script  to  use  plain  string  matching instead.  Hence `${array[(re)*]}'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              matches only the array element whose value is *.  Note that other forms of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              substitution such as parameter substitution are not inhibited.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              This flag can also be used to force * or @ to be interpreted as  a  single[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              key  rather  than as a reference to all values.  It may be used for either[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              purpose on the left side of an assignment.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       See Parameter Expansion Flags (zshexpn(1)) for additional ways to manipulate  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       results of array subscripting.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Subscript Parsing[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       This  discussion  applies mainly to associative array key strings and to patterns[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       used for reverse subscripting (the `r', `R', `i', etc. flags), but  it  may  also[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       affect parameter substitutions that appear as part of an arithmetic expression in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       an ordinary subscript.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       To  avoid  subscript parsing limitations in assignments to associative array ele‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ments, use the append syntax:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              aa+=('key with "*strange*" characters' 'value string')[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The basic rule to remember when writing a subscript expression is that  all  text[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       between  the opening `[' and the closing `]' is interpreted as if it were in dou‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ble quotes (see zshmisc(1)).  However, unlike double quotes which normally cannot[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       nest, subscript expressions may appear inside  double-quoted  strings  or  inside[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       other  subscript  expressions (or both!), so the rules have two important differ‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ences.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The first difference is that brackets (`[' and `]') must appear as balanced pairs[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       in a subscript expression unless they are preceded by a backslash (`\').   There‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       fore, within a subscript expression (and unlike true double-quoting) the sequence[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       `\['  becomes  `[',  and  similarly `\]' becomes `]'.  This applies even in cases[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       where a backslash is not normally required; for example, the pattern  `[^[]'  (to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       match  any  character  other than an open bracket) should be written `[^\[]' in a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       reverse-subscript pattern.  However, note that `\[^\[\]' and  even  `\[^[]'  mean[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the  same  thing, because backslashes are always stripped when they appear before[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       brackets![0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The same rule applies to parentheses (`(' and `)') and braces (`{' and `}'): they[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       must appear either in balanced pairs or preceded by a backslash, and  backslashes[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       that  protect  parentheses or braces are removed during parsing.  This is because[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       parameter expansions may be surrounded by balanced braces,  and  subscript  flags[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       are introduced by balanced parentheses.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The  second  difference is that a double-quote (`"') may appear as part of a sub‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       script expression without being preceded by a backslash, and therefore  that  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       two  characters  `\"'  remain  as  two  characters in the subscript (in true dou‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ble-quoting, `\"' becomes `"').  However, because of the standard  shell  quoting[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       rules, any double-quotes that appear must occur in balanced pairs unless preceded[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       by  a  backslash.   This  makes it more difficult to write a subscript expression[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       that contains an odd number of double-quote characters, but the reason  for  this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       difference  is  so  that  when  a  subscript  expression appears inside true dou‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ble-quotes, one can still write `\"' (rather than `\\\"') for `"'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       To use an odd number of double quotes as a key in an assignment, use the  typeset[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       builtin  and  an  enclosing  pair of double quotes; to refer to the value of that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       key, again use double quotes:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              typeset -A aa[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              typeset "aa[one\"two\"three\"quotes]"=QQQ[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              print "$aa[one\"two\"three\"quotes]"[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       It is important to note that the quoting rules do not change when a parameter ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       pansion with a subscript is nested inside another subscript expression.  That is,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       it is not necessary to use additional backslashes within the inner subscript  ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       pression; they are removed only once, from the innermost subscript outwards.  Pa‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       rameters  are also expanded from the innermost subscript first, as each expansion[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       is encountered left to right in the outer expression.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       A further complication arises from a way in which subscript parsing is  not  dif‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ferent from double quote parsing.  As in true double-quoting, the sequences `\*',[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       and `\@' remain as two characters when they appear in a subscript expression.  To[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       use a literal `*' or `@' as an associative array key, the `e' flag must be used:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              typeset -A aa[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              aa[(e)*]=star[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              print $aa[(e)*][0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       A last detail must be considered when reverse subscripting is performed.  Parame‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ters  appearing  in the subscript expression are first expanded and then the com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       plete expression is interpreted as a pattern.  This has two effects: first, para‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       meters behave as if GLOB_SUBST were on (and it cannot  be  turned  off);  second,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       backslashes  are  interpreted  twice,  once  when parsing the array subscript and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       again when parsing the pattern.  In a reverse subscript, it's  necessary  to  use[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       four  backslashes  to cause a single backslash to match literally in the pattern.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       For complex patterns, it is often easiest to assign the desired pattern to a  pa‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       rameter and then refer to that parameter in the subscript, because then the back‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       slashes,  brackets, parentheses, etc., are seen only when the complete expression[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       is converted to a pattern.  To match the value of a parameter literally in a  re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       verse  subscript,  rather than as a pattern, use `${(q)name}' (see zshexpn(1)) to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       quote the expanded value.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Note that the `k' and `K' flags are reverse subscripting for an  ordinary  array,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       but  are  not reverse subscripting for an associative array!  (For an associative[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       array, the keys in the array itself are interpreted as patterns by  those  flags;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the subscript is a plain string in that case.)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       One  final note, not directly related to subscripting: the numeric names of posi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tional parameters (described below) are parsed specially, so for example  `$2foo'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       is equivalent to `${2}foo'.  Therefore, to use subscript syntax to extract a sub‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       string  from  a positional parameter, the expansion must be surrounded by braces;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       for example, `${2[3,5]}' evaluates to the third through fifth characters  of  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       second  positional  parameter,  but `$2[3,5]' is the entire second parameter con‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       catenated with the filename generation pattern `[3,5]'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mPOSITIONAL PARAMETERS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The positional parameters provide access to the command-line arguments of a shell[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       function, shell script, or the shell itself; see the  section  `Invocation',  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       also  the  section `Functions'.  The parameter n, where n is a number, is the nth[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       positional parameter.  The parameter `$0' is a special case, see the section `Pa‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       rameters Set By The Shell'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The parameters *, @ and argv are arrays containing all the positional parameters;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       thus `$argv[n]', etc., is equivalent to  simply  `$n'.   Note  that  the  options[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       KSH_ARRAYS or KSH_ZERO_SUBSCRIPT apply to these arrays as well, so with either of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       those options set, `${argv[0]}' is equivalent to `$1' and so on.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Positional  parameters may be changed after the shell or function starts by using[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the set builtin, by assigning to the argv array, or by direct assignment  of  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       form  `n=value'  where n is the number of the positional parameter to be changed.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       This also creates (with empty values) any of the positions from 1 to  n  that  do[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       not  already  have  values.  Note that, because the positional parameters form an[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       array, an array assignment of the form `n=(value ...)' is allowed,  and  has  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       effect  of  shifting  all the values at positions greater than n by as many posi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tions as necessary to accommodate the new values.[0m
