[38;2;248;248;242mLOCAL PARAMETERS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Shell function executions delimit scopes for shell parameters.   (Parameters  are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       dynamically scoped.)  The typeset builtin, and its alternative forms declare, in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       teger, local and readonly (but not export), can be used to declare a parameter as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       being local to the innermost scope.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       When a parameter is read or assigned to, the innermost existing parameter of that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       name  is  used.   (That  is, the local parameter hides any less-local parameter.)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       However, assigning to a non-existent parameter, or declaring a new parameter with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       export, causes it to be created in the outermost scope.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Local parameters disappear when their scope ends.  unset can be used to delete  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       parameter  while  it  is still in scope; any outer parameter of the same name re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       mains hidden.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Special parameters may also be made local; they retain their  special  attributes[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       unless  either  the existing or the newly-created parameter has the -h (hide) at‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tribute.  This may have unexpected effects: there is  no  default  value,  so  if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       there is no assignment at the point the variable is made local, it will be set to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       an empty value (or zero in the case of integers).  The following:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              typeset PATH=/new/directory:$PATH[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       is  valid for temporarily allowing the shell or programmes called from it to find[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the programs in /new/directory inside a function.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Note that the restriction in older versions of zsh  that  local  parameters  were[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       never exported has been removed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mPARAMETERS SET BY THE SHELL[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       In  the  parameter lists that follow, the mark `<S>' indicates that the parameter[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       is special.  `<Z>' indicates that the parameter does not  exist  when  the  shell[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       initializes in sh or ksh emulation mode.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The  parameters  `!',  `#', `*', `-', `?', `@', `$', `ARGC', `HISTCMD', `LINENO',[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       `PPID',  `status',   `TTYIDLE',   `zsh_eval_context',   `ZSH_EVAL_CONTEXT',   and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       `ZSH_SUBSHELL' are read-only and thus cannot be restored by the user, so they are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       not  output  by  `typeset  -p'.   This  also applies to many read-only parameters[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       loaded from modules.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The following parameters are automatically set by the shell:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ! <S>  The process ID of the last command started in the background with  &,  put[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              into the background with the bg builtin, or spawned with coproc.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       # <S>  The  number of positional parameters in decimal.  Note that some confusion[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              may occur with the syntax $#param which substitutes the length  of  param.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Use  ${#} to resolve ambiguities.  In particular, the sequence `$#-...' in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              an arithmetic expression is interpreted as the length of the parameter  -,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              q.v.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ARGC <S> <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Same as #.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       $ <S>  The  process  ID of this shell, set when the shell initializes.  Processes[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              forked from the shell without executing a new  program,  such  as  command[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              substitutions  and  commands grouped with (...), are subshells that dupli‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cate the current shell, and thus substitute the same value for $$ as their[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              parent shell.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       - <S>  Flags supplied to the shell on invocation or by the  set  or  setopt  com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mands.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       * <S>  An array containing the positional parameters.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       argv <S> <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Same as *.  Assigning to argv changes the local positional parameters, but[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              argv  is  not  itself  a local parameter.  Deleting argv with unset in any[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              function deletes it everywhere, although only the innermost positional pa‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rameter array is deleted (so * and @ in other scopes are not affected).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       @ <S>  Same as argv[@], even when argv is not set.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ? <S>  The exit status returned by the last command.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       0 <S>  The name used to invoke the current shell, or as set  by  the  -c  command[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              line option upon invocation.  If the FUNCTION_ARGZERO option is set, $0 is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              set  upon  entry to a shell function to the name of the function, and upon[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              entry to a sourced script to the name of the script, and reset to its pre‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              vious value when the function or script returns.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       status <S> <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Same as ?.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       pipestatus <S> <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              An array containing the exit statuses returned by all commands in the last[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pipeline.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       _ <S>  The last argument of the previous command.  Also, this parameter is set in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the environment of every command executed to the full pathname of the com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mand.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       CPUTYPE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The machine type (microprocessor class or machine model), as determined at[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              run time.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       EGID <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The effective group ID of the shell process.  If you have sufficient priv‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ileges, you may change the effective group ID of the shell process by  as‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              signing to this parameter.  Also (assuming sufficient privileges), you may[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              start  a single command with a different effective group ID by `(EGID=gid;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              command)'[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If this is made local, it is not implicitly set to 0, but may  be  explic‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              itly set locally.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       EUID <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The effective user ID of the shell process.  If you have sufficient privi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              leges,  you  may  change the effective user ID of the shell process by as‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              signing to this parameter.  Also (assuming sufficient privileges), you may[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              start a single command with a different effective user ID  by  `(EUID=uid;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              command)'[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  this  is made local, it is not implicitly set to 0, but may be explic‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              itly set locally.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ERRNO <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The value of errno (see errno(3)) as set by the most recently failed  sys‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tem  call.   This  value is system dependent and is intended for debugging[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              purposes.  It is also useful with the zsh/system module which  allows  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              number to be turned into a name or message.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              To  use  this  parameter,  it  must first be assigned a value (typically 0[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (zero)).  It is initially unset for scripting compatibility.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       FUNCNEST <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Integer.  If greater than or equal to zero, the maximum nesting  depth  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              shell  functions.   When  it  is exceeded, an error is raised at the point[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              where a function is called.  The default  value  is  determined  when  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              shell is configured, but is typically 500.  Increasing the value increases[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  danger  of  a  runaway function recursion causing the shell to crash.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Setting a negative value turns off the check.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       GID <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The real group ID of the shell process.  If  you  have  sufficient  privi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              leges,  you  may  change the group ID of the shell process by assigning to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              this parameter.  Also (assuming sufficient privileges), you  may  start  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              single command under a different group ID by `(GID=gid; command)'[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  this  is made local, it is not implicitly set to 0, but may be explic‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              itly set locally.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HISTCMD[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The current history event number in an interactive shell, in  other  words[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  event number for the command that caused $HISTCMD to be read.  If the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              current history event modifies the history, HISTCMD  changes  to  the  new[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              maximum history event number.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HOST   The current hostname.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       LINENO <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The  line  number  of  the current line within the current script, sourced[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              file, or shell function being executed, whichever  was  started  most  re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cently.   Note  that in the case of shell functions the line number refers[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to the function as it appeared in the original definition, not necessarily[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              as displayed by the functions builtin.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       LOGNAME[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If the corresponding variable is not set in the environment of the  shell,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              it  is  initialized  to  the login name corresponding to the current login[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              session. This parameter is exported by default but this  can  be  disabled[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              using the typeset builtin.  The value is set to the string returned by the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              getlogin(3) system call if that is available.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       MACHTYPE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The machine type (microprocessor class or machine model), as determined at[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              compile time.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       OLDPWD The  previous  working  directory.  This is set when the shell initializes[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and whenever the directory changes.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       OPTARG <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The value of the last option argument processed by the getopts command.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       OPTIND <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The index of the last option argument processed by the getopts command.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       OSTYPE The operating system, as determined at compile time.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       PPID <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The process ID of the parent of the shell, set when the shell initializes.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              As with $$, the value does not change in subshells created as a  duplicate[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of the current shell.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       PWD    The present working directory.  This is set when the shell initializes and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              whenever the directory changes.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       RANDOM <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              A  pseudo-random  integer  from 0 to 32767, newly generated each time this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              parameter is referenced.  The random number generator can be seeded by as‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              signing a numeric value to RANDOM.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The values of RANDOM form an  intentionally-repeatable  pseudo-random  se‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              quence;   subshells   that  reference  RANDOM  will  result  in  identical[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pseudo-random values unless the value of RANDOM is referenced or seeded in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the parent shell in between subshell invocations.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       SECONDS <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The number of seconds since shell invocation.  If this  parameter  is  as‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              signed  a  value, then the value returned upon reference will be the value[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that was assigned plus the number of seconds since the assignment.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Unlike other special parameters, the type of the SECONDS parameter can  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              changed using the typeset command.  The type may be changed only to one of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  floating  point  types  or back to integer.  For example, `typeset -F[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              SECONDS' causes the value to be reported as a floating point number.   The[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              value  is  available  to microsecond accuracy, although the shell may show[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              more or fewer digits depending on the use of typeset.  See the  documenta‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion for the builtin typeset in zshbuiltins(1) for more details.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       SHLVL <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Incremented by one each time a new shell is started.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       signals[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              An array containing the names of the signals.  Note that with the standard[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zsh  numbering  of array indices, where the first element has index 1, the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              signals are offset by 1 from the signal number used by the operating  sys‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tem.   For  example,  on typical Unix-like systems HUP is signal number 1,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              but is referred to as $signals[2].  This is because of EXIT at position  1[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              in  the array, which is used internally by zsh but is not known to the op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              erating system.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TRY_BLOCK_ERROR <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              In an always block, indicates whether the preceding list of code caused an[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              error.  The value is 1 to indicate an error, 0 otherwise.  It may  be  re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              set, clearing the error condition.  See Complex Commands in zshmisc(1)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TRY_BLOCK_INTERRUPT <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  variable  works  in a similar way to TRY_BLOCK_ERROR, but represents[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the status of an interrupt from the signal SIGINT, which  typically  comes[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              from the keyboard when the user types ^C.  If set to 0, any such interrupt[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              will  be  reset;  otherwise,  the interrupt is propagated after the always[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              block.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that it is possible that an interrupt arrives during the execution of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the always block; this interrupt is also propagated.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TTY    The name of the tty associated with the shell, if any.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TTYIDLE <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The idle time of the tty associated with the shell in  seconds  or  -1  if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              there is no such tty.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       UID <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The real user ID of the shell process.  If you have sufficient privileges,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              you  may  change  the user ID of the shell by assigning to this parameter.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Also (assuming sufficient privileges), you may start a single command  un‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              der a different user ID by `(UID=uid; command)'[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  this  is made local, it is not implicitly set to 0, but may be explic‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              itly set locally.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       USERNAME <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The username corresponding to the real user ID of the shell  process.   If[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              you  have sufficient privileges, you may change the username (and also the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              user ID and group ID) of the shell by assigning to this  parameter.   Also[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (assuming  sufficient  privileges), you may start a single command under a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              different username (and user ID and group ID) by `(USERNAME=username; com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mand)'[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       VENDOR The vendor, as determined at compile time.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh_eval_context <S> <Z> (ZSH_EVAL_CONTEXT <S>)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              An array (colon-separated list) indicating the context of shell code  that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is  being  run.  Each time a piece of shell code that is stored within the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              shell is executed a string is temporarily appended to the array  to  indi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cate the type of operation that is being performed.  Read in order the ar‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ray  gives  an  indication of the stack of operations being performed with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the most immediate context last.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that the variable does not give information on syntactic context such[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              as pipelines or subshells.  Use $ZSH_SUBSHELL to detect subshells.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The context is one of the following:[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cmdarg Code specified by the -c option to the command  line  that  invoked[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the shell.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              cmdsubst[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Command substitution using the `...` or $(...) construct.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              equalsubst[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     File substitution using the =(...) construct.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              eval   Code executed by the eval builtin.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              evalautofunc[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Code executed with the KSH_AUTOLOAD mechanism in order to define an[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     autoloaded function.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              fc     Code  from  the  shell  history executed by the -e option to the fc[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     builtin.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              file   Lines of code being read directly from a file, for example  by  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     source builtin.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              filecode[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Lines  of code being read from a .zwc file instead of directly from[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the source file.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              globqual[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Code executed by the e or + glob qualifier.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              globsort[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Code executed to order files by the o glob qualifier.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              insubst[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     File substitution using the <(...) construct.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              loadautofunc[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Code read directly from a file to define an autoloaded function.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              outsubst[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     File substitution using the >(...) construct.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              sched  Code executed by the sched builtin.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              shfunc A shell function.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              stty   Code passed to stty by the  STTY  environment  variable.   Normally[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     this is passed directly to the system's stty command, so this value[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     is unlikely to be seen in practice.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              style  Code  executed  as  part of a style retrieved by the zstyle builtin[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     from the zsh/zutil module.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              toplevel[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     The highest execution level of a script or interactive shell.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              trap   Code executed as a trap defined by the trap builtin.  Traps defined[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     as functions have the context shfunc.  As  traps  are  asynchronous[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     they may have a different hierarchy from other code.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              zpty   Code executed by the zpty builtin from the zsh/zpty module.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              zregexparse-guard[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Code  executed  as  a  guard  by  the  zregexparse command from the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     zsh/zutil module.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              zregexparse-action[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Code executed as an action by  the  zregexparse  command  from  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     zsh/zutil module.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ZSH_ARGZERO[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  zsh was invoked to run a script, this is the name of the script.  Oth‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              erwise, it is the name used to invoke the current shell.  This is the same[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              as the value of $0 when the POSIX_ARGZERO option is  set,  but  is  always[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              available.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ZSH_EXECUTION_STRING[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  the  shell  was started with the option -c, this contains the argument[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              passed to the option.  Otherwise it is not set.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ZSH_NAME[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Expands to the basename of the command used to  invoke  this  instance  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zsh.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ZSH_PATCHLEVEL[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The  output of `git describe --tags --long' for the zsh repository used to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              build the shell.  This is most useful in order to keep track  of  versions[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of  the shell during development between releases; hence most users should[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              not use it and should instead rely on $ZSH_VERSION.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh_scheduled_events[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              See the section `The zsh/sched Module' in zshmodules(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ZSH_SCRIPT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If zsh was invoked to run a script, this is the name of the script, other‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              wise it is unset.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ZSH_SUBSHELL[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Readonly integer.  Initially zero, incremented each time the  shell  forks[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to  create  a  subshell for executing code.  Hence `(print $ZSH_SUBSHELL)'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and `print $(print $ZSH_SUBSHELL)' output 1,  while  `(  (print  $ZSH_SUB‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              SHELL) )' outputs 2.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ZSH_VERSION[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The version number of the release of zsh.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mPARAMETERS USED BY THE SHELL[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The  following parameters are used by the shell.  Again, `<S>' indicates that the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       parameter is special and `<Z>' indicates that the parameter does not  exist  when[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the shell initializes in sh or ksh emulation mode.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       In  cases where there are two parameters with an upper- and lowercase form of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       same name, such as path and PATH, the lowercase form is an array and  the  upper‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       case  form  is a scalar with the elements of the array joined together by colons.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       These are similar to tied parameters created via `typeset -T'.   The  normal  use[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       for the colon-separated form is for exporting to the environment, while the array[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       form is easier to manipulate within the shell.  Note that unsetting either of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       pair  will  unset the other; they retain their special properties when recreated,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       and recreating one of the pair will recreate the other.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ARGV0  If exported, its value is used as the argv[0] of external commands.   Usu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ally used in constructs like `ARGV0=emacs nethack'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       BAUD   The  rate in bits per second at which data reaches the terminal.  The line[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              editor will use this value in order to compensate for a slow  terminal  by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              delaying  updates to the display until necessary.  If the parameter is un‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              set or the value is zero the compensation mechanism is  turned  off.   The[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              parameter is not set by default.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              This parameter may be profitably set in some circumstances, e.g.  for slow[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              modems  dialing  into a communications server, or on a slow wide area net‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              work.  It should be set to the baud rate of the slowest part of  the  link[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for best performance.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       cdpath <S> <Z> (CDPATH <S>)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              An  array (colon-separated list) of directories specifying the search path[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for the cd command.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       COLUMNS <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The number of columns for this terminal session.  Used for printing select[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              lists and for the line editor.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       CORRECT_IGNORE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If set, is treated as a pattern during spelling correction.  Any potential[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              correction that matches the pattern is ignored.  For example, if the value[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is `_*' then completion functions (which, by convention, have names begin‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ning with `_') will never be offered as spelling corrections.  The pattern[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              does not apply to the correction of file names, as  applied  by  the  COR‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              RECT_ALL  option  (so with the example just given files beginning with `_'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              in the current directory would still be completed).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       CORRECT_IGNORE_FILE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If set, is treated as a pattern during spelling correction of file  names.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Any  file  name that matches the pattern is never offered as a correction.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              For example, if the value is `.*' then dot file names will  never  be  of‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fered  as  spelling  corrections.  This is useful with the CORRECT_ALL op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       DIRSTACKSIZE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The maximum size of the directory stack, by default there is no limit.  If[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the stack gets larger than this, it will be truncated automatically.  This[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is useful with the AUTO_PUSHD option.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ENV    If the ENV environment variable is set when zsh is invoked as sh  or  ksh,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              $ENV  is sourced after the profile scripts.  The value of ENV is subjected[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to parameter expansion, command substitution, and arithmetic expansion be‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fore being interpreted as a pathname.  Note that ENV is  not  used  unless[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the shell is interactive and zsh is emulating sh or ksh.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       FCEDIT The  default editor for the fc builtin.  If FCEDIT is not set, the parame‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ter EDITOR is used; if that is not set either, a builtin default,  usually[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              vi, is used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       fignore <S> <Z> (FIGNORE <S>)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              An array (colon separated list) containing the suffixes of files to be ig‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              nored  during  filename completion.  However, if completion only generates[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              files with suffixes in this list, then these files are completed anyway.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       fpath <S> <Z> (FPATH <S>)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              An array (colon separated list) of directories specifying the search  path[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for  function definitions.  This path is searched when a function with the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -u attribute is referenced.  If an executable file is found,  then  it  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              read and executed in the current environment.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       histchars <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Three  characters  used by the shell's history and lexical analysis mecha‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              nism.  The first character signals the start of a history  expansion  (de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fault  `!').   The  second  character signals the start of a quick history[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              substitution (default `^').  The third character is the comment  character[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (default `#').[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The  characters  must  be  in  the ASCII character set; any attempt to set[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              histchars to characters with a locale-dependent meaning will  be  rejected[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              with an error message.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HISTCHARS <S> <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Same as histchars.  (Deprecated.)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HISTFILE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The  file  to save the history in when an interactive shell exits.  If un‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              set, the history is not saved.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HISTORY_IGNORE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If set, is treated as a pattern at the time  history  files  are  written.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Any  potential history entry that matches the pattern is skipped.  For ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ample, if the value is `fc *' then commands that  invoke  the  interactive[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              history editor are never written to the history file.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that HISTORY_IGNORE defines a single pattern: to specify alternatives[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              use the `(first|second|...)' syntax.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Compare  the  HIST_NO_STORE  option  or  the zshaddhistory hook, either of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              which would prevent such commands from being added to the interactive his‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tory at all.  If you wish to use  HISTORY_IGNORE  to  stop  history  being[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              added in the first place, you can define the following hook:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     zshaddhistory() {[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                       emulate -L zsh[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                       ## uncomment if HISTORY_IGNORE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                       ## should use EXTENDED_GLOB syntax[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                       # setopt extendedglob[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                       [[ $1 != ${~HISTORY_IGNORE} ]][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     }[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HISTSIZE <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The  maximum number of events stored in the internal history list.  If you[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              use the HIST_EXPIRE_DUPS_FIRST option, setting this value larger than  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              SAVEHIST  size will give you the difference as a cushion for saving dupli‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cated history events.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If this is made local, it is not implicitly set to 0, but may  be  explic‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              itly set locally.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HOME <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The default argument for the cd command.  This is not set automatically by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  shell in sh, ksh or csh emulation, but it is typically present in the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              environment anyway, and if it becomes set it has its usual special  behav‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              iour.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       IFS <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Internal  field  separators (by default space, tab, newline and NUL), that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              are used to separate words which result from command or  parameter  expan‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sion  and  words  read  by  the read builtin.  Any characters from the set[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              space, tab and newline that appear in the IFS are called IFS white  space.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              One  or more IFS white space characters or one non-IFS white space charac‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ter together with any adjacent IFS white space character delimit a  field.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  an  IFS  white space character appears twice consecutively in the IFS,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              this character is treated as if it were not an IFS white space character.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If the parameter is unset, the default is used.  Note this has a different[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              effect from setting the parameter to an empty string.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       KEYBOARD_HACK[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This variable defines a character to be removed from the end of  the  com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mand line before interpreting it (interactive shells only). It is intended[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to  fix  the  problem  with keys placed annoyingly close to return and re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              places the SUNKEYBOARDHACK option which  did  this  for  backquotes  only.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Should  the  chosen  character be one of singlequote, doublequote or back‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              quote, there must also be an odd number of them on the  command  line  for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the last one to be removed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              For  backward  compatibility,  if the SUNKEYBOARDHACK option is explicitly[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              set, the value of KEYBOARD_HACK reverts to backquote.  If  the  option  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              explicitly unset, this variable is set to empty.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       KEYTIMEOUT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The  time the shell waits, in hundredths of seconds, for another key to be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pressed when reading bound multi-character sequences.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       LANG <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This variable determines the locale category for any category not specifi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cally selected via a variable starting with `LC_'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       LC_ALL <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This variable overrides the value of the `LANG' variable and the value  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              any of the other variables starting with `LC_'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       LC_COLLATE <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  variable  determines the locale category for character collation in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              formation within ranges in glob brackets and for sorting.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       LC_CTYPE <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This variable determines the locale category for character handling  func‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tions.   If the MULTIBYTE option is in effect this variable or LANG should[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              contain a value that reflects the character set in use, even if  it  is  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              single-byte  character  set, unless only the 7-bit subset (ASCII) is used.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              For example, if the character set is ISO-8859-1, a suitable value might be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              en_US.iso88591 (certain Linux distributions) or en_US.ISO8859-1 (MacOS).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       LC_MESSAGES <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This variable determines the language in which messages should be written.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that zsh does not use message catalogs.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       LC_NUMERIC <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This variable affects the decimal point character and thousands  separator[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              character  for  the formatted input/output functions and string conversion[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              functions.  Note that zsh ignores this setting when parsing floating point[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mathematical expressions.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       LC_TIME <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This variable determines the locale category for date and time  formatting[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              in prompt escape sequences.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       LINES <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The  number  of lines for this terminal session.  Used for printing select[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              lists and for the line editor.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       LISTMAX[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              In the line editor, the number of matches to list without asking first. If[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the value is negative, the list will be shown if it spans at most as  many[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              lines as given by the absolute value.  If set to zero, the shell asks only[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              if the top of the listing would scroll off the screen.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       MAIL   If this parameter is set and mailpath is not set, the shell looks for mail[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              in the specified file.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       MAILCHECK[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The interval in seconds between checks for new mail.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       mailpath <S> <Z> (MAILPATH <S>)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              An  array (colon-separated list) of filenames to check for new mail.  Each[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              filename can be followed by a `?' and a message that will be printed.  The[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              message will undergo parameter expansion, command substitution and  arith‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              metic  expansion with the variable $_ defined as the name of the file that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              has changed.  The default message is `You have new mail'.  If  an  element[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is  a  directory  instead of a file the shell will recursively check every[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              file in every subdirectory of the element.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       manpath <S> <Z> (MANPATH <S> <Z>)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              An array (colon-separated list) whose value is not used by the shell.  The[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              manpath array can be useful, however, since setting it also sets  MANPATH,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and vice versa.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       match[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       mbegin[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       mend   Arrays  set  by  the  shell  when  the  b globbing flag is used in pattern[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              matches.  See the subsection Globbing flags in the documentation for File‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              name Generation in zshexpn(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       MATCH[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       MBEGIN[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       MEND   Set by the shell when the m globbing flag is used in pattern matches.  See[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the subsection Globbing flags in the documentation for Filename Generation[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              in zshexpn(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       module_path <S> <Z> (MODULE_PATH <S>)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              An array (colon-separated list) of directories that zmodload searches  for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              dynamically loadable modules.  This is initialized to a standard pathname,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              usually  `/usr/local/lib/zsh/$ZSH_VERSION'.   (The  `/usr/local/lib'  part[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              varies from installation to  installation.)   For  security  reasons,  any[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              value set in the environment when the shell is started will be ignored.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              These  parameters  only  exist if the installation supports dynamic module[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              loading.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       NULLCMD <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The command name to assume if a redirection is specified with no  command.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Defaults to cat.  For sh/ksh behavior, change this to :.  For csh-like be‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              havior,  unset  this  parameter;  the shell will print an error message if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              null commands are entered.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       path <S> <Z> (PATH <S>)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              An array (colon-separated list) of directories  to  search  for  commands.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When  this parameter is set, each directory is scanned and all files found[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              are put in a hash table.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       POSTEDIT <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This string is output whenever the line editor exits.  It usually contains[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              termcap strings to reset the terminal.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       PROMPT <S> <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       PROMPT2 <S> <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       PROMPT3 <S> <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       PROMPT4 <S> <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Same as PS1, PS2, PS3 and PS4, respectively.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       prompt <S> <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Same as PS1.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       PROMPT_EOL_MARK[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When the PROMPT_CR and PROMPT_SP options are set, the PROMPT_EOL_MARK  pa‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rameter  can  be used to customize how the end of partial lines are shown.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This parameter undergoes prompt expansion, with the PROMPT_PERCENT  option[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              set.   If  not  set,  the  default  behavior  is  equivalent  to the value[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `%B%S%#%s%b'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       PS1 <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The primary prompt string, printed before a command is read.  It undergoes[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              a special form of expansion  before  being  displayed;  see  EXPANSION  OF[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              PROMPT SEQUENCES in zshmisc(1).  The default is `%m%# '.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       PS2 <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The  secondary  prompt,  printed  when the shell needs more information to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              complete a command.  It is expanded in the same way as PS1.   The  default[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is  `%_>  ',  which displays any shell constructs or quotation marks which[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              are currently being processed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       PS3 <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Selection prompt used within a select loop.  It is expanded  in  the  same[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              way as PS1.  The default is `?# '.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       PS4 <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The  execution  trace  prompt.   Default is `+%N:%i> ', which displays the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              name of the current shell structure and the line number within it.  In  sh[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              or ksh emulation, the default is `+ '.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       psvar <S> <Z> (PSVAR <S>)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              An  array  (colon-separated  list)  whose  elements  can be used in PROMPT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              strings.  Setting psvar also sets PSVAR, and vice versa.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       READNULLCMD <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The command name to assume if a single input redirection is specified with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              no command.  Defaults to more.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       REPORTMEMORY[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If nonnegative, commands whose maximum resident set size  (roughly  speak‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ing,  main memory usage) in kilobytes is greater than this value have tim‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ing statistics reported.  The format used  to  output  statistics  is  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              value  of  the  TIMEFMT parameter, which is the same as for the REPORTTIME[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              variable and the time builtin; note that by default this does  not  output[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              memory  usage.   Appending " max RSS %M" to the value of TIMEFMT causes it[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to output the value that triggered the report.  If REPORTTIME is  also  in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              use,  at  most a single report is printed for both triggers.  This feature[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              requires  the  getrusage()  system  call,  commonly  supported  by  modern[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Unix-like systems.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       REPORTTIME[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  nonnegative,  commands  whose combined user and system execution times[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (measured in seconds) are greater than this value have  timing  statistics[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              printed  for  them.  Output is suppressed for commands executed within the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              line editor, including completion; commands  explicitly  marked  with  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              time keyword still cause the summary to be printed in this case.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       REPLY  This  parameter  is  reserved  by convention to pass string values between[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              shell scripts and shell builtins in situations where a  function  call  or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              redirection  are  impossible or undesirable.  The read builtin and the se‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              lect complex command may set REPLY, and filename generation both sets  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              examines its value when evaluating certain expressions.  Some modules also[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              employ REPLY for similar purposes.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       reply  As REPLY, but for array values rather than strings.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       RPROMPT <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       RPS1 <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  prompt  is  displayed  on the right-hand side of the screen when the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              primary prompt is being displayed on the left.  This does not work if  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              SINGLE_LINE_ZLE option is set.  It is expanded in the same way as PS1.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       RPROMPT2 <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       RPS2 <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  prompt  is  displayed  on the right-hand side of the screen when the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              secondary prompt is being displayed on the left.  This does  not  work  if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the SINGLE_LINE_ZLE option is set.  It is expanded in the same way as PS2.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       SAVEHIST[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The maximum number of history events to save in the history file.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  this  is made local, it is not implicitly set to 0, but may be explic‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              itly set locally.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       SPROMPT <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The prompt used for spelling correction.  The sequence `%R' expands to the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              string which presumably needs spelling correction, and `%r' expands to the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              proposed correction.  All other prompt escapes are also allowed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The actions available at the prompt are [nyae]:[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              n (`no') (default)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Discard the correction and run the command.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              y (`yes')[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Make the correction and run the command.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              a (`abort')[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Discard the entire command line without running it.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              e (`edit')[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Resume editing the command line.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       STTY   If this parameter is set in a command's environment, the  shell  runs  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              stty command with the value of this parameter as arguments in order to set[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              up  the terminal before executing the command. The modes apply only to the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              command, and are reset when it finishes or is suspended. If the command is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              suspended and continued later with the fg or wait builtins it will see the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              modes specified by STTY, as if it were not  suspended.   This  (intention‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ally)  does  not apply if the command is continued via `kill -CONT'.  STTY[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is ignored if the command is run in the background, or if it is in the en‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              vironment of the shell but not explicitly assigned to in the  input  line.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This avoids running stty at every external command by accidentally export‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ing  it. Also note that STTY should not be used for window size specifica‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tions; these will not be local to the command.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If the parameter is set and empty, all of the above  applies  except  that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              stty  is  not  run. This can be useful as a way to freeze the tty around a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              single command, blocking its changes  to  tty  settings,  similar  to  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ttyctl builtin.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TERM <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The  type  of  terminal  in use.  This is used when looking up termcap se‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              quences.  An assignment to TERM causes zsh to re-initialize the  terminal,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              even  if  the value does not change (e.g., `TERM=$TERM').  It is necessary[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to make such an assignment upon any  change  to  the  terminal  definition[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              database or terminal type in order for the new settings to take effect.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TERMINFO <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              A reference to your terminfo database, used by the `terminfo' library when[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  system  has  it;  see  terminfo(5).  If set, this causes the shell to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              reinitialise the terminal, making the workaround `TERM=$TERM' unnecessary.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TERMINFO_DIRS <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              A colon-seprarated list of terminfo databases, used by the `terminfo'  li‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              brary  when the system has it; see terminfo(5). This variable is only used[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              by certain terminal libraries, in particular ncurses; see  terminfo(5)  to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              check  support  on  your  system.  If set, this causes the shell to reini‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tialise the terminal,  making  the  workaround  `TERM=$TERM'  unnecessary.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Note  that  unlike  other colon-separated arrays this is not tied to a zsh[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              array.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TIMEFMT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The format of process time reports with the time keyword.  The default  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `%J  %U user %S system %P cpu %*E total'.  Recognizes the following escape[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sequences, although not all may be available on all systems, and some that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              are available may not be useful:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              %%     A `%'.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %U     CPU seconds spent in user mode.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %S     CPU seconds spent in kernel mode.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %E     Elapsed time in seconds.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %P     The CPU percentage, computed as 100*(%U+%S)/%E.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %W     Number of times the process was swapped.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %X     The average amount in (shared) text space used in kilobytes.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %D     The  average  amount  in  (unshared) data/stack space used in kilo‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     bytes.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %K     The total space used (%X+%D) in kilobytes.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %M     The  maximum memory the process had in use at  any  time  in  kilo‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     bytes.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %F     The  number  of  major  page faults (page needed to be brought from[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     disk).[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %R     The number of minor page faults.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %I     The number of input operations.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %O     The number of output operations.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %r     The number of socket messages received.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %s     The number of socket messages sent.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %k     The number of signals received.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %w     Number of voluntary context switches (waits).[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %c     Number of involuntary context switches.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %J     The name of this job.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              A star may be inserted between the percent sign and  flags  printing  time[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (e.g., `%*E'); this causes the time to be printed in `hh:mm:ss.ttt' format[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (hours and minutes are only printed if they are not zero).  Alternatively,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `m'  or  `u' may be used (e.g., `%mE') to produce time output in millisec‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              onds or microseconds, respectively.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TMOUT  If this parameter is nonzero, the shell will receive an ALRM signal  if  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              command  is not entered within the specified number of seconds after issu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ing a prompt. If there is a trap on SIGALRM, it will be executed and a new[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              alarm is scheduled using the value of the TMOUT parameter after  executing[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  trap.   If  no  trap is set, and the idle time of the terminal is not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              less than the value of the TMOUT parameter, zsh terminates.   Otherwise  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              new alarm is scheduled to TMOUT seconds after the last keypress.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TMPPREFIX[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              A  pathname prefix which the shell will use for all temporary files.  Note[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that this should include an initial part for the file name as well as  any[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              directory names.  The default is `/tmp/zsh'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TMPSUFFIX[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              A  filename suffix which the shell will use for temporary files created by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              process substitutions (e.g., `=(list)').  Note that the value  should  in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              clude a leading dot `.' if intended to be interpreted as a file extension.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The default is not to append any suffix, thus this parameter should be as‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              signed only when needed and then unset again.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       WORDCHARS <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              A  list  of  non-alphanumeric  characters considered part of a word by the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              line editor.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ZBEEP  If set, this gives a string of characters, which  can  use  all  the  same[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              codes  as  the bindkey command as described in the zsh/zle module entry in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zshmodules(1), that will be output to the  terminal  instead  of  beeping.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  may  have  a  visible instead of an audible effect; for example, the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              string `\e[?5h\e[?5l' on a vt100 or xterm will have the effect of flashing[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              reverse video on and off (if you usually use reverse video, you should use[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the string `\e[?5l\e[?5h' instead).  This takes precedence over the NOBEEP[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              option.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ZDOTDIR[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The directory to search for shell startup  files  (.zshrc,  etc),  if  not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              $HOME.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zle_bracketed_paste[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Many  terminal  emulators have a feature that allows applications to iden‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tify when text is pasted into the terminal rather than  being  typed  nor‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mally.  For  ZLE, this means that special characters such as tabs and new‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              lines can be inserted instead of invoking editor  commands.   Furthermore,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pasted  text forms a single undo event and if the region is active, pasted[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              text will replace the region.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              This two-element array contains the terminal escape sequences for enabling[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and disabling the feature. These  escape  sequences  are  used  to  enable[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              bracketed  paste when ZLE is active and disable it at other times.  Unset‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ting the parameter has the effect of ensuring that bracketed paste remains[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              disabled.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zle_highlight[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              An array describing contexts in which ZLE should highlight the input text.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              See Character Highlighting in zshzle(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ZLE_LINE_ABORTED[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This parameter is set by the line editor when an error  occurs.   It  con‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tains  the  line  that was being edited at the point of the error.  `print[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -zr -- $ZLE_LINE_ABORTED' can be used to recover the line.  Only the  most[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              recent line of this kind is remembered.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ZLE_REMOVE_SUFFIX_CHARS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ZLE_SPACE_SUFFIX_CHARS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              These  parameters  are  used by the line editor.  In certain circumstances[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              suffixes (typically space or slash) added by the completion system will be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              removed automatically, either because the next editing command was not  an[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              insertable character, or because the character was marked as requiring the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              suffix to be removed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              These  variables  can  contain  the sets of characters that will cause the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              suffix to be removed.  If ZLE_REMOVE_SUFFIX_CHARS is set, those characters[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              will cause the suffix to be removed;  if  ZLE_SPACE_SUFFIX_CHARS  is  set,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              those  characters  will  cause  the suffix to be removed and replaced by a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              space.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If ZLE_REMOVE_SUFFIX_CHARS is not set, the default behaviour is equivalent[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     ZLE_REMOVE_SUFFIX_CHARS=$' \t\n;&|'[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If ZLE_REMOVE_SUFFIX_CHARS is set but is empty, no  characters  have  this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              behaviour.   ZLE_SPACE_SUFFIX_CHARS  takes precedence, so that the follow‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ing:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     ZLE_SPACE_SUFFIX_CHARS=$'&|'[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              causes the characters `&' and `|' to remove the suffix but to  replace  it[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              with a space.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              To illustrate the difference, suppose that the option AUTO_REMOVE_SLASH is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              in  effect and the directory DIR has just been completed, with an appended[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              /, following which the user types `&'.   The  default  result  is  `DIR&'.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              With  ZLE_REMOVE_SUFFIX_CHARS  set but without including `&' the result is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `DIR/&'.  With ZLE_SPACE_SUFFIX_CHARS set to include  `&'  the  result  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `DIR &'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note  that certain completions may provide their own suffix removal or re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              placement behaviour which overrides the values described  here.   See  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              completion system documentation in zshcompsys(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ZLE_RPROMPT_INDENT <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  set,  used  to give the indentation between the right hand side of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              right prompt in the line editor as given by RPS1 or RPROMPT and the  right[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              hand side of the screen.  If not set, the value 1 is used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Typically  this  will be used to set the value to 0 so that the prompt ap‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pears flush with the right hand side of the screen.  This is not  the  de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fault  as  many terminals do not handle this correctly, in particular when[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the prompt appears at the extreme bottom right of the screen.  Recent vir‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tual terminals are more likely to handle this case correctly.  Some exper‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              imentation is necessary.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mzsh 5.9                               May 14, 2022                           ZSHPARAM(1)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────[0m
[38;2;248;248;242mZSHOPTIONS(1)                    General Commands Manual                   ZSHOPTIONS(1)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mNAME[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zshoptions - zsh options[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mSPECIFYING OPTIONS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Options are primarily referred to by name.  These names are case insensitive  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       underscores  are  ignored.   For  example,  `allexport'  is equivalent to `A__ll‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       eXP_ort'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The sense of an option name may be inverted by preceding it with `no', so `setopt[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       No_Beep' is equivalent to `unsetopt beep'.  This inversion can only be done once,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       so `nonobeep' is not a synonym for `beep'.  Similarly, `tify' is  not  a  synonym[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       for `nonotify' (the inversion of `notify').[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Some  options  also  have one or more single letter names.  There are two sets of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       single letter options: one used by default, and another used  to  emulate  sh/ksh[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       (used  when  the SH_OPTION_LETTERS option is set).  The single letter options can[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       be used on the shell command line, or with the set, setopt and unsetopt builtins,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       as normal Unix options preceded by `-'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The sense of the single letter options may be inverted by using  `+'  instead  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       `-'.   Some  of  the  single letter option names refer to an option being off, in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       which case the inversion of that name refers to the option being on.   For  exam‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ple,  `+n'  is the short name of `exec', and `-n' is the short name of its inver‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       sion, `noexec'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       In strings of single letter options supplied to the shell  at  startup,  trailing[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       whitespace  will be ignored; for example the string `-f    ' will be treated just[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       as `-f', but the string `-f i' is an error.  This is because many  systems  which[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       implement  the  `#!'  mechanism  for calling scripts do not strip trailing white‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       space.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       It is possible for options to be set within a function scope.  See  the  descrip‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tion of the option LOCAL_OPTIONS below.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mDESCRIPTION OF OPTIONS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       In  the  following list, options set by default in all emulations are marked <D>;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       those set by default only in csh, ksh, sh, or zsh emulations are marked <C>, <K>,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       <S>, <Z> as appropriate.  When listing options (by `setopt', `unsetopt', `set -o'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       or `set +o'), those turned on by default appear in the list prefixed  with  `no'.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Hence (unless KSH_OPTION_PRINT is set), `setopt' shows all options whose settings[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       are changed from the default.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Changing Directories[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       AUTO_CD (-J)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If a command is issued that can't be executed as a normal command, and the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              command  is the name of a directory, perform the cd command to that direc‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tory.  This option is only applicable if the  option  SHIN_STDIN  is  set,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              i.e.  if  commands  are being read from standard input.  The option is de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              signed for interactive use; it is recommended that cd be  used  explicitly[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              in scripts to avoid ambiguity.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       AUTO_PUSHD (-N)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Make cd push the old directory onto the directory stack.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       CDABLE_VARS (-T)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  the argument to a cd command (or an implied cd with the AUTO_CD option[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              set) is not a directory, and does not begin with a slash,  try  to  expand[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  expression as if it were preceded by a `~' (see the section `Filename[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Expansion').[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       CD_SILENT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Never print the working directory after a cd (whether explicit or  implied[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              with  the  AUTO_CD  option  set). cd normally prints the working directory[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              when the argument given to it was -, a stack entry, or the name of  a  di‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rectory  found  under  CDPATH.  Note  that  this  is distinct from pushd's[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              stack-printing behaviour, which is controlled by PUSHD_SILENT. This option[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              overrides the printing-related effects of POSIX_CD.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       CHASE_DOTS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When changing to a directory containing a path segment  `..'  which  would[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              otherwise  be  treated  as  canceling the previous segment in the path (in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              other words, `foo/..' would be removed from the path, or if  `..'  is  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              first  part  of  the  path, the last part of the current working directory[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              would be removed), instead resolve the path  to  the  physical  directory.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This option is overridden by CHASE_LINKS.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              For  example, suppose /foo/bar is a link to the directory /alt/rod.  With‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              out this option set, `cd /foo/bar/..' changes to /foo;  with  it  set,  it[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              changes  to  /alt.   The same applies if the current directory is /foo/bar[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and `cd ..' is used.  Note that all other symbolic links in the path  will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              also be resolved.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       CHASE_LINKS (-w)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Resolve symbolic links to their true values when changing directory.  This[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              also  has  the  effect  of  CHASE_DOTS,  i.e.  a `..' path segment will be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              treated as referring to the physical parent, even if  the  preceding  path[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              segment is a symbolic link.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       POSIX_CD <K> <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Modifies  the  behaviour of cd, chdir and pushd commands to make them more[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              compatible with the POSIX standard. The behaviour with the option unset is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              described in the documentation for the cd builtin in  zshbuiltins(1).   If[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the option is set, the shell does not test for directories beneath the lo‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cal  directory  (`.')  until  after  all  directories  in cdpath have been[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tested, and the cd and chdir commands do not recognise  arguments  of  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              form `{+|-}n' as directory stack entries.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Also,  if  the  option is set, the conditions under which the shell prints[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the new directory after changing to it are modified.  It is no longer  re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              stricted  to  interactive shells (although printing of the directory stack[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              with pushd is still limited to interactive shells); and any use of a  com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ponent of CDPATH, including a `.' but excluding an empty component that is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              otherwise treated as `.', causes the directory to be printed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       PUSHD_IGNORE_DUPS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Don't push multiple copies of the same directory onto the directory stack.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       PUSHD_MINUS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Exchanges the meanings of `+' and `-' when used with a number to specify a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              directory in the stack.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       PUSHD_SILENT (-E)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Do not print the directory stack after pushd or popd.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       PUSHD_TO_HOME (-D)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Have pushd with no arguments act like `pushd $HOME'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Completion[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ALWAYS_LAST_PROMPT <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  unset,  key  functions that list completions try to return to the last[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              prompt if given a numeric argument. If set these functions try  to  return[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to the last prompt if given no numeric argument.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ALWAYS_TO_END[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  a  completion  is  performed with the cursor within a word, and a full[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              completion is inserted, the cursor is moved to the end of the word.   That[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is, the cursor is moved to the end of the word if either a single match is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              inserted or menu completion is performed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       AUTO_LIST (-9) <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Automatically list choices on an ambiguous completion.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       AUTO_MENU <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Automatically use menu completion after the second consecutive request for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              completion, for example by pressing the tab key repeatedly. This option is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              overridden by MENU_COMPLETE.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       AUTO_NAME_DIRS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Any  parameter that is set to the absolute name of a directory immediately[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              becomes a name for that directory, that will be used by the `%~'  and  re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              lated prompt sequences, and will be available when completion is performed[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              on  a  word  starting with `~'.  (Otherwise, the parameter must be used in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the form `~param' first.)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       AUTO_PARAM_KEYS <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If a parameter name was completed and a following  character  (normally  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              space)  automatically  inserted,  and  the  next character typed is one of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              those that have to come directly after the name (like `}', `:', etc.), the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              automatically added character is deleted,  so  that  the  character  typed[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              comes  immediately after the parameter name.  Completion in a brace expan‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sion is affected similarly: the added character is a `,',  which  will  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              removed if `}' is typed next.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       AUTO_PARAM_SLASH <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If a parameter is completed whose content is the name of a directory, then[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              add a trailing slash instead of a space.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       AUTO_REMOVE_SLASH <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When  the  last  character  resulting from a completion is a slash and the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              next character typed is a word delimiter, a slash,  or  a  character  that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ends a command (such as a semicolon or an ampersand), remove the slash.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       BASH_AUTO_LIST[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              On an ambiguous completion, automatically list choices when the completion[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              function  is  called  twice  in  succession.   This  takes precedence over[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              AUTO_LIST.  The setting of LIST_AMBIGUOUS is respected.  If  AUTO_MENU  is[0m
