[38;2;248;248;242m              set,  the  menu behaviour will then start with the third press.  Note that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              this will not work with MENU_COMPLETE, since repeated completion calls im‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mediately cycle through the list in that case.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       COMPLETE_ALIASES[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Prevents aliases on the command line from being internally substituted be‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fore completion is attempted.  The effect is to make the alias a  distinct[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              command for completion purposes.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       COMPLETE_IN_WORD[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  unset,  the  cursor  is  set  to  the end of the word if completion is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              started. Otherwise it stays there and completion is done from both ends.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       GLOB_COMPLETE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When the current word has a glob pattern, do not insert all the words  re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sulting  from the expansion but generate matches as for completion and cy‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cle through them like MENU_COMPLETE. The matches are generated as if a `*'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              was added to the end of the word, or inserted  at  the  cursor  when  COM‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              PLETE_IN_WORD  is set.  This actually uses pattern matching, not globbing,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              so it works not only for files but for any completion,  such  as  options,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              user names, etc.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that when the pattern matcher is used, matching control (for example,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              case-insensitive  or  anchored  matching) cannot be used.  This limitation[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              only applies when the current word contains a pattern; simply  turning  on[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the GLOB_COMPLETE option does not have this effect.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HASH_LIST_ALL <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Whenever  a  command  completion or spelling correction is attempted, make[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sure the entire command path is hashed first.  This makes the  first  com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pletion slower but avoids false reports of spelling errors.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       LIST_AMBIGUOUS <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  option works when AUTO_LIST or BASH_AUTO_LIST is also set.  If there[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is an unambiguous prefix to insert on the command line, that is done with‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              out a completion list being displayed; in other words, auto-listing behav‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              iour only takes place when nothing would be  inserted.   In  the  case  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              BASH_AUTO_LIST, this means that the list will be delayed to the third call[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of the function.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       LIST_BEEP <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Beep on an ambiguous completion.  More accurately, this forces the comple‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion  widgets  to return status 1 on an ambiguous completion, which causes[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the shell to beep if the option BEEP is also set; this may be modified  if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              completion is called from a user-defined widget.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       LIST_PACKED[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Try to make the completion list smaller (occupying less lines) by printing[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the matches in columns with different widths.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       LIST_ROWS_FIRST[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Lay  out the matches in completion lists sorted horizontally, that is, the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              second match is to the right of the first one, not under it as usual.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       LIST_TYPES (-X) <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When listing files that are possible completions, show the  type  of  each[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              file with a trailing identifying mark.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       MENU_COMPLETE (-Y)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              On  an  ambiguous completion, instead of listing possibilities or beeping,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              insert the first match immediately.  Then  when  completion  is  requested[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              again,  remove  the  first  match  and insert the second match, etc.  When[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              there are  no  more  matches,  go  back  to  the  first  one  again.   re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              verse-menu-complete  may be used to loop through the list in the other di‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rection. This option overrides AUTO_MENU.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       REC_EXACT (-S)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If the string on the command line exactly matches one of the possible com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pletions, it is accepted, even if there is another completion  (i.e.  that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              string with something else added) that also matches.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Expansion and Globbing[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       BAD_PATTERN (+2) <C> <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  a pattern for filename generation is badly formed, print an error mes‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sage.  (If this option is unset, the pattern will be left unchanged.)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       BARE_GLOB_QUAL <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              In a glob pattern, treat a trailing set  of  parentheses  as  a  qualifier[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              list,  if it contains no `|', `(' or (if special) `~' characters.  See the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              section `Filename Generation'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       BRACE_CCL[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Expand expressions in braces which would not otherwise undergo  brace  ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pansion  to  a lexically ordered list of all the characters.  See the sec‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion `Brace Expansion'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       CASE_GLOB <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Make globbing (filename generation) sensitive to case.   Note  that  other[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              uses  of  patterns  are always sensitive to case.  If the option is unset,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the presence of any character which is special to filename generation will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cause case-insensitive matching.  For example, cvs(/) can match the direc‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tory CVS owing to the presence of the globbing  flag  (unless  the  option[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              BARE_GLOB_QUAL is unset).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       CASE_MATCH <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Make  regular  expressions  using  the zsh/regex module (including matches[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              with =~) sensitive to case.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       CASE_PATHS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If CASE_PATHS is not set (the default), CASE_GLOB affects the  interpreta‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion  of every path component, whenever a special character appears in any[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              component.  When CASE_PATHS is set, file path components that do not  con‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tain  special filename generation characters are always sensitive to case,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              thus restricting NO_CASE_GLOB to components that contain globbing  charac‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ters.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note  that  if  the  filesystem  itself  is  not  sensitive  to case, then[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              CASE_PATHS has no effect.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       CSH_NULL_GLOB <C>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If a pattern for filename generation has no matches,  delete  the  pattern[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              from  the argument list; do not report an error unless all the patterns in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              a command have no matches.  Overrides NOMATCH.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       EQUALS <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Perform = filename expansion.  (See the section `Filename Expansion'.)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       EXTENDED_GLOB[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Treat the `#', `~' and `^' characters as part  of  patterns  for  filename[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              generation, etc.  (An initial unquoted `~' always produces named directory[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              expansion.)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       FORCE_FLOAT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Constants  in arithmetic evaluation will be treated as floating point even[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              without the use of a decimal point; the values of integer  variables  will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              be converted to floating point when used in arithmetic expressions.  Inte‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              gers in any base will be converted.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       GLOB (+F, ksh: +f) <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Perform filename generation (globbing).  (See the section `Filename Gener‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ation'.)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       GLOB_ASSIGN <C>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  this option is set, filename generation (globbing) is performed on the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              right hand side of scalar parameter assignments of the form  `name=pattern[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (e.g.  `foo=*').   If the result has more than one word the parameter will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              become an array with those words as arguments. This option is provided for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              backwards compatibility only: globbing is always performed  on  the  right[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              hand side of array assignments of the form `name=(value)' (e.g. `foo=(*)')[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and  this form is recommended for clarity; with this option set, it is not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              possible to predict whether the result will be an array or a scalar.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       GLOB_DOTS (-4)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Do not require a leading `.' in a filename to be matched explicitly.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       GLOB_STAR_SHORT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When this option is set and the default zsh-style globbing is  in  effect,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  pattern `**/*' can be abbreviated to `**' and the pattern `***/*' can[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              be abbreviated to ***.  Hence `**.c' finds a file ending in .c in any sub‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              directory, and `***.c' does the same while also following symbolic  links.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              A  /  immediately after the `**' or `***' forces the pattern to be treated[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              as the unabbreviated form.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       GLOB_SUBST <C> <K> <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Treat any characters resulting from parameter expansion as being  eligible[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for filename expansion and filename generation, and any characters result‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ing  from  command substitution as being eligible for filename generation.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Braces (and commas in between) do not become eligible for expansion.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HIST_SUBST_PATTERN[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Substitutions using the :s and :& history  modifiers  are  performed  with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pattern matching instead of string matching.  This occurs wherever history[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              modifiers  are  valid,  including glob qualifiers and parameters.  See the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              section `Modifiers' in zshexpn(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       IGNORE_BRACES (-I) <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Do not perform brace expansion.  For historical reasons this also includes[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the effect of the IGNORE_CLOSE_BRACES option.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       IGNORE_CLOSE_BRACES[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When neither this option nor IGNORE_BRACES is  set,  a  sole  close  brace[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              character `}' is syntactically significant at any point on a command line.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  has  the effect that no semicolon or newline is necessary before the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              brace terminating a function or current shell construct.  When either  op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion  is set, a closing brace is syntactically significant only in command[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              position.  Unlike IGNORE_BRACES, this option does not disable brace expan‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sion.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              For example, with both options unset a function may be defined in the fol‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              lowing fashion:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     args() { echo $# }[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              while if either option is set, this does not work and something equivalent[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to the following is required:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     args() { echo $#; }[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       KSH_GLOB <K>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              In pattern matching, the interpretation of parentheses is  affected  by  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              preceding  `@',  `*',  `+', `?' or `!'.  See the section `Filename Genera‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       MAGIC_EQUAL_SUBST[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              All unquoted arguments of the form `anything=expression'  appearing  after[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  command name have filename expansion (that is, where expression has a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              leading `~' or `=') performed on expression as if it were a parameter  as‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              signment.   The  argument is not otherwise treated specially; it is passed[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to the command as a single argument, and not used as an  actual  parameter[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              assignment.   For  example, in echo foo=~/bar:~/rod, both occurrences of ~[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              would be replaced.  Note that this happens anyway with typeset and similar[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              statements.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              This option respects the setting of  the  KSH_TYPESET  option.   In  other[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              words,  if  both options are in effect, arguments looking like assignments[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              will not undergo word splitting.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       MARK_DIRS (-8, ksh: -X)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Append a trailing `/' to all directory names resulting from filename  gen‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              eration (globbing).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       MULTIBYTE <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Respect  multibyte  characters when found in strings.  When this option is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              set, strings are examined using the system library to determine  how  many[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              bytes form a character, depending on the current locale.  This affects the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              way characters are counted in pattern matching, parameter values and vari‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ous delimiters.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The  option is on by default if the shell was compiled with MULTIBYTE_SUP‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              PORT; otherwise it is off by default and has no effect if turned on.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If the option is off a single byte is always treated as a  single  charac‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ter.   This setting is designed purely for examining strings known to con‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tain raw bytes or other values that may not be characters in  the  current[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              locale.   It is not necessary to unset the option merely because the char‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              acter set for the current locale does not contain multibyte characters.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The option does not affect the shell's editor,  which always uses the  lo‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cale to determine multibyte characters.  This is because the character set[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              displayed by the terminal emulator is independent of shell settings.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       NOMATCH (+3) <C> <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  a  pattern for filename generation has no matches, print an error, in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              stead of leaving it unchanged in the argument list.  This also applies  to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              file expansion of an initial `~' or `='.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       NULL_GLOB (-G)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  a  pattern  for filename generation has no matches, delete the pattern[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              from the argument list instead of reporting an error.  Overrides NOMATCH.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       NUMERIC_GLOB_SORT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If numeric filenames are matched by a filename  generation  pattern,  sort[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the filenames numerically rather than lexicographically.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       RC_EXPAND_PARAM (-P)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Array  expansions of the form `foo${xx}bar', where the parameter xx is set[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to (a b c), are substituted with `fooabar foobbar foocbar' instead of  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              default  `fooa b cbar'.  Note that an empty array will therefore cause all[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              arguments to be removed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       REMATCH_PCRE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If set,  regular  expression  matching  with  the  =~  operator  will  use[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Perl-Compatible  Regular Expressions from the PCRE library.  (The zsh/pcre[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              module must be available.)  If not set, regular expressions will  use  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              extended regexp syntax provided by the system libraries.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       SH_GLOB <K> <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Disables the special meaning of `(', `|', `)' and '<' for globbing the re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sult  of  parameter  and  command  substitutions, and in some other places[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              where the shell accepts patterns.  If SH_GLOB is set but KSH_GLOB is  not,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  shell  allows  the interpretation of subshell expressions enclosed in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              parentheses in some cases where there  is  no  space  before  the  opening[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              parenthesis,  e.g.  !(true)  is interpreted as if there were a space after[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the !.  This option is set by default if zsh is invoked as sh or ksh.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       UNSET (+u, ksh: +u) <K> <S> <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Treat unset parameters as if they were empty when substituting, and as  if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              they  were  zero  when  reading  their  values in arithmetic expansion and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              arithmetic commands.  Otherwise they are treated as an error.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       WARN_CREATE_GLOBAL[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Print a warning message when a global parameter is created in  a  function[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              by  an assignment or in math context.  This often indicates that a parame‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ter has not been declared local when it should have been.  Parameters  ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              plicitly  declared  global  from within a function using typeset -g do not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cause a warning.  Note that there is no warning when a local parameter  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              assigned to in a nested function, which may also indicate an error.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       WARN_NESTED_VAR[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Print a warning message when an existing parameter from an enclosing func‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion  scope,  or  global, is set in a function by an assignment or in math[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              context.  Assignment to shell special parameters does not cause a warning.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This is the companion to WARN_CREATE_GLOBAL as in this case the warning is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              only printed when a parameter is not  created.   Where  possible,  use  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              typeset  -g  to set the parameter suppresses the error, but note that this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              needs to be used every time the parameter is set.  To restrict the  effect[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of this option to a single function scope, use `functions -W'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              For  example, the following code produces a warning for the assignment in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              side the function nested as that overrides the value within toplevel[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     toplevel() {[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                       local foo="in fn"[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                       nested[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     }[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     nested() {[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                          foo="in nested"[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     }[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     setopt warn_nested_var[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     toplevel[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   History[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       APPEND_HISTORY <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If this is set, zsh sessions will append their history list to the history[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              file, rather than replace it. Thus, multiple parallel  zsh  sessions  will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              all  have  the  new  entries from their history lists added to the history[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              file, in the order that they exit.  The file will  still  be  periodically[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              re-written  to trim it when the number of lines grows 20% beyond the value[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              specified by $SAVEHIST (see also the HIST_SAVE_BY_COPY option).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       BANG_HIST (+K) <C> <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Perform textual history expansion, csh-style, treating the  character  `!'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              specially.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       EXTENDED_HISTORY <C>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Save  each  command's beginning timestamp (in seconds since the epoch) and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the duration (in seconds) to the history file.  The format  of  this  pre‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fixed data is:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              `: <beginning time>:<elapsed seconds>;<command>'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HIST_ALLOW_CLOBBER[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Add `|' to output redirections in the history.  This allows history refer‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ences to clobber files even when CLOBBER is unset.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HIST_BEEP <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Beep  in  ZLE when a widget attempts to access a history entry which isn't[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              there.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HIST_EXPIRE_DUPS_FIRST[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If the internal history needs to be trimmed to  add  the  current  command[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              line,  setting  this option will cause the oldest history event that has a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              duplicate to be lost before losing a unique  event  from  the  list.   You[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              should  be sure to set the value of HISTSIZE to a larger number than SAVE‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              HIST in order to give you some room for the duplicated  events,  otherwise[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              this  option  will  behave just like HIST_IGNORE_ALL_DUPS once the history[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fills up with unique events.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HIST_FCNTL_LOCK[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When writing out the history file, by default zsh uses ad-hoc file locking[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to avoid known problems with locking on some operating systems.  With this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              option locking is done by means of the system's  fcntl  call,  where  this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              method  is available.  On recent operating systems this may provide better[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              performance, in particular avoiding  history  corruption  when  files  are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              stored on NFS.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HIST_FIND_NO_DUPS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When  searching for history entries in the line editor, do not display du‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              plicates of a line previously found, even if the duplicates are  not  con‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tiguous.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HIST_IGNORE_ALL_DUPS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  a new command line being added to the history list duplicates an older[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              one, the older command is removed from the list (even if  it  is  not  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              previous event).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HIST_IGNORE_DUPS (-h)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Do not enter command lines into the history list if they are duplicates of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the previous event.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HIST_IGNORE_SPACE (-g)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Remove command lines from the history list when the first character on the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              line  is  a  space, or when one of the expanded aliases contains a leading[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              space.  Only normal aliases (not global or suffix aliases) have  this  be‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              haviour.   Note that the command lingers in the internal history until the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              next command is entered before it vanishes, allowing you to briefly  reuse[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              or edit the line.  If you want to make it vanish right away without enter‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ing another command, type a space and press return.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HIST_LEX_WORDS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              By  default,  shell history that is read in from files is split into words[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              on all white space.  This means that arguments with quoted whitespace  are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              not  correctly  handled,  with the consequence that references to words in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              history lines that have been read from a file  may  be  inaccurate.   When[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              this  option is set, words read in from a history file are divided up in a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              similar fashion to normal shell command line handling.  Although this pro‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              duces more accurately delimited words, if the size of the history file  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              large this can be slow.  Trial and error is necessary to decide.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HIST_NO_FUNCTIONS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Remove function definitions from the history list.  Note that the function[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              lingers  in  the internal history until the next command is entered before[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              it vanishes, allowing you to briefly reuse or edit the definition.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HIST_NO_STORE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Remove the history (fc -l) command from the  history  list  when  invoked.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Note  that the command lingers in the internal history until the next com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mand is entered before it vanishes, allowing you to briefly reuse or  edit[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the line.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HIST_REDUCE_BLANKS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Remove  superfluous  blanks from each command line being added to the his‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tory list.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HIST_SAVE_BY_COPY <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When the history file is re-written, we normally write out a copy  of  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              file named $HISTFILE.new and then rename it over the old one.  However, if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              this  option  is unset, we instead truncate the old history file and write[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              out the new version in-place.  If one of the history-appending options  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              enabled,  this  option  only  has an effect when the enlarged history file[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              needs to be re-written to trim it down to size.  Disable this only if  you[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              have  special needs, as doing so makes it possible to lose history entries[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              if zsh gets interrupted during the save.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              When writing out a copy of the history file, zsh preserves the old  file's[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              permissions and group information, but will refuse to write out a new file[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              if it would change the history file's owner.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HIST_SAVE_NO_DUPS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When  writing  out  the  history file, older commands that duplicate newer[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ones are omitted.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HIST_VERIFY[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Whenever the user enters a line with history expansion, don't execute  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              line directly; instead, perform history expansion and reload the line into[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the editing buffer.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       INC_APPEND_HISTORY[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  option  works  like APPEND_HISTORY except that new history lines are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              added to the $HISTFILE incrementally (as soon as they are entered), rather[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              than waiting until the shell exits.  The file will still  be  periodically[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              re-written  to trim it when the number of lines grows 20% beyond the value[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              specified by $SAVEHIST (see also the HIST_SAVE_BY_COPY option).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       INC_APPEND_HISTORY_TIME[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This option is a variant of INC_APPEND_HISTORY in which,  where  possible,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  history  entry  is  written out to the file after the command is fin‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ished, so that the time taken by the command is recorded correctly in  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              history  file in EXTENDED_HISTORY format.  This means that the history en‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              try will not be available immediately from other instances  of  the  shell[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that are using the same history file.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              This  option  is  only  useful if INC_APPEND_HISTORY and SHARE_HISTORY are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              turned off.  The three options should be considered mutually exclusive.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       SHARE_HISTORY <K>[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              This option both imports new commands from  the  history  file,  and  also[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              causes  your typed commands to be appended to the history file (the latter[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is like specifying INC_APPEND_HISTORY, which should be turned off if  this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              option  is  in effect).  The history lines are also output with timestamps[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ala EXTENDED_HISTORY (which makes it easier to find the spot where we left[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              off reading the file after it gets re-written).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              By default, history movement commands visit the imported lines as well  as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  local  lines,  but  you  can  toggle this on and off with the set-lo‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cal-history zle binding.  It is also possible to create a zle widget  that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              will make some commands ignore imported commands, and some include them.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  you  find  that you want more control over when commands get imported,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              you may wish to turn  SHARE_HISTORY  off,  INC_APPEND_HISTORY  or  INC_AP‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              PEND_HISTORY_TIME  (see above) on, and then manually import commands when‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ever you need them using `fc -RI'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Initialisation[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ALL_EXPORT (-a, ksh: -a)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              All parameters subsequently defined are automatically exported.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       GLOBAL_EXPORT <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If this option is set, passing the -x flag to the builtins declare, float,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              integer, readonly and typeset (but not local) will also set the  -g  flag;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              hence parameters exported to the environment will not be made local to the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              enclosing  function,  unless they were already or the flag +g is given ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              plicitly.  If the option is unset, exported parameters will be made  local[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              in just the same way as any other parameter.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              This option is set by default for backward compatibility; it is not recom‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mended  that  its  behaviour be relied upon.  Note that the builtin export[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              always sets both the -x and -g flags, and hence its effect extends  beyond[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  scope  of  the  enclosing  function; this is the most portable way to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              achieve this behaviour.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       GLOBAL_RCS (+d) <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If this option is unset,  the  startup  files  /etc/zprofile,  /etc/zshrc,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              /etc/zlogin  and  /etc/zlogout  will  not  be run.  It can be disabled and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              re-enabled at any time, including  inside  local  startup  files  (.zshrc,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              etc.).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       RCS (+f) <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              After  /etc/zshenv  is  sourced on startup, source the .zshenv, /etc/zpro‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              file, .zprofile, /etc/zshrc, .zshrc, /etc/zlogin,  .zlogin,  and  .zlogout[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              files,  as described in the section `Files'.  If this option is unset, the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              /etc/zshenv file is still sourced, but any of the others will not  be;  it[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              can  be  set  at any time to prevent the remaining startup files after the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              currently executing one from being sourced.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Input/Output[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ALIASES <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Expand aliases.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       CLOBBER (+C, ksh: +C) <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Allows `>' redirection to truncate existing files.  Otherwise `>!' or `>|'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              must be used to truncate a file.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If the option is not set, and the option APPEND_CREATE is  also  not  set,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `>>!'  or  `>>|'  must be used to create a file.  If either option is set,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `>>' may be used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       CLOBBER_EMPTY[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This option is only used if the option CLOBBER is not set: note that it is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              set by default.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If this option is set, then regular files of zero length may be ovewritten[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (`clobbered').  Note that it is possible another process  has  written  to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  file  between  this  test and use of the file by the current process.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This option should therefore not be used in cases where files to be  clob‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              bered may be written to asynchronously.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       CORRECT (-0)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Try   to   correct   the  spelling  of  commands.   Note  that,  when  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              HASH_LIST_ALL option is not set or when some directories in the  path  are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              not  readable, this may falsely report spelling errors the first time some[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              commands are used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The shell variable CORRECT_IGNORE may be set to a pattern to  match  words[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that will never be offered as corrections.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       CORRECT_ALL (-O)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Try to correct the spelling of all arguments in a line.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The  shell  variable  CORRECT_IGNORE_FILE may be set to a pattern to match[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              file names that will never be offered as corrections.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       DVORAK Use the Dvorak keyboard instead of the standard qwerty keyboard as a basis[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for examining spelling mistakes for the CORRECT  and  CORRECT_ALL  options[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and the spell-word editor command.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       FLOW_CONTROL <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  this  option  is  unset, output flow control via start/stop characters[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (usually assigned to ^S/^Q) is disabled in the shell's editor.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       IGNORE_EOF (-7)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Do not exit on end-of-file.  Require the use of exit  or  logout  instead.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              However,  ten  consecutive  EOFs  will  cause the shell to exit anyway, to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              avoid the shell hanging if its tty goes away.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Also, if this option is set and the Zsh Line Editor is used,  widgets  im‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              plemented  by  shell  functions  can  be bound to EOF (normally Control-D)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              without printing the normal warning message.  This works only  for  normal[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              widgets, not for completion widgets.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       INTERACTIVE_COMMENTS (-k) <K> <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Allow comments even in interactive shells.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HASH_CMDS <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Note  the  location of each command the first time it is executed.  Subse‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              quent invocations of the same command will use the saved location,  avoid‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ing  a  path  search.  If this option is unset, no path hashing is done at[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              all.  However, when CORRECT is set, commands whose names do not appear  in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  functions or aliases hash tables are hashed in order to avoid report‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ing them as spelling errors.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HASH_DIRS <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Whenever a command name is hashed, hash the directory  containing  it,  as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              well  as all directories that occur earlier in the path.  Has no effect if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              neither HASH_CMDS nor CORRECT is set.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HASH_EXECUTABLES_ONLY[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When hashing commands because of HASH_CMDS, check  that  the  file  to  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              hashed  is  actually an executable.  This option is unset by default as if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the path contains a large number of commands, or consists of  many  remote[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              files,  the  additional  tests  can  take a long time.  Trial and error is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              needed to show if this option is beneficial.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       MAIL_WARNING (-U)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Print a warning message if a mail file has been accessed since  the  shell[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              last checked.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       PATH_DIRS (-Q)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Perform a path search even on command names with slashes in them.  Thus if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `/usr/local/bin'  is  in the user's path, and he or she types `X11/xinit',[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the command `/usr/local/bin/X11/xinit' will be executed (assuming  it  ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ists).  Commands explicitly beginning with `/', `./' or `../' are not sub‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ject  to  the  path  search.   This  also  applies  to  the `.' and source[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              builtins.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that subdirectories of the current directory are always searched  for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              executables  specified  in  this form.  This takes place before any search[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              indicated by this option, and regardless of whether `.' or the current di‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rectory appear in the command search path.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       PATH_SCRIPT <K> <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If this option is not set, a script passed as the first  non-option  argu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ment  to the shell must contain the name of the file to open.  If this op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion is set, and the script does not specify a directory path, the  script[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is  looked  for  first in the current directory, then in the command path.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              See the section INVOCATION in zsh(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       PRINT_EIGHT_BIT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Print eight bit characters literally in completion lists, etc.   This  op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion is not necessary if your system correctly returns the printability of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              eight bit characters (see ctype(3)).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       PRINT_EXIT_VALUE (-1)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Print  the exit value of programs with non-zero exit status.  This is only[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              available at the command line in interactive shells.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       RC_QUOTES[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Allow the character sequence `''' to signify a single quote within  singly[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              quoted strings.  Note this does not apply in quoted strings using the for‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mat $'...', where a backslashed single quote can be used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       RM_STAR_SILENT (-H) <K> <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Do not query the user before executing `rm *' or `rm path/*'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       RM_STAR_WAIT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  querying  the  user before executing `rm *' or `rm path/*', first wait[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ten seconds and ignore anything typed in that time.  This avoids the prob‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              lem of reflexively answering `yes' to the query  when  one  didn't  really[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mean it.  The wait and query can always be avoided by expanding the `*' in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ZLE (with tab).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       SHORT_LOOPS <C> <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Allow the short forms of for, repeat, select, if, and function constructs.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       SHORT_REPEAT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Allow the short form repeat as SHORT_LOOPS but without enabling it for the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              other constructs.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       SUN_KEYBOARD_HACK (-L)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If a line ends with a backquote, and there are an odd number of backquotes[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              on  the  line, ignore the trailing backquote.  This is useful on some key‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              boards where the return key is too small, and the backquote key  lies  an‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              noyingly  close  to it.  As an alternative the variable KEYBOARD_HACK lets[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              you choose the character to be removed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Job Control[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       AUTO_CONTINUE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              With this option set, stopped jobs that are removed  from  the  job  table[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              with  the  disown  builtin command are automatically sent a CONT signal to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              make them running.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       AUTO_RESUME (-W)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Treat single word simple commands without redirection  as  candidates  for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              resumption of an existing job.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       BG_NICE (-6) <C> <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Run  all  background  jobs at a lower priority.  This option is set by de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fault.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       CHECK_JOBS <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Report the status of background and suspended jobs before exiting a  shell[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              with  job  control;  a  second  attempt  to  exit  the shell will succeed.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              NO_CHECK_JOBS is best used only in combination with NO_HUP, else such jobs[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              will be killed automatically.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The check is omitted if the commands run from the  previous  command  line[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              included  a  `jobs'  command,  since  it is assumed the user is aware that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              there are background or suspended jobs.  A `jobs' command run from one  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  hook functions defined in the section SPECIAL FUNCTIONS in zshmisc(1)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is not counted for this purpose.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       CHECK_RUNNING_JOBS <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Check for both running and suspended  jobs  when  CHECK_JOBS  is  enabled.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When  this  option  is disabled, zsh checks only for suspended jobs, which[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              matches the default behavior of bash.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              This option has no effect unless CHECK_JOBS is set.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       HUP <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Send the HUP signal to running jobs when the shell exits.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       LONG_LIST_JOBS (-R)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Print job notifications in the long format by default.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       MONITOR (-m, ksh: -m)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Allow job control.  Set by default in interactive shells.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       NOTIFY (-5, ksh: -b) <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Report the status of background jobs immediately, rather than waiting  un‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              til just before printing a prompt.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       POSIX_JOBS <K> <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This option makes job control more compliant with the POSIX standard.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              When  the  option is not set, the MONITOR option is unset on entry to sub‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              shells, so that job control is no longer active.  When the option is  set,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the MONITOR option and job control remain active in the subshell, but note[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that the subshell has no access to jobs in the parent shell.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              When  the option is not set, jobs put in the background or foreground with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              bg or fg are displayed with the same information that would be reported by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              jobs.  When the option is set, only the text is printed.  The output  from[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              jobs itself is not affected by the option.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              When the option is not set, job information from the parent shell is saved[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for  output  within a subshell (for example, within a pipeline).  When the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              option is set, the output of jobs is empty until a job is  started  within[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the subshell.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              In  previous  versions of the shell, it was necessary to enable POSIX_JOBS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              in order for the builtin command wait to return the status  of  background[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              jobs that had already exited.  This is no longer the case.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Prompting[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       PROMPT_BANG <K>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  set,  `!'  is treated specially in prompt expansion.  See EXPANSION OF[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              PROMPT SEQUENCES in zshmisc(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       PROMPT_CR (+V) <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Print a carriage return just before printing a prompt in the line  editor.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This is on by default as multi-line editing is only possible if the editor[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              knows where the start of the line appears.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       PROMPT_SP <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Attempt  to  preserve  a partial line (i.e. a line that did not end with a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              newline) that would otherwise be covered up by the command prompt  due  to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  PROMPT_CR option.  This works by outputting some cursor-control char‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              acters, including a series of spaces, that should make the  terminal  wrap[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to  the  next  line when a partial line is present (note that this is only[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              successful if your terminal has automatic margins, which is typical).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              When a partial line is preserved, by default you will see an  inverse+bold[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              character  at  the  end of the partial line:  a `%' for a normal user or a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `#' for root.  If set, the shell parameter PROMPT_EOL_MARK can be used  to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              customize how the end of partial lines are shown.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              NOTE:  if  the PROMPT_CR option is not set, enabling this option will have[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              no effect.  This option is on by default.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       PROMPT_PERCENT <C> <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If set, `%' is treated specially in prompt expansion.   See  EXPANSION  OF[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              PROMPT SEQUENCES in zshmisc(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       PROMPT_SUBST <K> <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If set, parameter expansion, command substitution and arithmetic expansion[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              are  performed in prompts.  Substitutions within prompts do not affect the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              command status.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TRANSIENT_RPROMPT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Remove any right prompt from display when accepting a command line.   This[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              may be useful with terminals with other cut/paste methods.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Scripts and Functions[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ALIAS_FUNC_DEF <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              By default, zsh does not allow the definition of functions using the `name[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ()'  syntax  if name was expanded as an alias: this causes an error.  This[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is usually the desired behaviour, as otherwise the combination of an alias[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and a function based on the same definition can easily cause problems.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              When this option is set, aliases can be used for defining functions.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              For example, consider the following definitions as they might occur  in  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              startup file.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     alias foo=bar[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     foo() {[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                       print This probably does not do what you expect.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     }[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Here,  foo is expanded as an alias to bar before the () is encountered, so[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the function defined would be named bar.  By default this  is  instead  an[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              error in native mode.  Note that quoting any part of the function name, or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              using the keyword function, avoids the problem, so is recommended when the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              function name can also be an alias.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       C_BASES[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Output  hexadecimal  numbers  in the standard C format, for example `0xFF'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              instead of the usual `16#FF'.  If the option OCTAL_ZEROES is also set  (it[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is  not by default), octal numbers will be treated similarly and hence ap‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pear as `077' instead of `8#77'.  This option has no effect on the  choice[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of  the output base, nor on the output of bases other than hexadecimal and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              octal.  Note that these formats will be understood on  input  irrespective[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of the setting of C_BASES.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       C_PRECEDENCES[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  alters  the precedence of arithmetic operators to be more like C and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              other programming languages; the section  ARITHMETIC  EVALUATION  in  zsh‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              misc(1) has an explicit list.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       DEBUG_BEFORE_CMD <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Run  the  DEBUG  trap  before each command; otherwise it is run after each[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              command.  Setting this option mimics the behaviour of ksh 93; with the op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion unset the behaviour is that of ksh 88.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ERR_EXIT (-e, ksh: -e)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If a command has a non-zero exit status, execute the ZERR  trap,  if  set,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and exit.  This is disabled while running initialization scripts.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The  behaviour  is also disabled inside DEBUG traps.  In this case the op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion is handled specially: it is unset on entry to the trap.  If  the  op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion DEBUG_BEFORE_CMD is set, as it is by default, and the option ERR_EXIT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is  found  to  have been set on exit, then the command for which the DEBUG[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              trap is being executed is skipped.  The option is restored after the  trap[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              exits.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Non-zero  status in a command list containing && or || is ignored for com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mands not at the end of the list.  Hence[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     false && true[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              does not trigger exit.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Exiting due to ERR_EXIT has certain interactions  with  asynchronous  jobs[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              noted in the section JOBS in zshmisc(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ERR_RETURN[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  a  command has a non-zero exit status, return immediately from the en‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              closing function.  The logic is similar to that for ERR_EXIT, except  that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              an  implicit  return  statement is executed instead of an exit.  This will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              trigger an exit at the outermost level of a non-interactive script.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Normally this option inherits the behaviour of ERR_EXIT that code followed[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              by `&&' `||' does not trigger a return.  Hence in the following:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     summit || true[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              no return is forced as the combined effect always has a zero  return  sta‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tus.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note.  however,  that if summit in the above example is itself a function,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              code inside it is considered separately: it may force a return from summit[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (assuming the option remains set within summit), but not from the  enclos‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ing  context.   This  behaviour  is different from ERR_EXIT which is unaf‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fected by function scope.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       EVAL_LINENO <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If set, line numbers of expressions evaluated using the builtin  eval  are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tracked separately of the enclosing environment.  This applies both to the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              parameter  LINENO  and the line number output by the prompt escape %i.  If[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the option is set, the prompt escape %N will output  the  string  `(eval)'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              instead of the script or function name as an indication.   (The two prompt[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              escapes  are typically used in the parameter PS4 to be output when the op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion XTRACE is set.)  If EVAL_LINENO is unset, the line number of the sur‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rounding script or function is retained during the evaluation.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       EXEC (+n, ksh: +n) <D>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Do execute commands.  Without this option, commands are read  and  checked[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for  syntax errors, but not executed.  This option cannot be turned off in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              an interactive shell, except  when  `-n'  is  supplied  to  the  shell  at[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              startup.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       FUNCTION_ARGZERO <C> <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When  executing  a shell function or sourcing a script, set $0 temporarily[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to the name of the function/script.  Note that  toggling  FUNCTION_ARGZERO[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              from  on  to  off  (or off to on) does not change the current value of $0.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Only the state upon entry to the function or script has an  effect.   Com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pare POSIX_ARGZERO.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       LOCAL_LOOPS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When this option is not set, the effect of break and continue commands may[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              propagate  outside  function  scope, affecting loops in calling functions.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When the option is set in a calling function, a break or a  continue  that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is  not  caught within a called function (regardless of the setting of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              option within that function) produces a warning and  the  effect  is  can‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              celled.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       LOCAL_OPTIONS <K>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  this  option is set at the point of return from a shell function, most[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              options (including this one) which were in force upon entry to  the  func‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion  are  restored;  options that are not restored are PRIVILEGED and RE‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              STRICTED.  Otherwise, only this option, and the  LOCAL_LOOPS,  XTRACE  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              PRINT_EXIT_VALUE  options are restored.  Hence if this is explicitly unset[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              by a shell function the other options in force at the point of return will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              remain so.  A shell function can also guarantee itself a known shell  con‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              figuration  with a formulation like `emulate -L zsh'; the -L activates LO‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              CAL_OPTIONS.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       LOCAL_PATTERNS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If this option is set at the point of return from a  shell  function,  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              state  of  pattern disables, as set with the builtin command `disable -p',[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is restored to what it was when the function was entered.   The  behaviour[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of this option is similar to the effect of LOCAL_OPTIONS on options; hence[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `emulate  -L  sh' (or indeed any other emulation with the -L option) acti‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              vates LOCAL_PATTERNS.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       LOCAL_TRAPS <K>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If this option is set when a signal trap is set inside  a  function,  then[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  previous status of the trap for that signal will be restored when the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              function exits.  Note that this option must be set prior to  altering  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              trap behaviour in a function; unlike LOCAL_OPTIONS, the value on exit from[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  function  is  irrelevant.  However, it does not need to be set before[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              any global trap for that to be correctly restored by a function.  For  ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ample,[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     unsetopt localtraps[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     trap - INT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     fn() { setopt localtraps; trap '' INT; sleep 3; }[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              will restore normal handling of SIGINT after the function exits.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       MULTI_FUNC_DEF <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Allow  definitions  of  multiple  functions  at  once  in  the  form  `fn1[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fn2...()'; if the option is not set, this causes a parse  error.   Defini‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion  of  multiple  functions with the function keyword is always allowed.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Multiple function definitions are not often used and can cause obscure er‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rors.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       MULTIOS <Z>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Perform implicit tees or cats when  multiple  redirections  are  attempted[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (see the section `Redirection').[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       OCTAL_ZEROES <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Interpret  any  integer constant beginning with a 0 as octal, per IEEE Std[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              1003.2-1992 (ISO 9945-2:1993).  This is  not  enabled  by  default  as  it[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              causes  problems  with parsing of, for example, date and time strings with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              leading zeroes.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Sequences of digits indicating a numeric base such as the  `08'  component[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              in  `08#77'  are  always interpreted as decimal, regardless of leading ze‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              roes.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       PIPE_FAIL[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              By default, when a pipeline exits the exit status recorded  by  the  shell[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and  returned by the shell variable $? reflects that of the rightmost ele‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ment of a pipeline.  If this option is set, the exit  status  instead  re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              flects  the  status  of  the  rightmost  element  of the pipeline that was[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              non-zero, or zero if all elements exited with zero status.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       SOURCE_TRACE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If set, zsh will print an informational message  announcing  the  name  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              each  file  it loads.  The format of the output is similar to that for the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              XTRACE option, with the message <sourcetrace>.  A file may  be  loaded  by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the shell itself when it starts up and shuts down (Startup/Shutdown Files)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              or by the use of the `source' and `dot' builtin commands.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TYPESET_SILENT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  this  is unset, executing any of the `typeset' family of commands with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              no options and a list of parameters that have no values to be assigned but[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              already exist will display the value of the parameter.  If the  option  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              set,  they  will  only be shown when parameters are selected with the `-m'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              option.  The option `-p' is available whether or not the option is set.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TYPESET_TO_UNSET <K> <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When declaring a new parameter with any of the `typeset' family of related[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              commands, the parameter remains unset unless and until a value is  explic‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ity  assigned  to it, either in the `typeset' command itself or as a later[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              assignment statement.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       VERBOSE (-v, ksh: -v)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Print shell input lines as they are read.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       XTRACE (-x, ksh: -x)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Print commands and their arguments as they are executed.   The  output  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              preceded  by  the value of $PS4, formatted as described in the section EX‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              PANSION OF PROMPT SEQUENCES in zshmisc(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Shell Emulation[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       APPEND_CREATE <K> <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This option only applies when NO_CLOBBER (-C) is in effect.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If this option is not set, the shell will report an error  when  a  append[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              redirection  (>>) is used on a file that does not already exists (the tra‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ditional zsh behaviour of NO_CLOBBER).  If the option is set, no error  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              reported (POSIX behaviour).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       BASH_REMATCH[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When set, matches performed with the =~ operator will set the BASH_REMATCH[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              array  variable,  instead  of  the default MATCH and match variables.  The[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              first element of the BASH_REMATCH array will contain  the  entire  matched[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              text  and subsequent elements will contain extracted substrings.  This op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion makes more sense when KSH_ARRAYS is also  set,  so  that  the  entire[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              matched  portion  is stored at index 0 and the first substring is at index[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              1.  Without this option, the MATCH variable contains  the  entire  matched[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              text and the match array variable contains substrings.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       BSD_ECHO <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Make  the echo builtin compatible with the BSD echo(1) command.  This dis‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ables backslashed escape sequences in echo strings unless the -e option is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              specified.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       CONTINUE_ON_ERROR[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If a fatal error is encountered (see the section  ERRORS  in  zshmisc(1)),[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and  the  code  is running in a script, the shell will resume execution at[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the next statement in the script at the top level, in other words  outside[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              all functions or shell constructs such as loops and conditions.  This mim‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ics  the  behaviour  of interactive shells, where the shell returns to the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              line editor to read a new command; it was the normal behaviour in versions[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of zsh before 5.0.1.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       CSH_JUNKIE_HISTORY <C>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              A history reference without an event specifier will always  refer  to  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              previous command.  Without this option, such a history reference refers to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  same  event  as the previous history reference on the current command[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              line, defaulting to the previous command.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       CSH_JUNKIE_LOOPS <C>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Allow loop bodies to take the form `list; end' instead of `do list; done'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       CSH_JUNKIE_QUOTES <C>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Changes the rules for single- and double-quoted text to match that of csh.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              These require that embedded newlines be preceded by a backslash; unescaped[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              newlines will cause an error message.  In  double-quoted  strings,  it  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              made  impossible to escape `$', ``' or `"' (and `\' itself no longer needs[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              escaping).  Command substitutions are only expanded once,  and  cannot  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              nested.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       CSH_NULLCMD <C>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Do not use the values of NULLCMD and READNULLCMD when running redirections[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              with no command.  This make such redirections fail (see the section `Redi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rection').[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       KSH_ARRAYS <K> <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Emulate ksh array handling as closely as possible.  If this option is set,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              array  elements  are  numbered  from zero, an array parameter without sub‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              script refers to the first element instead of the whole array, and  braces[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              are  required  to  delimit  a  subscript  (`${path[2]}'  rather  than just[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `$path[2]') or to apply modifiers to any parameter (`${PWD:h}' rather than[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `$PWD:h').[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       KSH_AUTOLOAD <K> <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Emulate ksh function autoloading.  This means that when a function is  au‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              toloaded,  the  corresponding file is merely executed, and must define the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              function itself.  (By default, the function is defined to the contents  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  file.  However, the most common ksh-style case - of the file contain‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ing only a simple definition of the function - is always  handled  in  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ksh-compatible manner.)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       KSH_OPTION_PRINT <K>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Alters  the way options settings are printed: instead of separate lists of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              set and unset options, all options are shown, marked `on' if they  are  in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the non-default state, `off' otherwise.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       KSH_TYPESET[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  option  is now obsolete: a better appropximation to the behaviour of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              other shells is obtained with the reserved word interface to declare,  ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              port,  float,  integer, local, readonly and typeset.  Note that the option[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is only applied when the reserved word interface is not in use.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Alters the way arguments to the typeset family of commands, including  de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              clare, export, float, integer, local and readonly, are processed.  Without[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              this  option, zsh will perform normal word splitting after command and pa‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rameter expansion in arguments of an assignment; with it,  word  splitting[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              does not take place in those cases.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       KSH_ZERO_SUBSCRIPT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Treat use of a subscript of value zero in array or string expressions as a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              reference to the first element, i.e. the element that usually has the sub‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              script 1.  Ignored if KSH_ARRAYS is also set.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If neither this option nor KSH_ARRAYS is set, accesses to an element of an[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              array  or  string  with  subscript zero return an empty element or string,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              while attempts to set element zero of an array or string are treated as an[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              error.  However, attempts to set an otherwise valid subscript  range  that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              includes  zero  will  succeed.   For example, if KSH_ZERO_SUBSCRIPT is not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              set,[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     array[0]=(element)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              is an error, while[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     array[0,1]=(element)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              is not and will replace the first element of the array.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              This option is for compatibility with older versions of the shell  and  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              not recommended in new code.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       POSIX_ALIASES <K> <S>[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When  this  option is set, reserved words are not candidates for alias ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pansion:  it is still possible to declare any of them as an alias, but the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              alias will never be expanded.  Reserved words are described in the section[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              RESERVED WORDS in zshmisc(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Alias expansion takes place while text is being read; hence when this  op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion is set it does not take effect until the end of any function or other[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              piece  of  shell code parsed as one unit.  Note this may cause differences[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              from other shells even when the option is in effect.   For  example,  when[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              running a command with `zsh -c', or even `zsh -o posixaliases -c', the en‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tire command argument is parsed as one unit, so aliases defined within the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              argument are not available even in later lines.  If in doubt, avoid use of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              aliases in non-interactive code.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       POSIX_ARGZERO[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  option  may  be  used  to  temporarily  disable FUNCTION_ARGZERO and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              thereby restore the value of $0 to the name used to invoke the  shell  (or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              as  set  by  the -c command line option).  For compatibility with previous[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              versions of the  shell,  emulations  use  NO_FUNCTION_ARGZERO  instead  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              POSIX_ARGZERO,  which may result in unexpected scoping of $0 if the emula‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion mode is changed inside a function or script.  To avoid this,  explic‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              itly enable POSIX_ARGZERO in the emulate command:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     emulate sh -o POSIX_ARGZERO[0m
