[38;2;248;248;242m                       (( $1 * $1 * $1 ))[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                       return 0[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     }[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     functions -M cube 1 1 zmath_cube[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     print $(( cube(3) ))[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              This will print `0' because of the return.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Commenting the return out would lead to a different problem:  the  ((...))[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              statement  would  become the last statement in the function, so the return[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              status ($?) of the function would be non-zero (indicating  failure)  when‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ever the arithmetic result of the function would happen to be zero (numer‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ically):[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     # WRONG[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     zmath_cube() {[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                       (( $1 * $1 * $1 ))[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     }[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     functions -M cube 1 1 zmath_cube[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     print $(( cube(0) ))[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Instead, the true builtin can be used:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     # RIGHT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     zmath_cube() {[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                       (( $1 * $1 * $1 ))[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                       true[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     }[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     functions -M cube 1 1 zmath_cube[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     print $(( cube(3) ))[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  the additional option -s is given to functions -M, the argument to the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              function is a single string: anything between  the  opening  and  matching[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              closing  parenthesis  is passed to the function as a single argument, even[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              if it includes commas or white space.  The minimum  and  maximum  argument[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              specifiers must therefore be 1 if given.  An empty argument list is passed[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              as a zero-length string.  Thus, the following string function takes a sin‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              gle argument, including the commas, and prints 11:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     stringfn() { (( $#1 )); true }[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     functions -Ms stringfn[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     print $(( stringfn(foo,bar,rod) ))[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              functions  -M  with  no arguments lists all such user-defined functions in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the same form as a definition.  With the additional option -m and  a  list[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of  arguments, all functions whose mathfn matches one of the pattern argu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ments are listed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              function +M removes the list of mathematical  functions;  with  the  addi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tional  option  -m the arguments are treated as patterns and all functions[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              whose mathfn matches the pattern are removed.  Note that the  shell  func‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion  implementing the behaviour is not removed (regardless of whether its[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              name coincides with mathfn).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       getcap See the section `The zsh/cap Module' in zshmodules(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       getln [ -AclneE ] name ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Read the top value from the buffer stack and put it in the shell parameter[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              name.  Equivalent to read -zr.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       getopts optstring name [ arg ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Checks the args for legal options.  If the args are omitted, use the posi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tional parameters.  A valid option argument begins with a `+'  or  a  `-'.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              An  argument not beginning with a `+' or a `-', or the argument `--', ends[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the options.  Note that a single `-' is not considered a valid option  ar‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              gument.   optstring  contains  the  letters that getopts recognizes.  If a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              letter is followed by a `:', that option requires an  argument.   The  op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tions can be separated from the argument by blanks.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Each  time it is invoked, getopts places the option letter it finds in the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              shell parameter name, prepended with a `+' when arg  begins  with  a  `+'.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The  index  of  the next arg is stored in OPTIND.  The option argument, if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              any, is stored in OPTARG.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The first option to be examined may be changed by explicitly assigning  to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              OPTIND.   OPTIND  has an initial value of 1, and is normally set to 1 upon[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              entry to a shell function and restored upon exit.  (The POSIX_BUILTINS op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion disables this, and also changes the way the value  is  calculated  to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              match  other  shells.)  OPTARG is not reset and retains its value from the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              most recent call to getopts.  If either of OPTIND or OPTARG is  explicitly[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              unset,  it  remains unset, and the index or option argument is not stored.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The option itself is still stored in name in this case.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              A leading `:' in optstring causes getopts to store the letter of  any  in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              valid  option  in OPTARG, and to set name to `?' for an unknown option and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to `:' when a required argument is missing.  Otherwise, getopts sets  name[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to  `?'  and  prints an error message when an option is invalid.  The exit[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              status is nonzero when there are no more options.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       hash [ -Ldfmrv ] [ name[=value] ] ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              hash can be used to directly modify the contents of the command  hash  ta‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ble,  and the named directory hash table.  Normally one would modify these[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tables by modifying one's PATH (for the command hash table) or by creating[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              appropriate shell parameters (for the named directory  hash  table).   The[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              choice  of  hash  table to work on is determined by the -d option; without[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the option the command hash table is used, and with the option  the  named[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              directory hash table is used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              A  command name starting with a / is never hashed, whether by explicit use[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of the hash command or otherwise.  Such a command is always found  by  di‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rect look up in the file system.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Given  no  arguments,  and neither the -r or -f options, the selected hash[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              table will be listed in full.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The -r option causes the selected hash table to be emptied.   It  will  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              subsequently  rebuilt in the normal fashion.  The -f option causes the se‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              lected hash table to be fully rebuilt immediately.  For the  command  hash[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              table  this  hashes  all the absolute directories in the PATH, and for the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              named directory hash table this adds all users' home  directories.   These[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              two options cannot be used with any arguments.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The  -m  option causes the arguments to be taken as patterns (which should[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              be quoted) and the elements of the hash table matching those patterns  are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              printed.   This is the only way to display a limited selection of hash ta‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ble elements.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              For each name with a corresponding value, put `name' in the selected  hash[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              table,  associating it with the pathname `value'.  In the command hash ta‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ble, this means that whenever `name' is used as a  command  argument,  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              shell  will try to execute the file given by `value'.  In the named direc‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tory hash table, this means that `value' may be referred to as `~name'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              For each name with no corresponding value, attempt to add name to the hash[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              table, checking what the appropriate value is in  the  normal  manner  for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that  hash  table.   If an appropriate value can't be found, then the hash[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              table will be unchanged.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The -v option causes hash table entries to be listed as they are added  by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              explicit specification.  If has no effect if used with -f.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  the  -L  flag is present, then each hash table entry is printed in the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              form of a call to hash.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       history[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Same as fc -l.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       integer [ {+|-}Hghlprtux ] [ {+|-}LRZi [ n ] ] [ name[=value] ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Equivalent to typeset -i, except that options irrelevant to  integers  are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              not permitted.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       jobs [ -dlprs ] [ job ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       jobs -Z string[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Lists  information  about  each  given job, or all jobs if job is omitted.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The -l flag lists process IDs, and the -p flag lists process  groups.   If[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  -r  flag  is specified only running jobs will be listed and if the -s[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              flag is given only stopped jobs are shown.  If the -d flag is  given,  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              directory from which the job was started (which may not be the current di‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rectory of the job) will also be shown.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The -Z option replaces the shell's argument and environment space with the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              given  string, truncated if necessary to fit.  This will normally be visi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ble in ps (ps(1)) listings.  This feature is typically used by daemons, to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              indicate their state.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Full job control is only available in the top-level interactive shell, not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              in commands run in the left hand side of pipelines  or  within  the  (...)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              construct.   However,  a snapshot of the job state at that point is taken,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              so it is still possible to use the jobs builtin, or any parameter  provid‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ing  job  information.   This gives information about the state of jobs at[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the point the subshell was created.  If background processes  are  created[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              within  the  subshell,  then  instead information about those processes is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              provided.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              For example,[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     sleep 10 &    # Job in background[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     (             # Shell forks[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     jobs          # Shows information about "sleep 10 &"[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     sleep 5 &     # Process in background (no job control)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     jobs          # Shows information about "sleep 5 &"[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     )[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       kill [ -s signal_name | -n signal_number | -sig ] job ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       kill -l [ sig ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Sends either SIGTERM  or  the  specified  signal  to  the  given  jobs  or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              processes.   Signals  are given by number or by names, with or without the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `SIG' prefix.  If the signal being sent is not `KILL' or `CONT', then  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              job  will  be sent a `CONT' signal if it is stopped.  The argument job can[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              be the process ID of a job not in the job list.  In the second form,  kill[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -l,  if  sig is not specified the signal names are listed.  Otherwise, for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              each sig that is a name, the corresponding signal number is  listed.   For[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              each  sig that is a signal number or a number representing the exit status[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of a process which was terminated or stopped by a signal the name  of  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              signal is printed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              On  some  systems, alternative signal names are allowed for a few signals.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Typical examples are SIGCHLD and SIGCLD or  SIGPOLL  and  SIGIO,  assuming[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              they  correspond  to  the  same signal number.  kill -l will only list the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              preferred form, however kill -l alt will show if the alternative form cor‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              responds to a signal number.  For example, under Linux kill -l IO and kill[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -l POLL both output 29, hence kill -IO and kill -POLL have  the  same  ef‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fect.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Many systems will allow process IDs to be negative to kill a process group[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              or zero to kill the current process group.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       let arg ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Evaluate  each  arg  as an arithmetic expression.  See the section `Arith‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              metic Evaluation' in zshmisc(1) for a description  of  arithmetic  expres‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sions.   The  exit  status  is  0  if  the value of the last expression is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              nonzero, 1 if it is zero, and 2 if an error occurred.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       limit [ -hs ] [ resource [ limit ] ] ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Set or display resource limits.  Unless the -s flag is  given,  the  limit[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              applies  only the children of the shell.  If -s is given without other ar‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              guments, the resource limits of the current shell is set to the previously[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              set resource limits of the children.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If limit is not specified, print the current  limit  placed  on  resource,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              otherwise  set the limit to the specified value.  If the -h flag is given,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              use hard limits instead of soft limits.  If no resource  is  given,  print[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              all limits.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              When  looping over multiple resources, the shell will abort immediately if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              it detects a badly formed argument.  However, if it fails to set  a  limit[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for some other reason it will continue trying to set the remaining limits.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              resource can be one of:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              addressspace[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Maximum amount of address space used.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              aiomemorylocked[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Maximum amount of memory locked in RAM for AIO operations.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              aiooperations[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Maximum number of AIO operations.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cachedthreads[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Maximum number of cached threads.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              coredumpsize[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Maximum size of a core dump.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cputime[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Maximum CPU seconds per process.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              datasize[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Maximum data size (including stack) for each process.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              descriptors[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Maximum value for a file descriptor.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              filesize[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Largest single file allowed.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              kqueues[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Maximum number of kqueues allocated.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              maxproc[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Maximum number of processes.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              maxpthreads[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Maximum number of threads per process.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              memorylocked[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Maximum amount of memory locked in RAM.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              memoryuse[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Maximum resident set size.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              msgqueue[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Maximum number of bytes in POSIX message queues.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              posixlocks[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Maximum number of POSIX locks per user.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pseudoterminals[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Maximum number of pseudo-terminals.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              resident[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Maximum resident set size.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sigpending[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Maximum number of pending signals.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sockbufsize[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Maximum size of all socket buffers.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              stacksize[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Maximum stack size for each process.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              swapsize[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Maximum amount of swap used.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              vmemorysize[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Maximum amount of virtual memory.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Which  of  these resource limits are available depends on the system.  re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              source can be abbreviated to any unambiguous prefix.  It can  also  be  an[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              integer,  which corresponds to the integer defined for the resource by the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              operating system.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If argument corresponds to a number which is out of the range of  the  re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sources configured into the shell, the shell will try to read or write the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              limit  anyway,  and will report an error if this fails.  As the shell does[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              not store such resources internally, an attempt to set the limit will fail[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              unless the -s option is present.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              limit is a number, with an optional scaling factor, as follows:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              nh     hours[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              nk     kilobytes (default)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              nm     megabytes or minutes[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ng     gigabytes[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              [mm:]ss[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     minutes and seconds[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The limit command is not made available by default when the  shell  starts[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              in a mode emulating another shell.  It can be made available with the com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mand `zmodload -F zsh/rlimits b:limit'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       local [ {+|-}AHUahlprtux ] [ {+|-}EFLRZi [ n ] ] [ name[=value] ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Same as typeset, except that the options -g, and -f are not permitted.  In[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              this  case the -x option does not force the use of -g, i.e. exported vari‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ables will be local to functions.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       logout [ n ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Same as exit, except that it only works in a login shell.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       noglob simple command[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              See the section `Precommand Modifiers' in zshmisc(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       popd [ -q ] [ {+|-}n ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Remove an entry from the directory stack, and perform a cd to the new  top[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              directory.   With no argument, the current top entry is removed.  An argu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ment of the form `+n' identifies a stack entry by counting from  the  left[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of the list shown by the dirs command, starting with zero.  An argument of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  form -n counts from the right.  If the PUSHD_MINUS option is set, the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              meanings of `+' and `-' in this context are swapped.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If the -q (quiet) option is specified, the hook  function  chpwd  and  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              functions in the array $chpwd_functions are not called, and the new direc‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tory  stack  is not printed.  This is useful for calls to popd that do not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              change the environment seen by an interactive user.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       print [ -abcDilmnNoOpPrsSz ] [ -u n ] [ -f format ] [ -C cols ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m             [ -v name ] [ -xX tabstop ] [ -R [ -en ]] [ arg ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              With the `-f' option the arguments are printed  as  described  by  printf.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              With no flags or with the flag `-', the arguments are printed on the stan‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              dard  output as described by echo, with the following differences: the es‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cape sequence `\M-x' (or `\Mx') metafies the character x (sets the highest[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              bit), `\C-x' (or `\Cx') produces a control character  (`\C-@'  and  `\C-?'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              give  the characters NULL and delete), a character code in octal is repre‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sented by `\NNN' (instead of `\0NNN'), and `\E' is  a  synonym  for  `\e'.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Finally, if not in an escape sequence, `\' escapes the following character[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and is not printed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -a     Print  arguments  with  the column incrementing first.  Only useful[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     with the -c and -C options.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -b     Recognize all the escape sequences defined for the bindkey command,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     see the section `Zle Builtins' in zshzle(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -c     Print the arguments in columns.  Unless -a is also given, arguments[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     are printed with the row incrementing first.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -C cols[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Print the arguments in cols columns.  Unless -a is also given,  ar‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     guments are printed with the row incrementing first.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -D     Treat  the  arguments as paths, replacing directory prefixes with ~[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     expressions corresponding to directory names, as appropriate.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -i     If given together with -o or -O, sorting is performed case-indepen‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     dently.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -l     Print the arguments separated by newlines instead of spaces.  Note:[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     if the list of arguments is empty, print -l will still  output  one[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     empty  line.  To  print  a possibly-empty list of arguments one per[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     line, use print -C1, as in `print -rC1 -- "$list[@]"'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -m     Take the first argument as a pattern (should be quoted), and remove[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     it from the argument list together with subsequent  arguments  that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     do not match this pattern.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -n     Do not add a newline to the output.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -N     Print the arguments separated and terminated by nulls. Again, print[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     -rNC1  --  "$list[@]" is a canonical way to print an arbitrary list[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     as null-delimited records.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -o     Print the arguments sorted in ascending order.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -O     Print the arguments sorted in descending order.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -p     Print the arguments to the input of the coprocess.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -P     Perform prompt expansion (see EXPANSION OF PROMPT SEQUENCES in zsh‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     misc(1)).  In combination with `-f', prompt  escape  sequences  are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     parsed  only  within  interpolated arguments, not within the format[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     string.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -r     Ignore the escape conventions of echo.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -R     Emulate the BSD echo command, which does  not  process  escape  se‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     quences  unless  the  -e flag is given.  The -n flag suppresses the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     trailing newline.  Only the -e and -n flags  are  recognized  after[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     -R; all other arguments and options are printed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -s     Place  the  results  in the history list instead of on the standard[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     output.  Each argument to the print command is treated as a  single[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     word in the history, regardless of its content.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -S     Place  the  results  in the history list instead of on the standard[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     output.  In this case only a single argument is allowed; it will be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     split into words as if it were a full shell command line.  The  ef‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     fect  is  similar  to reading the line from a history file with the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     HIST_LEX_WORDS option active.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -u n   Print the arguments to file descriptor n.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -v name[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Store the printed arguments as the value of the parameter name.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -x tab-stop[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Expand leading tabs on each line of output in  the  printed  string[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     assuming a tab stop every tab-stop characters.  This is appropriate[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     for  formatting  code  that  may  be indented with tabs.  Note that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     leading tabs of any argument to print, not just the first, are  ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     panded,  even  if  print is using spaces to separate arguments (the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     column count is maintained across arguments but may be incorrect on[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     output owing to previous unexpanded tabs).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     The start of the output of each print  command  is  assumed  to  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     aligned  with  a tab stop.  Widths of multibyte characters are han‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     dled if the option MULTIBYTE is in effect.  This option is  ignored[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     if  other formatting options are in effect, namely column alignment[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     or printf style, or if output is to  a  special  location  such  as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     shell history or the command line editor.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -X tab-stop[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     This  is  similar to -x, except that all tabs in the printed string[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     are expanded.  This is appropriate if tabs in the arguments are be‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ing used to produce a table format.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -z     Push the arguments onto the  editing  buffer  stack,  separated  by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     spaces.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  any  of `-m', `-o' or `-O' are used in combination with `-f' and there[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              are no arguments (after the removal process in  the  case  of  `-m')  then[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              nothing is printed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       printf [ -v name ] format [ arg ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Print  the  arguments  according  to  the format specification. Formatting[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rules are the same as used in C. The same escape sequences as for echo are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              recognised in the format. All C conversion specifications ending in one of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              csdiouxXeEfgGn are handled. In addition to this, `%b' can be used  instead[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of  `%s'  to  cause  escape sequences in the argument to be recognised and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `%q' can be used to quote the argument in such a way that allows it to  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              reused  as  shell input. With the numeric format specifiers, if the corre‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sponding argument starts with a quote character, the numeric value of  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              following character is used as the number to print; otherwise the argument[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is  evaluated  as  an  arithmetic  expression. See the section `Arithmetic[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Evaluation' in zshmisc(1) for a  description  of  arithmetic  expressions.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              With  `%n',  the corresponding argument is taken as an identifier which is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              created as an integer parameter.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Normally, conversion specifications are applied to each argument in  order[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              but  they can explicitly specify the nth argument is to be used by replac‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ing `%' by `%n$' and `*' by `*n$'.  It is recommended that you do not  mix[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              references  of  this explicit style with the normal style and the handling[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of such mixed styles may be subject to future change.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If arguments remain unused after formatting, the format string  is  reused[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              until  all  arguments have been consumed. With the print builtin, this can[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              be suppressed by using the -r option. If more arguments  are  required  by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  format  than  have  been specified, the behaviour is as if zero or an[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              empty string had been specified as the argument.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The -v option causes the output to be stored as the value of the parameter[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              name, instead of printed. If name is an array and  the  format  string  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              reused  when  consuming  arguments then one array element will be used for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              each use of the format string.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       pushd [ -qsLP ] [ arg ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       pushd [ -qsLP ] old new[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       pushd [ -qsLP ] {+|-}n[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Change the current directory, and push the old current directory onto  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              directory  stack.  In the first form, change the current directory to arg.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If arg is not specified, change to the second directory on the stack (that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is, exchange the top two entries), or change to $HOME if the PUSHD_TO_HOME[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              option is set or if there is only one entry on the stack.  Otherwise,  arg[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is  interpreted  as  it would be by cd.  The meaning of old and new in the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              second form is also the same as for cd.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The third form of pushd changes directory by rotating the directory  list.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              An argument of the form `+n' identifies a stack entry by counting from the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              left  of the list shown by the dirs command, starting with zero.  An argu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ment of the form `-n' counts from the right.  If the PUSHD_MINUS option is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              set, the meanings of `+' and `-' in this context are swapped.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If the -q (quiet) option is specified, the hook  function  chpwd  and  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              functions in the array $chpwd_functions are not called, and the new direc‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tory  stack is not printed.  This is useful for calls to pushd that do not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              change the environment seen by an interactive user.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If the option -q is not specified and the shell option PUSHD_SILENT is not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              set, the directory stack will be printed after a pushd is performed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The options -s, -L and -P have the same meanings as for the cd builtin.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       pushln [ arg ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Equivalent to print -nz.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       pwd [ -rLP ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Print the absolute pathname of the current working directory.  If  the  -r[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              or  the  -P flag is specified, or the CHASE_LINKS option is set and the -L[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              flag is not given, the printed path will not contain symbolic links.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       r      Same as fc -e -.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       read [ -rszpqAclneE ] [ -t [ num ] ] [ -k [ num ] ] [ -d delim ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m            [ -u n ] [ [name][?prompt] ] [ name ...  ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Read one line and break it into fields using the  characters  in  $IFS  as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              separators,  except  as  noted  below.  The first field is assigned to the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              first name, the second field to  the  second  name,  etc.,  with  leftover[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fields  assigned  to the last name.  If name is omitted then REPLY is used[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for scalars and reply for arrays.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -r     Raw mode: a `\' at the end of a line does not signify line continu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ation and backslashes in the line don't quote the following charac‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ter and are not removed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -s     Don't echo back characters if reading from the terminal.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -q     Read only one character from the terminal and set name  to  `y'  if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     this character was `y' or `Y' and to `n' otherwise.  With this flag[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     set the return status is zero only if the character was `y' or `Y'.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     This  option may be used with a timeout (see -t); if the read times[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     out, or encounters end of file, status 2  is  returned.   Input  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     read from the terminal unless one of -u or -p is present.  This op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     tion may also be used within zle widgets.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -k [ num ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Read  only  one (or num) characters.  All are assigned to the first[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     name, without word splitting.  This flag  is  ignored  when  -q  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     present.  Input is read from the terminal unless one of -u or -p is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     present.  This option may also be used within zle widgets.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     Note  that  despite  the  mnemonic `key' this option does read full[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     characters, which may consist  of  multiple  bytes  if  the  option[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     MULTIBYTE is set.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -z     Read  one  entry  from the editor buffer stack and assign it to the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     first name, without word splitting.  Text is pushed onto the  stack[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     with  `print  -z'  or with push-line from the line editor (see zsh‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     zle(1)).  This flag is ignored when the -k or -q flags are present.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -e[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -E     The input read is printed (echoed) to the standard output.  If  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     -e flag is used, no input is assigned to the parameters.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -A     The  first  name is taken as the name of an array and all words are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     assigned to it.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -c[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -l     These flags are allowed only if called inside a function  used  for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     completion (specified with the -K flag to compctl).  If the -c flag[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     is given, the words of the current command are read. If the -l flag[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     is  given,  the  whole line is assigned as a scalar.  If both flags[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     are present, -l is used and -c is ignored.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -n     Together with -c, the number of the word the cursor is on is  read.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     With -l, the index of the character the cursor is on is read.  Note[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     that  the  command name is word number 1, not word 0, and that when[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the cursor is at the end of the line, its character  index  is  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     length of the line plus one.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -u n   Input is read from file descriptor n.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -p     Input is read from the coprocess.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -d delim[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Input  is  terminated by the first character of delim instead of by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     newline.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -t [ num ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Test if input is available before attempting to read.   If  num  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     present, it must begin with a digit and will be evaluated to give a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     number  of  seconds,  which may be a floating point number; in this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     case the read times out if input is not available within this time.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     If num is not present, it is taken to be zero, so that read returns[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     immediately if no input is available.  If no  input  is  available,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     return status 1 and do not set any variables.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     This  option  is  not available when reading from the editor buffer[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     with -z, when called from within completion with -c or -l, with  -q[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     which  clears  the  input queue before reading, or within zle where[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     other mechanisms should be used to test for input.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     Note that read does not attempt to alter the input processing mode.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     The default mode is canonical input, in which  an  entire  line  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     read  at  a time, so usually `read -t' will not read anything until[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     an entire line has been typed.  However, when reading from the ter‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     minal with -k input is processed one key at a time; in  this  case,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     only  availability  of  the first character is tested, so that e.g.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     `read -t -k 2' can still block on the second  character.   Use  two[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     instances of `read -t -k' if this is not what is wanted.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  the  first argument contains a `?', the remainder of this word is used[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              as a prompt on standard error when the shell is interactive.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The value (exit status) of read is 1 when an end-of-file  is  encountered,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              or  when  -c or -l is present and the command is not called from a compctl[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              function, or as described for -q.  Otherwise the value is 0.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The behavior of some combinations of the -k, -p, -q, -u and  -z  flags  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              undefined.  Presently -q cancels all the others, -p cancels -u, -k cancels[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -z, and otherwise -z cancels both -p and -u.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The -c or -l flags cancel any and all of -kpquz.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       readonly[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Same  as  typeset -r.  With the POSIX_BUILTINS option set, same as typeset[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -gr.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       rehash Same as hash -r.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       return [ n ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Causes a shell function or `.' script to return  to  the  invoking  script[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              with the return status specified by an arithmetic expression n.  For exam‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ple, the following prints `42':[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     () { integer foo=40; return "foo + 2" }[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     echo $?[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If n is omitted, the return status is that of the last command executed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  return  was  executed from a trap in a TRAPNAL function, the effect is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              different for zero and non-zero return status.  With zero status (or after[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              an implicit return at the end of the trap), the shell will return to what‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ever it was previously processing; with a non-zero status, the shell  will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              behave  as  interrupted  except  that the return status of the trap is re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tained.  Note that the numeric value of the signal which caused  the  trap[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is  passed  as the first argument, so the statement `return "128+$1"' will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              return the same status as if the signal had not been trapped.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       sched  See the section `The zsh/sched Module' in zshmodules(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       set [ {+|-}options | {+|-}o [ option_name ] ] ... [ {+|-}A [ name ] ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m           [ arg ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Set the options for the shell and/or set the positional parameters, or de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              clare and set an array.  If the -s option is given, it causes  the  speci‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fied  arguments to be sorted before assigning them to the positional para‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              meters (or to the array name if -A is used).  With +s  sort  arguments  in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              descending  order.  For the meaning of the other flags, see zshoptions(1).[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Flags may be specified by name using the -o option. If no option  name  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              supplied with -o, the current option states are printed:  see the descrip‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion of setopt below for more information on the format.  With +o they are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              printed in a form that can be used as input to the shell.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  the -A flag is specified, name is set to an array containing the given[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              args; if no name is specified, all arrays are printed together with  their[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              values.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  +A  is used and name is an array, the given arguments will replace the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              initial elements of that array; if no name is specified,  all  arrays  are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              printed without their values.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The behaviour of arguments after -A name or +A name depends on whether the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              option  KSH_ARRAYS is set.  If it is not set, all arguments following name[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              are treated as values for the array, regardless of their form.  If the op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion is set, normal option processing continues at that point; only  regu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              lar arguments are treated as values for the array.  This means that[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     set -A array -x -- foo[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              sets  array to `-x -- foo' if KSH_ARRAYS is not set, but sets the array to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              foo and turns on the option `-x' if it is set.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If the -A flag is not present, but there are arguments beyond the options,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the positional parameters are set.  If the option list (if any) is  termi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              nated  by `--', and there are no further arguments, the positional parame‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ters will be unset.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If no arguments and no `--' are given, then the names and  values  of  all[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              parameters  are  printed  on the standard output.  If the only argument is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `+', the names of all parameters are printed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              For historical reasons, `set -' is treated as `set +xv' and `set  -  args'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              as  `set  +xv  -- args' when in any other emulation mode than zsh's native[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mode.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       setcap See the section `The zsh/cap Module' in zshmodules(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       setopt [ {+|-}options | {+|-}o option_name ] [ -m ] [ name ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Set the options for the shell.  All options specified either with flags or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              by name are set.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If no arguments are supplied, the names of all options currently  set  are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              printed.   The  form  is chosen so as to minimize the differences from the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              default options for the current emulation (the default emulation being na‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tive zsh, shown as <Z> in zshoptions(1)).  Options that are on by  default[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for the emulation are shown with the prefix no only if they are off, while[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              other options are shown without the prefix no and only if they are on.  In[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              addition  to  options  changed from the default state by the user, any op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tions activated automatically by the shell (for example, SHIN_STDIN or IN‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              TERACTIVE) will be shown in the list.  The format is further  modified  by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  option  KSH_OPTION_PRINT,  however the rationale for choosing options[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              with or without the no prefix remains the same in this case.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If the -m flag is given the arguments are taken as patterns (which  should[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              be  quoted  to protect them from filename expansion), and all options with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              names matching these patterns are set.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that a bad option name does not cause execution of  subsequent  shell[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              code  to be aborted; this is behaviour is different from that of `set -o'.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This is because set is regarded as a special builtin by  the  POSIX  stan‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              dard, but setopt is not.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       shift [ -p ] [ n ] [ name ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The  positional parameters ${n+1} ... are renamed to $1 ..., where n is an[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              arithmetic expression that defaults to 1.  If any names are given then the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              arrays with these names are shifted instead of the positional parameters.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If the option -p is given arguments are instead removed (popped) from  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              end rather than the start of the array.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       source file [ arg ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Same  as  `.', except that the current directory is always searched and is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              always searched first, before directories in $path.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       stat   See the section `The zsh/stat Module' in zshmodules(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       suspend [ -f ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Suspend the execution of the shell (send it a SIGTSTP) until it receives a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              SIGCONT.  Unless the -f option is given, this will refuse to suspend a lo‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              gin shell.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       test [ arg ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       [ [ arg ... ] ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Like the system version of test.  Added for compatibility; use conditional[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              expressions instead (see the section `Conditional Expressions').  The main[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              differences between the conditional expression syntax and the test  and  [[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              builtins  are:  these commands are not handled syntactically, so for exam‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ple an empty variable expansion may cause an argument to be omitted;  syn‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tax  errors  cause  status  2 to be returned instead of a shell error; and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              arithmetic operators expect integer arguments rather than  arithmetic  ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pressions.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The command attempts to implement POSIX and its extensions where these are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              specified.   Unfortunately  there are intrinsic ambiguities in the syntax;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              in particular there is no distinction between test operators  and  strings[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that resemble them.  The standard attempts to resolve these for small num‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              bers  of  arguments (up to four); for five or more arguments compatibility[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cannot be relied on.  Users are urged wherever possible to  use  the  `[['[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              test syntax which does not have these ambiguities.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       times  Print  the  accumulated  user  and  system  times  for  the  shell and for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              processes run from the shell.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       trap [ arg ] [ sig ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              arg is a series of commands (usually quoted to protect it  from  immediate[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              evaluation  by  the shell) to be read and executed when the shell receives[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              any of the signals specified by one or more sig args.   Each  sig  can  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              given  as  a number, or as the name of a signal either with or without the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              string SIG in front (e.g. 1, HUP, and SIGHUP are all the same signal).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If arg is `-', then the specified signals are reset to their defaults, or,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              if no sig args are present, all traps are reset.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If arg is an empty string, then the specified signals are ignored  by  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              shell (and by the commands it invokes).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  arg  is omitted but one or more sig args are provided (i.e.  the first[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              argument is a valid signal number or name), the effect is the same  as  if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              arg had been specified as `-'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The  trap  command  with no arguments prints a list of commands associated[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              with each signal.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If sig is ZERR then arg will be executed after each command with a nonzero[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              exit status.  ERR is an alias for ZERR on systems that have no SIGERR sig‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              nal (this is the usual case).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If sig is DEBUG then arg will be executed before each command if  the  op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion  DEBUG_BEFORE_CMD  is set (as it is by default), else after each com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mand.  Here, a `command' is what is described as a `sublist' in the  shell[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              grammar,  see  the  section SIMPLE COMMANDS & PIPELINES in zshmisc(1).  If[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              DEBUG_BEFORE_CMD is set various additional features are available.  First,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              it is possible to skip the next command by setting  the  option  ERR_EXIT;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              see  the  description  of the ERR_EXIT option in zshoptions(1).  Also, the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              shell parameter ZSH_DEBUG_CMD is set to the string  corresponding  to  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              command  to  be executed following the trap.  Note that this string is re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              constructed from the internal format and may not be formatted the same way[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              as the original text.  The parameter is unset after the trap is executed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If sig is 0 or EXIT and the trap statement is executed inside the body  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              a function, then the command arg is executed after the function completes.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The  value of $? at the start of execution is the exit status of the shell[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              or the return status of the function exiting.  If sig is 0 or EXIT and the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              trap statement is not executed inside the body of  a  function,  then  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              command  arg  is  executed when the shell terminates; the trap runs before[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              any zshexit hook functions.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              ZERR, DEBUG, and EXIT traps are not executed inside other traps.  ZERR and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              DEBUG traps are kept within subshells, while other traps are reset.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that traps defined with the trap builtin are slightly different  from[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              those  defined as `TRAPNAL () { ... }', as the latter have their own func‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion environment (line numbers, local variables, etc.)  while  the  former[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              use  the  environment of the command in which they were called.  For exam‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ple,[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     trap 'print $LINENO' DEBUG[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              will print the line number of a command executed after it has run, while[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     TRAPDEBUG() { print $LINENO; }[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              will always print the number zero.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Alternative signal names  are  allowed  as  described  under  kill  above.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Defining  a  trap  under  either name causes any trap under an alternative[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              name to be removed.  However, it is recommended that for consistency users[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              stick exclusively to one name or another.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       true [ arg ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Do nothing and return an exit status of 0.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ttyctl [ -fu ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The -f option freezes the tty (i.e. terminal or terminal emulator), and -u[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              unfreezes it.  When the tty is frozen, no changes made to the tty settings[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              by external programs will be honored by the shell, except for  changes  in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  size of the screen; the shell will simply reset the settings to their[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              previous values as soon as each command exits or is suspended.  Thus, stty[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and similar programs have no effect when the tty is frozen.  Freezing  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tty  does not cause the current state to be remembered: instead, it causes[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              future changes to the state to be blocked.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Without options it reports whether the terminal is frozen or not.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that, regardless of whether the tty is frozen or not, the shell needs[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to change the settings when the line editor starts, so unfreezing the  tty[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              does  not  guarantee  settings  made  on  the  command line are preserved.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Strings of commands run between editing the command line will see  a  con‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sistent  tty  state.  See also the shell variable STTY for a means of ini‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tialising the tty before running external commands and/or freezing the tty[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              around a single command.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       type [ -wfpamsS ] name ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Equivalent to whence -v.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       typeset [ {+|-}AHUaghlmrtux ] [ {+|-}EFLRZip [ n ] ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m               [ + ] [ name[=value] ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       typeset -T [ {+|-}Uglrux ] [ {+|-}LRZp [ n ] ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m               [ + | SCALAR[=value] array[=(value ...)] [ sep ] ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       typeset -f [ {+|-}TUkmtuz ] [ + ] [ name ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Set or display attributes and values for shell parameters.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Except as noted below for control flags that change the behavior, a  para‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              meter  is  created for each name that does not already refer to one.  When[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              inside a function, a new parameter is created for every name  (even  those[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that  already exist), and is unset again when the function completes.  See[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `Local Parameters' in zshparam(1).  The same rules apply to special  shell[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              parameters, which retain their special attributes when made local.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              For  each  name=value  assignment, the parameter name is set to value.  If[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the assignment is omitted and name does not refer to an  existing  parame‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ter,  a  new parameter is intialized to empty string, zero, or empty array[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (as appropriate), unless the shell option TYPESET_TO_UNSET is  set.   When[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that  option is set, the parameter attributes are recorded but the parame‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ter remains unset.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If the shell option TYPESET_SILENT is not set,  for  each  remaining  name[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that  refers to a parameter that is already set, the name and value of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              parameter are printed in the form of an assignment.   Nothing  is  printed[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for newly-created parameters, or when any attribute flags listed below are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              given along with the name.  Using `+' instead of minus to introduce an at‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tribute turns it off.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If no name is present, the names and values of all parameters are printed.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              In  this case the attribute flags restrict the display to only those para‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              meters that have the specified attributes, and using `+' rather  than  `-'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to introduce the flag suppresses printing of the values of parameters when[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              there is no parameter name.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              All  forms  of  the command handle scalar assignment.  Array assignment is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              possible if any of the reserved words declare, export, float, integer, lo‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cal, readonly or typeset is matched when the line is parsed (N.B. not when[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              it is executed).  In this case the arguments are  parsed  as  assignments,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              except  that the `+=' syntax and the GLOB_ASSIGN option are not supported,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and scalar values after = are not split further into words,  even  if  ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              panded  (regardless  of the setting of the KSH_TYPESET option; this option[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is obsolete).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Examples of the differences between command and reserved word parsing:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     # Reserved word parsing[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     typeset svar=$(echo one word) avar=(several words)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The above creates a scalar parameter svar and an array parameter  avar  as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              if the assignments had been[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     svar="one word"[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     avar=(several words)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              On the other hand:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     # Normal builtin interface[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     builtin typeset svar=$(echo two words)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The builtin keyword causes the above to use the standard builtin interface[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to  typeset  in which argument parsing is performed in the same way as for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              other commands.  This example creates a scalar svar containing  the  value[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              two  and  another scalar parameter words with no value.  An array value in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              this case would either cause an error or be treated as an obscure  set  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              glob qualifiers.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Arbitrary arguments are allowed if they take the form of assignments after[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              command line expansion; however, these only perform scalar assignment:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     var='svar=val'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     typeset $var[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The  above  sets  the scalar parameter svar to the value val.  Parentheses[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              around the value within var would not cause array assignment as they  will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              be treated as ordinary characters when $var is substituted.  Any non-triv‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ial expansion in the name part of the assignment causes the argument to be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              treated in this fashion:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     typeset {var1,var2,var3}=name[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The  above  syntax  is  valid,  and has the expected effect of setting the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              three parameters to the same value, but the command line is  parsed  as  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              set  of  three  normal  command line arguments to typeset after expansion.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Hence it is not possible to assign to multiple arrays by this means.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that each interface to any of the  commands  may  be  disabled  sepa‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rately.   For example, `disable -r typeset' disables the reserved word in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              terface to typeset, exposing the builtin interface, while  `disable  type‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              set'  disables  the builtin.  Note that disabling the reserved word inter‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              face for typeset may cause problems with the output of `typeset -p', which[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              assumes the reserved word interface is available in order to restore array[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and associative array values.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Unlike parameter assignment statements, typeset's exit status  on  an  as‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              signment  that  involves  a command substitution does not reflect the exit[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              status of the command substitution.  Therefore, to test for an error in  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              command  substitution,  separate the declaration of the parameter from its[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              initialization:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     # WRONG[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     typeset var1=$(exit 1) || echo "Trouble with var1"[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     # RIGHT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     typeset var1 && var1=$(exit 1) || echo "Trouble with var1"[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              To initialize a parameter param to a command output and mark it  readonly,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              use  typeset  -r  param  or  readonly param after the parameter assignment[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              statement.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If no attribute flags are given, and either no name arguments are  present[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              or  the flag +m is used, then each parameter name printed is preceded by a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              list of the attributes of that parameter  (array,  association,  exported,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              float,  integer,  readonly, or undefined for autoloaded parameters not yet[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              loaded).  If +m is used with attribute flags, and all those flags are  in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              troduced with +, the matching parameter names are printed but their values[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              are not.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The following control flags change the behavior of typeset:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              +      If  `+'  appears  by  itself in a separate word as the last option,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     then the names of all parameters (functions with -f)  are  printed,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     but  the  values  (function bodies) are not.  No name arguments may[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     appear, and it is an error for any other  options  to  follow  `+'.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     The  effect  of  `+'  is as if all attribute flags which precede it[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     were given with a `+' prefix.   For  example,  `typeset  -U  +'  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     equivalent  to  `typeset  +U'  and displays the names of all arrays[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     having the uniqueness attribute, whereas `typeset -f -U +' displays[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the names of all autoloadable functions.  If + is the only  option,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     then  type  information (array, readonly, etc.) is also printed for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     each parameter, in the same manner as `typeset +m "*"'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -g     The -g (global) means that any resulting parameter will not be  re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     stricted  to local scope.  Note that this does not necessarily mean[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     that the parameter will be global, as the flag will  apply  to  any[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     existing  parameter  (even  if  unset)  from an enclosing function.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     This flag does not affect the parameter after  creation,  hence  it[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     has  no  effect when listing existing parameters, nor does the flag[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     +g have any effect except in combination with -m (see below).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -m     If the -m flag is given the name arguments are  taken  as  patterns[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     (use  quoting  to prevent these from being interpreted as file pat‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     terns).  With no attribute flags, all parameters (or functions with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the -f flag) with matching names  are  printed  (the  shell  option[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     TYPESET_SILENT is not used in this case).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     If  the  +g flag is combined with -m, a new local parameter is cre‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ated for every matching parameter that is not already local.   Oth‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     erwise  -m  applies  all other flags or assignments to the existing[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     parameters.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     Except when assignments are made with name=value, using  +m  forces[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the  matching  parameters  and their attributes to be printed, even[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     inside a function.  Note that -m is  ignored  if  no  patterns  are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     given, so `typeset -m' displays attributes but `typeset -a +m' does[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     not.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -p [ n ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     If the -p option is given, parameters and values are printed in the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     form  of  a typeset command with an assignment, regardless of other[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     flags and options.  Note that the -H  flag  on  parameters  is  re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     spected; no value will be shown for these parameters.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     -p may be followed by an optional integer argument.  Currently only[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the  value 1 is supported.  In this case arrays and associative ar‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     rays are printed with newlines between indented elements for  read‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ability.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -T [ scalar[=value] array[=(value ...)] [ sep ] ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     This  flag  has  a  different meaning when used with -f; see below.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Otherwise the -T option requires zero, two, or three  arguments  to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     be  present.   With no arguments, the list of parameters created in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     this fashion is shown.  With two or three arguments, the first  two[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     are  the name of a scalar and of an array parameter (in that order)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     that will be tied together in the manner of $PATH and  $path.   The[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     optional  third argument is a single-character separator which will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     be used to join the elements of the array to form  the  scalar;  if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     absent,  a  colon is used, as with $PATH.  Only the first character[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     of the separator is significant; any remaining characters  are  ig‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     nored.  Multibyte characters are not yet supported.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     Only one of the scalar and array parameters may be assigned an ini‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     tial  value  (the  restrictions on assignment forms described above[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     also apply).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     Both the scalar and the array may be manipulated as normal.  If one[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     is unset, the other will automatically be unset too.  There  is  no[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     way  of  untying  the variables without unsetting them, nor of con‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     verting the type of one of them with another  typeset  command;  +T[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     does  not  work,  assigning an array to scalar is an error, and as‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     signing a scalar to array sets it to be a single-element array.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     Note that both `typeset -xT ...'  and `export  -T  ...'  work,  but[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     only the scalar will be marked for export.  Setting the value using[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the  scalar  version causes a split on all separators (which cannot[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     be quoted).  It is possible to apply  -T  to  two  previously  tied[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     variables  but  with a different separator character, in which case[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the variables remain joined as before but the separator is changed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     When an existing scalar is tied to a new array, the  value  of  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     scalar is preserved but no attribute other than export will be pre‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     served.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Attribute  flags  that  transform the final value (-L, -R, -Z, -l, -u) are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              only applied to the expanded value at the point of a  parameter  expansion[0m
