[38;2;248;248;242m              expression  using `$'.  They are not applied when a parameter is retrieved[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              internally by the shell for any purpose.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The following attribute flags may be specified:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -A     The names refer to associative array parameters; see `Array Parame‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ters' in zshparam(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -L [ n ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Left justify and remove leading blanks from the value when the  pa‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     rameter  is expanded.  If n is nonzero, it defines the width of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     field.  If n is zero, the width is determined by the width  of  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     value  of the first assignment.  In the case of numeric parameters,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the length of the complete value assigned to the parameter is  used[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     to determine the width, not the value that would be output.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     The  width is the count of characters, which may be multibyte char‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     acters if the MULTIBYTE option is in effect.  Note that the  screen[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     width  of  the  character is not taken into account; if this is re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     quired, use padding with parameter expansion flags ${(ml...)...} as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     described in `Parameter Expansion Flags' in zshexpn(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     When the parameter is expanded, it is  filled  on  the  right  with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     blanks or truncated if necessary to fit the field.  Note truncation[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     can  lead  to  unexpected results with numeric parameters.  Leading[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     zeros are removed if the -Z flag is also set.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -R [ n ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Similar to -L, except that right justification is  used;  when  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     parameter  is  expanded,  the  field  is left filled with blanks or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     truncated from the end.  May not be combined with the -Z flag.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -U     For arrays (but not for associative arrays), keep  only  the  first[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     occurrence of each duplicated value.  This may also be set for tied[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     parameters (see -T) or colon-separated special parameters like PATH[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     or FIGNORE, etc.  Note the flag takes effect on assignment, and the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     type  of the variable being assigned to is determinative; for vari‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ables with shared values it is therefore  recommended  to  set  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     flag for all interfaces, e.g. `typeset -U PATH path'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     This flag has a different meaning when used with -f; see below.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -Z [ n ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Specially  handled if set along with the -L flag.  Otherwise, simi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     lar to -R, except that leading zeros are used for  padding  instead[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     of blanks if the first non-blank character is a digit.  Numeric pa‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     rameters  are  specially  handled:  they  are  always  eligible for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     padding with zeroes, and the zeroes are inserted at an  appropriate[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     place in the output.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -a     The  names  refer  to  array parameters.  An array parameter may be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     created this way, but it may be assigned to in the  typeset  state‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ment only if the reserved word form of typeset is enabled (as it is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     by  default).   When displaying, both normal and associative arrays[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     are shown.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -f     The names refer to functions rather than  parameters.   No  assign‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ments  can  be made, and the only other valid flags are -t, -T, -k,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     -u, -U and -z.  The flag -t turns on  execution  tracing  for  this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     function;  the flag -T does the same, but turns off tracing for any[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     named (not anonymous) function called from the present one,  unless[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     that  function  also  has  the  -t or -T flag.  The -u and -U flags[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     cause the function to be marked for  autoloading;  -U  also  causes[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     alias  expansion to be suppressed when the function is loaded.  See[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the description of the `autoload' builtin for details.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     Note that the builtin functions provides the same  basic  capabili‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ties  as  typeset  -f  but gives access to a few extra options; au‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     toload gives further additional options for the  case  typeset  -fu[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     and typeset -fU.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -h     Hide: only useful for special parameters (those marked `<S>' in the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     table  in zshparam(1)), and for local parameters with the same name[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     as a special parameter, though harmless for others.  A special  pa‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     rameter with this attribute will not retain its special effect when[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     made  local.   Thus  after `typeset -h PATH', a function containing[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     `typeset PATH' will create an ordinary local parameter without  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     usual  behaviour  of  PATH.  Alternatively, the local parameter may[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     itself be given this attribute; hence inside a function `typeset -h[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     PATH' creates an ordinary local parameter and the special PATH  pa‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     rameter is not altered in any way.  It is also possible to create a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     local parameter using `typeset +h special', where the local copy of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     special will retain its special properties regardless of having the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     -h  attribute.  Global special parameters loaded from shell modules[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     (currently those in zsh/mapfile and  zsh/parameter)  are  automati‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     cally given the -h attribute to avoid name clashes.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -H     Hide  value:  specifies  that typeset will not display the value of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the parameter when listing parameters; the display for such parame‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ters is always as if the `+' flag had been given.  Use of the para‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     meter is in other respects normal, and the option does not apply if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the parameter is specified by name, or by pattern with the  -m  op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     tion.   This is on by default for the parameters in the zsh/parame‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ter and zsh/mapfile modules.  Note, however,  that  unlike  the  -h[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     flag this is also useful for non-special parameters.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -i [ n ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Use an internal integer representation.  If n is nonzero it defines[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the output arithmetic base, otherwise it is determined by the first[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     assignment.  Bases from 2 to 36 inclusive are allowed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -E [ n ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Use an internal double-precision floating point representation.  On[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     output the variable will be converted to scientific notation.  If n[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     is nonzero it defines the number of significant figures to display;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the default is ten.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -F [ n ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Use an internal double-precision floating point representation.  On[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     output  the variable will be converted to fixed-point decimal nota‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     tion.  If n is nonzero it defines the number of digits  to  display[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     after the decimal point; the default is ten.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -l     Convert  the  result  to  lower  case whenever the parameter is ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     panded.  The value is not converted when assigned.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -r     The given names are marked readonly.  Note that if name is  a  spe‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     cial parameter, the readonly attribute can be turned on, but cannot[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     then be turned off.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     If the POSIX_BUILTINS option is set, the readonly attribute is more[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     restrictive: unset variables can be marked readonly and cannot then[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     be  set; furthermore, the readonly attribute cannot be removed from[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     any variable.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     It is still possible to change other  attributes  of  the  variable[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     though,  some  of  which like -U or -Z would affect the value. More[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     generally, the readonly attribute should not be relied on as a  se‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     curity mechanism.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     Note that in zsh (like in pdksh but unlike most other shells) it is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     still  possible to create a local variable of the same name as this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     is considered a different variable (though this variable, too,  can[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     be marked readonly). Special variables that have been made readonly[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     retain their value and readonly attribute when made local.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -t     Tags  the  named  parameters.   Tags have no special meaning to the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     shell.  This flag has a different meaning when used  with  -f;  see[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     above.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -u     Convert  the  result  to  upper  case whenever the parameter is ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     panded.  The value is not converted when assigned.  This flag has a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     different meaning when used with -f; see above.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -x     Mark for automatic export to the environment of  subsequently  exe‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     cuted  commands.   If the option GLOBAL_EXPORT is set, this implies[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the option -g, unless +g is also explicitly given; in  other  words[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the parameter is not made local to the enclosing function.  This is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     for compatibility with previous versions of zsh.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ulimit [ -HSa ] [ { -bcdfiklmnpqrsTtvwx | -N resource } [ limit ] ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Set  or  display resource limits of the shell and the processes started by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the shell.  The value of limit can be a number in the unit specified below[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              or one of the values `unlimited', which removes the limit on the resource,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              or `hard', which uses the current value of the hard limit on the resource.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              By default, only soft limits are manipulated. If the -H flag is given  use[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              hard limits instead of soft limits.  If the -S flag is given together with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the -H flag set both hard and soft limits.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If no options are used, the file size limit (-f) is assumed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  limit  is  omitted  the  current  value of the specified resources are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              printed.  When more than one resource value is printed, the limit name and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              unit is printed before each value.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              When looping over multiple resources, the shell will abort immediately  if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              it  detects  a badly formed argument.  However, if it fails to set a limit[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for some other reason it will continue trying to set the remaining limits.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Not all the following resources are supported  on  all  systems.   Running[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ulimit -a will show which are supported.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -a     Lists all of the current resource limits.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -b     Socket buffer size in bytes (N.B. not kilobytes)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -c     512-byte blocks on the size of core dumps.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -d     Kilobytes on the size of the data segment.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -f     512-byte blocks on the size of files written.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -i     The number of pending signals.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -k     The number of kqueues allocated.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -l     Kilobytes on the size of locked-in memory.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -m     Kilobytes on the size of physical memory.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -n     open file descriptors.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -p     The number of pseudo-terminals.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -q     Bytes in POSIX message queues.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -r     Maximum  real  time  priority.   On  some systems where this is not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     available, such as NetBSD, this has the same effect as -T for  com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     patibility with sh.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -s     Kilobytes on the size of the stack.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -T     The number of simultaneous threads available to the user.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -t     CPU seconds to be used.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -u     The number of processes available to the user.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -v     Kilobytes  on  the  size  of  virtual memory.  On some systems this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     refers to the limit called `address space'.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -w     Kilobytes on the size of swapped out memory.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -x     The number of locks on files.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              A resource may also be specified by integer in  the  form  `-N  resource',[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              where  resource corresponds to the integer defined for the resource by the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              operating system.  This may be used to set the limits for resources  known[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to  the shell which do not correspond to option letters.  Such limits will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              be shown by number in the output of `ulimit -a'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The number may alternatively be out of the range of limits  compiled  into[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the shell.  The shell will try to read or write the limit anyway, and will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              report an error if this fails.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       umask [ -S ] [ mask ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The  umask  is  set to mask.  mask can be either an octal number or a sym‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              bolic value as described in the chmod(1) man page.  If  mask  is  omitted,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the current value is printed.  The -S option causes the mask to be printed[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              as  a  symbolic value.  Otherwise, the mask is printed as an octal number.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that in the symbolic form the permissions you specify are those which[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              are to be allowed (not denied) to the users specified.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       unalias [ -ams ] name ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Removes aliases.  This command works the same as unhash  -a,  except  that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the -a option removes all regular or global aliases, or with -s all suffix[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              aliases:  in  this case no name arguments may appear.  The options -m (re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              move by pattern) and -s without -a (remove listed suffix  aliases)  behave[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              as  for  unhash -a.  Note that the meaning of -a is different between una‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              lias and unhash.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       unfunction[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Same as unhash -f.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       unhash [ -adfms ] name ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Remove the element named name from an internal hash table.  The default is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              remove elements from the command hash table.  The -a option causes  unhash[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to  remove  regular or global aliases; note when removing a global aliases[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that the argument must be quoted to prevent it from being expanded  before[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              being passed to the command.  The -s option causes unhash to remove suffix[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              aliases.   The  -f option causes unhash to remove shell functions.  The -d[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              options causes unhash to remove named directories.   If  the  -m  flag  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              given  the arguments are taken as patterns (should be quoted) and all ele‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ments of the corresponding hash table with matching names will be removed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       unlimit [ -hs ] resource ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The resource limit for each resource is set to the hard limit.  If the  -h[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              flag  is given and the shell has appropriate privileges, the hard resource[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              limit for each resource is removed.  The resources of  the  shell  process[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              are only changed if the -s flag is given.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The unlimit command is not made available by default when the shell starts[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              in a mode emulating another shell.  It can be made available with the com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mand `zmodload -F zsh/rlimits b:unlimit'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       unset [ -fmv ] name ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Each  named parameter is unset.  Local parameters remain local even if un‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              set; they appear unset within scope, but the  previous  value  will  still[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              reappear when the scope ends.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Individual  elements of associative array parameters may be unset by using[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              subscript syntax on name, which should be quoted (or  the  entire  command[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              prefixed with noglob) to protect the subscript from filename generation.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If the -m flag is specified the arguments are taken as patterns (should be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              quoted)  and all parameters with matching names are unset.  Note that this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cannot be used when unsetting associative array elements, as the subscript[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              will be treated as part of the pattern.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The -v flag specifies that name refers to parameters. This is the  default[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              behaviour.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              unset -f is equivalent to unfunction.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       unsetopt [ {+|-}options | {+|-}o option_name ] [ name ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Unset  the options for the shell.  All options specified either with flags[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              or by name are unset.  If no arguments are supplied, the names of all  op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tions  currently unset are printed.  If the -m flag is given the arguments[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              are taken as patterns (which should be quoted to preserve them from  being[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              interpreted  as  glob patterns), and all options with names matching these[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              patterns are unset.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vared  See the section `Zle Builtins' in zshzle(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       wait [ job ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Wait for the specified jobs or processes.  If job is not  given  then  all[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              currently active child processes are waited for.  Each job can be either a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              job  specification  or the process ID of a job in the job table.  The exit[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              status from this command is that of the job waited for.  If job represents[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              an  unknown  job  or  process  ID,  a  warning  is  printed  (unless   the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              POSIX_BUILTINS option is set) and the exit status is 127.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              It  is possible to wait for recent processes (specified by process ID, not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              by job) that were running in the background even if the  process  has  ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ited.  Typically the process ID will be recorded by capturing the value of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  variable $! immediately after the process has been started.  There is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              a limit on the number of process IDs remembered  by  the  shell;  this  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              given  by the value of the system configuration parameter CHILD_MAX.  When[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              this limit is reached, older process IDs  are  discarded,  least  recently[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              started processes first.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note  there  is no protection against the process ID wrapping, i.e. if the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              wait is not executed soon enough there is a chance the process waited  for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is the wrong one.  A conflict implies both process IDs have been generated[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              by  the  shell,  as other processes are not recorded, and that the user is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              potentially interested in both, so this problem is  intrinsic  to  process[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              IDs.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       whence [ -vcwfpamsS ] [ -x num ] name ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              For  each  name, indicate how it would be interpreted if used as a command[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              name.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If name is not an alias, built-in command, external command,  shell  func‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion,  hashed  command,  or  a  reserved  word,  the  exit status shall be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              non-zero, and -- if -v, -c, or -w was passed -- a message will be  written[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to  standard output.  (This is different from other shells that write that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              message to standard error.)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              whence is most useful when name is only the last path component of a  com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mand,  i.e.  does  not include a `/'; in particular, pattern matching only[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              succeeds if just the non-directory component of the command is passed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -v     Produce a more verbose report.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -c     Print the results in a csh-like format.  This takes precedence over[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     -v.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -w     For each name, print `name: word'  where  word  is  one  of  alias,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     builtin,  command, function, hashed, reserved or none, according as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     name corresponds to an alias, a built-in command, an external  com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     mand,  a shell function, a command defined with the hash builtin, a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     reserved word, or is not recognised.  This takes precedence over -v[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     and -c.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -f     Causes the contents of a shell  function  to  be  displayed,  which[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     would otherwise not happen unless the -c flag were used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -p     Do  a  path  search for name even if it is an alias, reserved word,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     shell function or builtin.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -a     Do a search for all occurrences  of  name  throughout  the  command[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     path.  Normally only the first occurrence is printed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -m     The  arguments  are taken as patterns (pattern characters should be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     quoted), and the information is displayed for each command matching[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     one of these patterns.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -s     If a pathname contains symlinks, print the symlink-free pathname as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     well.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -S     As -s, but if the pathname had to be resolved by following multiple[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     symlinks, the intermediate steps are printed, too.  The symlink re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     solved at each step might be anywhere in the path.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -x num Expand tabs when outputting shell functions using  the  -c  option.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     This has the same effect as the -x option to the functions builtin.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       where [ -wpmsS ] [ -x num ] name ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Equivalent to whence -ca.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       which [ -wpamsS ] [ -x num ] name ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Equivalent to whence -c.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zcompile [ -U ] [ -z | -k ] [ -R | -M ] file [ name ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zcompile -ca [ -m ] [ -R | -M ] file [ name ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zcompile -t file [ name ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  builtin command can be used to compile functions or scripts, storing[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the compiled form in a file, and to examine files containing the  compiled[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              form.  This allows faster autoloading of functions and sourcing of scripts[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              by avoiding parsing of the text when the files are read.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The first form (without the -c, -a or -t options) creates a compiled file.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  only  the  file  argument  is  given,  the  output  file  has the name[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `file.zwc' and will be placed in the same  directory  as  the  file.   The[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              shell will load the compiled file instead of the normal function file when[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  function  is  autoloaded;  see the section `Autoloading Functions' in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zshmisc(1) for a description of how  autoloaded  functions  are  searched.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The extension .zwc stands for `zsh word code'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  there  is at least one name argument, all the named files are compiled[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              into the output file given as the first argument.  If file does not end in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              .zwc, this extension is automatically appended.  Files containing multiple[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              compiled functions are called `digest' files, and are intended to be  used[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              as elements of the FPATH/fpath special array.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The  second  form,  with the -c or -a options, writes the compiled defini‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tions for all the named functions into file.  For -c, the  names  must  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              functions  currently  defined in the shell, not those marked for autoload‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ing.  Undefined functions that are marked for autoloading may  be  written[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              by  using  the -a option, in which case the fpath is searched and the con‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tents of the definition files for those functions, if found, are  compiled[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              into  file.   If both -c and -a are given, names of both defined functions[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and functions marked for autoloading may be given.  In  either  case,  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              functions  in files written with the -c or -a option will be autoloaded as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              if the KSH_AUTOLOAD option were unset.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The reason for handling loaded and not-yet-loaded functions with different[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              options is that some definition  files  for  autoloading  define  multiple[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              functions,  including the function with the same name as the file, and, at[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the end, call that function.  In such cases the output  of  `zcompile  -c'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              does  not  include  the  additional functions defined in the file, and any[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              other initialization code in the file is lost.  Using `zcompile  -a'  cap‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tures all this extra information.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If the -m option is combined with -c or -a, the names are used as patterns[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and all functions whose names match one of these patterns will be written.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If no name is given, the definitions of all functions currently defined or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              marked as autoloaded will be written.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note  the  second form cannot be used for compiling functions that include[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              redirections as part of the definition rather than within the body of  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              function; for example[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     fn1() { { ... } >~/logfile }[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              can be compiled but[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     fn1() { ... } >~/logfile[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              cannot.   It  is possible to use the first form of zcompile to compile au‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              toloadable functions that include the full function definition instead  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              just the body of the function.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The  third  form,  with the -t option, examines an existing compiled file.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Without further arguments, the names of the original files  compiled  into[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              it  are  listed.   The first line of output shows the version of the shell[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              which compiled the file and how the file will be used (i.e. by reading  it[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              directly or by mapping it into memory).  With arguments, nothing is output[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and  the  return  status  is set to zero if definitions for all names were[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              found in the compiled file, and non-zero if the definition  for  at  least[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              one name was not found.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Other options:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -U     Aliases are not expanded when compiling the named files.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -R     When  the  compiled  file is read, its contents are copied into the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     shell's memory, rather than memory-mapped (see -M).   This  happens[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     automatically on systems that do not support memory mapping.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     When compiling scripts instead of autoloadable functions, it is of‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ten desirable to use this option; otherwise the whole file, includ‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ing  the  code to define functions which have already been defined,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     will remain mapped, consequently wasting memory.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -M     The compiled file is mapped into the shell's memory when read. This[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     is done in such a way that multiple instances of the shell  running[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     on the same host will share this mapped file.  If neither -R nor -M[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     is given, the zcompile builtin decides what to do based on the size[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     of the compiled file.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -k[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -z     These  options  are  used when the compiled file contains functions[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     which are to be autoloaded. If -z is given, the  function  will  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     autoloaded  as if the KSH_AUTOLOAD option is not set, even if it is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     set at the time the compiled file is  read,  while  if  the  -k  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     given,  the  function  will  be  loaded  as if KSH_AUTOLOAD is set.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     These options also take precedence over any -k or -z options speci‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     fied to the autoload builtin. If neither of these options is given,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the function will be loaded as determined by  the  setting  of  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     KSH_AUTOLOAD option at the time the compiled file is read.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     These  options  may  also appear as many times as necessary between[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the listed names to specify the  loading  style  of  all  following[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     functions, up to the next -k or -z.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     The  created file always contains two versions of the compiled for‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     mat, one for big-endian machines and one for small-endian machines.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     The upshot of this is that the compiled file is machine independent[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     and if it is read or mapped, only one half of the file is  actually[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     used (and mapped).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zformat[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              See the section `The zsh/zutil Module' in zshmodules(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zftp   See the section `The zsh/zftp Module' in zshmodules(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zle    See the section `Zle Builtins' in zshzle(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zmodload [ -dL ] [ -s ] [ ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zmodload -F [ -alLme -P param ] module [ [+-]feature ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zmodload -e [ -A ] [ ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zmodload [ -a [ -bcpf [ -I ] ] ] [ -iL ] ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zmodload -u [ -abcdpf [ -I ] ] [ -iL ] ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zmodload -A [ -L ] [ modalias[=module] ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zmodload -R modalias ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Performs  operations  relating to zsh's loadable modules.  Loading of mod‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ules while the shell is running (`dynamical loading') is not available  on[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              all  operating  systems, or on all installations on a particular operating[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              system, although the zmodload command itself is always available  and  can[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              be  used to manipulate modules built into versions of the shell executable[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              without dynamical loading.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Without arguments the names of all currently  loaded  binary  modules  are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              printed.   The -L option causes this list to be in the form of a series of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload commands.  Forms with arguments are:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload [ -is ] name ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload -u [ -i ] name ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     In the simplest case, zmodload loads a binary module.   The  module[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     must be in a file with a name consisting of the specified name fol‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     lowed  by a standard suffix, usually `.so' (`.sl' on HPUX).  If the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     module to be loaded is already loaded the duplicate module  is  ig‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     nored.   If  zmodload  detects an inconsistency, such as an invalid[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     module name or circular dependency list, the current code block  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     aborted.   If  it  is available, the module is loaded if necessary,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     while if it is not available, non-zero status is silently returned.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     The option -i is accepted for compatibility but has no effect.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     The named module is searched for in the same way a command is,  us‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ing  $module_path  instead  of  $path.  However, the path search is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     performed even when the module name contains a `/', which  it  usu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ally does.  There is no way to prevent the path search.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     If  the module supports features (see below), zmodload tries to en‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     able all features when loading a module.  If the  module  was  suc‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     cessfully  loaded  but  not all features could be enabled, zmodload[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     returns status 2.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     If the option -s is given, no error is printed if  the  module  was[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     not  available  (though  other errors indicating a problem with the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     module are printed).  The return status indicates if the module was[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     loaded.  This is appropriate if the caller considers the module op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     tional.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     With -u, zmodload unloads modules.  The same  name  must  be  given[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     that  was given when the module was loaded, but it is not necessary[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     for the module to exist in the file system.   The  -i  option  sup‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     presses  the  error if the module is already unloaded (or was never[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     loaded).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     Each module has a boot and a cleanup function.  The module will not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     be loaded if its boot function fails.  Similarly a module can  only[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     be unloaded if its cleanup function runs successfully.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload -F [ -almLe -P param ] module [ [+-]feature ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     zmodload  -F  allows  more selective control over the features pro‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     vided by modules.  With no options apart from -F, the module  named[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     module  is  loaded,  if  it was not already loaded, and the list of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     features is set to the required state.  If no features  are  speci‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     fied,  the  module is loaded, if it was not already loaded, but the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     state of features is unchanged.  Each feature may be preceded by  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     +  to turn the feature on, or - to turn it off; the + is assumed if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     neither character is present.  Any feature not explicitly mentioned[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     is left in its current state; if  the  module  was  not  previously[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     loaded  this means any such features will remain disabled.  The re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     turn status is zero if all features  were  set,  1  if  the  module[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     failed  to load, and 2 if some features could not be set (for exam‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ple, a parameter couldn't be added because there  was  a  different[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     parameter of the same name) but the module was loaded.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     The standard features are builtins, conditions, parameters and math[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     functions;  these  are indicated by the prefix `b:', `c:' (`C:' for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     an infix condition), `p:' and `f:', respectively, followed  by  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     name  that  the corresponding feature would have in the shell.  For[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     example,  `b:strftime'  indicates  a  builtin  named  strftime  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     p:EPOCHSECONDS  indicates a parameter named EPOCHSECONDS.  The mod‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ule may provide other (`abstract') features of its own as indicated[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     by its documentation; these have no prefix.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     With -l or -L, features provided by the module are listed.  With -l[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     alone, a list of features together with their states is shown,  one[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     feature  per line.  With -L alone, a zmodload -F command that would[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     cause enabled features of the module to  be  turned  on  is  shown.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     With  -lL,  a zmodload -F command that would cause all the features[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     to be set to their current state is shown.  If one of these  combi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     nations  is given with the option -P param then the parameter param[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     is set to an array of features, either features together with their[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     state or (if -L alone is given) enabled features.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     With the option -L the module name may be omitted; then a  list  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     all  enabled features for all modules providing features is printed[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     in the form of zmodload -F commands.  If  -l  is  also  given,  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     state of both enabled and disabled features is output in that form.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     A set of features may be provided together with -l or -L and a mod‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ule  name; in that case only the state of those features is consid‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ered.  Each feature may be preceded by + or - but the character has[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     no effect.  If no set of features is  provided,  all  features  are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     considered.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     With  -e,  the command first tests that the module is loaded; if it[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     is not, status 1 is returned.  If the module is loaded, the list of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     features given as an argument is examined.  Any feature given  with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     no  prefix  is  simply tested to see if the module provides it; any[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     feature given with a prefix + or - is tested to see if is  provided[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     and  in  the given state.  If the tests on all features in the list[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     succeed, status 0 is returned, else status 1.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     With -m, each entry in the given list of features  is  taken  as  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     pattern  to be matched against the list of features provided by the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     module.  An initial + or - must be given explicitly.  This may  not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     be  combined  with the -a option as autoloads must be specified ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     plicitly.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     With -a, the given list of features is marked for autoload from the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     specified module, which may not yet be loaded.  An optional  +  may[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     appear before the feature name.  If the feature is prefixed with -,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     any  existing  autoload  is  removed.  The options -l and -L may be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     used to list autoloads.  Autoloading is specific to individual fea‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     tures; when the module is loaded only the requested feature is  en‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     abled.   Autoload  requests  are  preserved if the module is subse‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     quently unloaded until an explicit `zmodload -Fa  module  -feature'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     is issued.  It is not an error to request an autoload for a feature[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     of a module that is already loaded.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     When the module is loaded each autoload is checked against the fea‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     tures  actually  provided by the module; if the feature is not pro‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     vided the autoload request is deleted.  A warning message  is  out‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     put;  if the module is being loaded to provide a different feature,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     and that autoload is successful, there is no effect on  the  status[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     of  the  current  command.   If the module is already loaded at the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     time when zmodload -Fa is run, an error message is printed and sta‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     tus 1 returned.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     zmodload -Fa can be used with the -l, -L, -e  and  -P  options  for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     listing  and  testing  the  existence of autoloadable features.  In[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     this case -l is ignored if -L is specified.  zmodload -FaL with  no[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     module name lists autoloads for all modules.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     Note  that  only  standard  features  as described above can be au‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     toloaded; other features require the module to be loaded before en‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     abling.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload -d [ -L ] [ name ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload -d name dep ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload -ud name [ dep ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     The -d option can be used to specify module dependencies.  The mod‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ules named in the second and subsequent arguments  will  be  loaded[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     before the module named in the first argument.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     With  -d  and  one  argument,  all dependencies for that module are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     listed.  With -d and no  arguments,  all  module  dependencies  are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     listed.  This listing is by default in a Makefile-like format.  The[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     -L option changes this format to a list of zmodload -d commands.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     If  -d and -u are both used, dependencies are removed.  If only one[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     argument is given, all dependencies for that module are removed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload -ab [ -L ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload -ab [ -i ] name [ builtin ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload -ub [ -i ] builtin ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     The -ab option defines autoloaded builtins.  It defines the  speci‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     fied  builtins.   When  any of those builtins is called, the module[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     specified in the first argument is loaded and all its features  are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     enabled  (for selective control of features use `zmodload -F -a' as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     described above).  If only the name is given, one  builtin  is  de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     fined,  with  the same name as the module.  -i suppresses the error[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     if the builtin is already defined or autoloaded, but not if another[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     builtin of the same name is already defined.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     With -ab and no arguments, all autoloaded builtins are listed, with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the module name (if  different)  shown  in  parentheses  after  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     builtin name.  The -L option changes this format to a list of zmod‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     load -a commands.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     If -b is used together with the -u option, it removes builtins pre‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     viously  defined with -ab.  This is only possible if the builtin is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     not yet loaded.  -i suppresses the error if the builtin is  already[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     removed (or never existed).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     Autoload  requests  are  retained if the module is subsequently un‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     loaded until an explicit `zmodload -ub builtin' is issued.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload -ac [ -IL ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload -ac [ -iI ] name [ cond ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload -uc [ -iI ] cond ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     The -ac option is used to define autoloaded  condition  codes.  The[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     cond  strings  give the names of the conditions defined by the mod‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ule. The optional -I option  is  used  to  define  infix  condition[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     names. Without this option prefix condition names are defined.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     If given no condition names, all defined names are listed (as a se‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ries of zmodload commands if the -L option is given).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     The -uc option removes definitions for autoloaded conditions.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload -ap [ -L ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload -ap [ -i ] name [ parameter ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload -up [ -i ] parameter ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     The  -p  option  is  like the -b and -c options, but makes zmodload[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     work on autoloaded parameters instead.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload -af [ -L ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload -af [ -i ] name [ function ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload -uf [ -i ] function ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     The -f option is like the -b, -p, and -c options, but  makes  zmod‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     load work on autoloaded math functions instead.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload -a [ -L ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload -a [ -i ] name [ builtin ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload -ua [ -i ] builtin ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Equivalent to -ab and -ub.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload -e [ -A ] [ string ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     The -e option without arguments lists all loaded modules; if the -A[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     option  is  also given, module aliases corresponding to loaded mod‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ules are  also  shown.   If  arguments  are  provided,  nothing  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     printed;  the  return status is set to zero if all strings given as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     arguments are names of loaded modules and to one  if  at  least  on[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     string  is  not  the  name of a loaded module.  This can be used to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     test for the availability of things  implemented  by  modules.   In[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     this  case,  any aliases are automatically resolved and the -A flag[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     is not used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload -A [ -L ] [ modalias[=module] ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     For each argument, if both modalias and module  are  given,  define[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     modalias  to  be  an  alias  for  the module module.  If the module[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     modalias is ever subsequently requested, either via a call to zmod‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     load or implicitly, the shell will attempt to load module  instead.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     If module is not given, show the definition of modalias.  If no ar‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     guments  are given, list all defined module aliases.  When listing,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     if the -L flag was also given, list the definition  as  a  zmodload[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     command to recreate the alias.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     The  existence  of aliases for modules is completely independent of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     whether the name resolved is actually loaded as a module: while the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     alias exists, loading and unloading the module under any alias  has[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     exactly  the  same  effect as using the resolved name, and does not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     affect the connection between the alias and the resolved name which[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     can be removed either by zmodload -R or by  redefining  the  alias.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Chains  of aliases (i.e. where the first resolved name is itself an[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     alias) are valid so long as these are not circular.  As the aliases[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     take the same format as module names, they may include path separa‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     tors:  in this case, there is no requirement for any  part  of  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     path named to exist as the alias will be resolved first.  For exam‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ple, `any/old/alias' is always a valid alias.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     Dependencies added to aliased modules are actually added to the re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     solved  module;  these remain if the alias is removed.  It is valid[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     to create an alias whose name is one of the standard shell  modules[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     and which resolves to a different module.  However, if a module has[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     dependencies,  it will not be possible to use the module name as an[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     alias as the module will already be marked as a loadable module  in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     its own right.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     Apart  from  the above, aliases can be used in the zmodload command[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     anywhere module names are required.  However, aliases will  not  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     shown in lists of loaded modules with a bare `zmodload'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              zmodload -R modalias ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     For  each modalias argument that was previously defined as a module[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     alias via zmodload -A, delete the alias.  If any was  not  defined,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     an error is caused and the remainder of the line is ignored.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note  that  zsh makes no distinction between modules that were linked into[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the shell and modules that are loaded  dynamically.  In  both  cases  this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              builtin  command  has  to be used to make available the builtins and other[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              things defined by modules (unless the module is autoloaded on these defin‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              itions). This is true even for systems that don't support dynamic  loading[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of modules.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zparseopts[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              See the section `The zsh/zutil Module' in zshmodules(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zprof  See the section `The zsh/zprof Module' in zshmodules(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zpty   See the section `The zsh/zpty Module' in zshmodules(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zregexparse[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              See the section `The zsh/zutil Module' in zshmodules(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsocket[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              See the section `The zsh/net/socket Module' in zshmodules(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zstyle See the section `The zsh/zutil Module' in zshmodules(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ztcp   See the section `The zsh/net/tcp Module' in zshmodules(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mzsh 5.9                               May 14, 2022                        ZSHBUILTINS(1)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────[0m
[38;2;248;248;242mZSHZLE(1)                        General Commands Manual                       ZSHZLE(1)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mNAME[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zshzle - zsh command line editor[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mDESCRIPTION[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       If  the  ZLE option is set (which it is by default in interactive shells) and the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       shell input is attached to the terminal, the user is able to edit command lines.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       There are two display modes.  The first, multiline mode, is the default.  It only[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       works if the TERM parameter is set to a valid terminal type  that  can  move  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       cursor up.  The second, single line mode, is used if TERM is invalid or incapable[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       of  moving  the cursor up, or if the SINGLE_LINE_ZLE option is set.  This mode is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       similar to ksh, and uses no termcap sequences.  If TERM is "emacs", the  ZLE  op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tion will be unset by default.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The parameters BAUD, COLUMNS, and LINES are also used by the line editor. See Pa‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       rameters Used By The Shell in zshparam(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The  parameter zle_highlight is also used by the line editor; see Character High‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       lighting below.  Highlighting of special characters and the  region  between  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       cursor  and  the  mark  (as  set  with  set-mark-command in Emacs mode, or by vi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       sual-mode in Vi mode) is enabled by default; consult this reference for more  in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       formation.   Irascible  conservatives will wish to know that all highlighting may[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       be disabled by the following setting:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              zle_highlight=(none)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       In many places, references are made to the numeric argument.  This can by default[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       be entered in emacs mode by holding the alt key and typing a number, or  pressing[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       escape  before each digit, and in vi command mode by typing the number before en‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tering a command.  Generally the numeric argument causes the next command entered[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       to be repeated the specified number of times, unless otherwise noted below;  this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       is implemented by the digit-argument widget. See also the Arguments subsection of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the Widgets section for some other ways the numeric argument can be modified.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mKEYMAPS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       A  keymap  in  ZLE  contains a set of bindings between key sequences and ZLE com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       mands.  The empty key sequence cannot be bound.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       There can be any number of keymaps at any time, and each keymap has one  or  more[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       names.   If  all  of a keymap's names are deleted, it disappears.  bindkey can be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       used to manipulate keymap names.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Initially, there are eight keymaps:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       emacs  EMACS emulation[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       viins  vi emulation - insert mode[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       vicmd  vi emulation - command mode[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       viopp  vi emulation - operator pending[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       visual vi emulation - selection active[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       isearch[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              incremental search mode[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       command[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              read a command name[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       .safe  fallback keymap[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The `.safe' keymap is special.  It can never be altered, and the name  can  never[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       be  removed.  However, it can be linked to other names, which can be removed.  In[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the future other special keymaps may be added; users should avoid using names be‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ginning with `.' for their own keymaps.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       In addition to these names, either `emacs' or `viins' is also linked to the  name[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       `main'.   If one of the VISUAL or EDITOR environment variables contain the string[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       `vi' when the shell starts up then it will  be  `viins',  otherwise  it  will  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       `emacs'.   bindkey's  -e and -v options provide a convenient way to override this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       default choice.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       When the editor starts up, it will select the  `main'  keymap.   If  that  keymap[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       doesn't exist, it will use `.safe' instead.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       In  the  `.safe'  keymap,  each single key is bound to self-insert, except for ^J[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       (line feed) and ^M (return) which are bound to accept-line.  This is deliberately[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       not pleasant to use; if you are using it, it means you deleted the  main  keymap,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       and you should put it back.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Reading Commands[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       When  ZLE  is reading a command from the terminal, it may read a sequence that is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       bound to some command and is also a prefix of a longer  bound  string.   In  this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       case ZLE will wait a certain time to see if more characters are typed, and if not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       (or  they don't match any longer string) it will execute the binding.  This time‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       out is defined by the KEYTIMEOUT parameter; its default is 0.4 sec.  There is  no[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       timeout if the prefix string is not itself bound to a command.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The  key  timeout  is also applied when ZLE is reading the bytes from a multibyte[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       character string when it is in the appropriate mode.   (This  requires  that  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       shell  was  compiled  with  multibyte mode enabled; typically also the locale has[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       characters with the UTF-8 encoding, although any multibyte encoding known to  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       operating  system  is supported.)  If the second or a subsequent byte is not read[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       within the timeout period, the shell acts as if ? were typed and resets the input[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       state.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       As well as ZLE commands, key sequences can be bound to other  strings,  by  using[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       `bindkey  -s'.   When  such  a sequence is read, the replacement string is pushed[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       back as input, and the command reading process starts again using these fake key‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       strokes.  This input can itself invoke further replacement strings, but in  order[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       to detect loops the process will be stopped if there are twenty such replacements[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       without a real command being read.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       A  key  sequence  typed  by the user can be turned into a command name for use in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       user-defined widgets with the read-command widget, described  in  the  subsection[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       `Miscellaneous' of the section `Standard Widgets' below.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Local Keymaps[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       While for normal editing a single keymap is used exclusively, in many modes a lo‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       cal  keymap allows for some keys to be customised. For example, in an incremental[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       search mode, a binding in the isearch keymap will override a binding in the  main[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       keymap but all keys that are not overridden can still be used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       If  a  key  sequence is defined in a local keymap, it will hide a key sequence in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the global keymap that is a prefix of that sequence. An example  of  this  occurs[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       with the binding of iw in viopp as this hides the binding of i in vicmd. However,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       a  longer sequence in the global keymap that shares the same prefix can still ap‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ply so for example the binding of ^Xa in the global keymap will be unaffected  by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the binding of ^Xb in the local keymap.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mZLE BUILTINS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The  ZLE  module contains three related builtin commands. The bindkey command ma‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       nipulates keymaps and key bindings; the vared command invokes ZLE on the value of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       a shell parameter; and the zle command manipulates  editing  widgets  and  allows[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       command line access to ZLE commands from within shell functions.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       bindkey [ options ] -l [ -L ] [ keymap ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       bindkey [ options ] -d[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       bindkey [ options ] -D keymap ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       bindkey [ options ] -A old-keymap new-keymap[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       bindkey [ options ] -N new-keymap [ old-keymap ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       bindkey [ options ] -m[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       bindkey [ options ] -r in-string ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       bindkey [ options ] -s in-string out-string ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       bindkey [ options ] in-string command ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       bindkey [ options ] [ in-string ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              bindkey's  options  can be divided into three categories: keymap selection[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for the current command, operation selection, and others.  The keymap  se‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              lection options are:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -e     Selects  keymap  `emacs' for any operations by the current command,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     and also links `emacs' to `main' so that it is selected by  default[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the next time the editor starts.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -v     Selects  keymap  `viins' for any operations by the current command,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     and also links `viins' to `main' so that it is selected by  default[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the next time the editor starts.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -a     Selects keymap `vicmd' for any operations by the current command.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -M keymap[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     The  keymap specifies a keymap name that is selected for any opera‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     tions by the current command.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If a keymap selection is required and none of the options above are  used,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  `main'  keymap is used.  Some operations do not permit a keymap to be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              selected, namely:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -l     List all existing keymap names; if any arguments  are  given,  list[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     just those keymaps.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     If the -L option is also used, list in the form of bindkey commands[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     to  create  or  link  the  keymaps.  `bindkey -lL main' shows which[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     keymap is linked to `main', if any, and hence if the standard emacs[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     or vi emulation is in effect.  This option does not show the  .safe[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     keymap  because it cannot be created in that fashion; however, nei‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     ther is `bindkey -lL .safe' reported as an error, it simply outputs[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     nothing.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -d     Delete all existing keymaps and reset to the default state.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -D keymap ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Delete the named keymaps.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -A old-keymap new-keymap[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Make the new-keymap name an alias  for  old-keymap,  so  that  both[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     names  refer to the same keymap.  The names have equal standing; if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     either is deleted, the other remains.  If there is already a keymap[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     with the new-keymap name, it is deleted.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -N new-keymap [ old-keymap ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Create a new keymap, named new-keymap.  If  a  keymap  already  has[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     that  name, it is deleted.  If an old-keymap name is given, the new[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     keymap is initialized to be a duplicate of it,  otherwise  the  new[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     keymap will be empty.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              To use a newly created keymap, it should be linked to main.  Hence the se‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              quence of commands to create and use a new keymap `mymap' initialized from[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the emacs keymap (which remains unchanged) is:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     bindkey -N mymap emacs[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     bindkey -A mymap main[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that while `bindkey -A newmap main' will work when newmap is emacs or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              viins,  it will not work for vicmd, as switching from vi insert to command[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mode becomes impossible.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The following operations act on the `main' keymap if no  keymap  selection[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              option was given:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -m     Add  the  built-in set of meta-key bindings to the selected keymap.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Only keys that are unbound or bound to self-insert are affected.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -r in-string ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Unbind the specified in-strings in the selected  keymap.   This  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     exactly equivalent to binding the strings to undefined-key.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     When -R is also used, interpret the in-strings as ranges.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     When -p is also used, the in-strings specify prefixes.  Any binding[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     that has the given in-string as a prefix, not including the binding[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     for the in-string itself, if any, will be removed.  For example,[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                            bindkey -rpM viins '^['[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     will  remove all bindings in the vi-insert keymap beginning with an[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     escape character (probably cursor keys), but leave the binding  for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the escape character itself (probably vi-cmd-mode).  This is incom‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     patible with the option -R.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              -s in-string out-string ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Bind  each  in-string to each out-string.  When in-string is typed,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     out-string will be pushed back and treated as input to the line ed‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     itor.  When -R is also used, interpret the in-strings as ranges.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     Note that both in-string and out-string are  subject  to  the  same[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     form of interpretation, as described below.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              in-string command ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Bind  each  in-string  to each command.  When -R is used, interpret[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the in-strings as ranges.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              [ in-string ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     List key bindings.  If an in-string is specified,  the  binding  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     that  string  in  the selected keymap is displayed.  Otherwise, all[0m
