[38;2;248;248;242m              ward-delete-char,  vi-backward-delete-char,  backward-kill-word,  vi-back‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ward-kill-word, clear-screen, redisplay, quoted-insert  and  vi-quoted-in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sert.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              vi-cmd-mode is treated the same as accept-line, and magic-space is treated[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              as  a  space.   Any  other  character  that is not bound to self-insert or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              self-insert-unmeta will beep and be ignored. If  the  function  is  called[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              from  vi  command  mode,  the  bindings of the current insert mode will be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If called from a function by the zle command with arguments, the first ar‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              gument is taken as the string for which to search, rather than  the  first[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              word in the buffer.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       history-search-forward (ESC-N ESC-n) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Search  forward in the history for a line beginning with the first word in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the buffer.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If called from a function by the zle command with arguments, the first ar‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              gument is taken as the string for which to search, rather than  the  first[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              word in the buffer.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-history-search-forward (unbound) (?) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Search  forward in the history for a specified string.  The string may be‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              gin with `^' to anchor the search to the beginning of the line. The  func‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tions   available   in  the  mini-buffer  are  the  same  as  for  vi-his‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tory-search-backward.  Argument handling is also the same as for that com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mand.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       infer-next-history (^X^N) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Search in the history list for a line matching the current one  and  fetch[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the event following it.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       insert-last-word (ESC-_ ESC-.) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Insert  the  last word from the previous history event at the cursor posi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion.  If a positive numeric argument is given, insert that word from  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              end  of  the  previous history event.  If the argument is zero or negative[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              insert that word from the left (zero inserts the previous  command  word).[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Repeating  this command replaces the word just inserted with the last word[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              from the history event prior to the one just used; numeric  arguments  can[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              be used in the same way to pick a word from that event.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              When  called from a shell function invoked from a user-defined widget, the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              command can take one to three arguments.  The first argument  specifies  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              history  offset which applies to successive calls to this widget: if it is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -1, the default behaviour is used, while if it is 1, successive calls will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              move forwards through the history.  The value 0 can be  used  to  indicate[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that  the  history  line examined by the previous execution of the command[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              will be reexamined.  Note that negative numbers should be  preceded  by  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `--' argument to avoid confusing them with options.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  two  arguments are given, the second specifies the word on the command[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              line in normal array index notation (as a more natural alternative to  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              numeric argument).  Hence 1 is the first word, and -1 (the default) is the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              last word.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If a third argument is given, its value is ignored, but it is used to sig‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              nify  that  the  history  offset  is relative to the current history line,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rather than the one remembered  after  the  previous  invocations  of  in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sert-last-word.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              For example, the default behaviour of the command corresponds to[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     zle insert-last-word -- -1 -1[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              while the command[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     zle insert-last-word -- -1 1 -[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              always copies the first word of the line in the history immediately before[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the line being edited.  This has the side effect that later invocations of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the widget will be relative to that line.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-repeat-search (unbound) (n) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Repeat the last vi history search.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-rev-repeat-search (unbound) (N) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Repeat the last vi history search, but in reverse.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       up-line-or-history (^P ESC-[A) (k) (ESC-[A)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Move  up  a line in the buffer, or if already at the top line, move to the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              previous event in the history list.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-up-line-or-history (unbound) (-) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Move up a line in the buffer, or if already at the top line, move  to  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              previous  event  in  the  history  list.  Then move to the first non-blank[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              character on the line.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       up-line-or-search[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Move up a line in the buffer, or if already at the top line, search  back‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ward  in  the  history  for  a  line  beginning with the first word in the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              buffer.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If called from a function by the zle command with arguments, the first ar‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              gument is taken as the string for which to search, rather than  the  first[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              word in the buffer.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       up-history (unbound) (^P) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Move to the previous event in the history list.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       history-beginning-search-forward[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Search  forward  in the history for a line beginning with the current line[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              up to the cursor.  This leaves the cursor in its original position.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       set-local-history[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              By default, history movement commands visit the imported lines as well  as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  local  lines.  This widget lets you toggle this on and off, or set it[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              with the numeric argument. Zero for both  local  and  imported  lines  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              nonzero for only local lines.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Modifying Text[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-add-eol (unbound) (A) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Move to the end of the line and enter insert mode.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-add-next (unbound) (a) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Enter  insert  mode  after  the  current cursor position, without changing[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              lines.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       backward-delete-char (^H ^?) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Delete the character behind the cursor.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-backward-delete-char (unbound) (X) (^H)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Delete the character behind the cursor, without changing lines.  If in in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sert mode, this won't delete past the point where insert mode was last en‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tered.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       backward-delete-word[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Delete the word behind the cursor.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       backward-kill-line[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Kill from the beginning of the line to the cursor position.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       backward-kill-word (^W ESC-^H ESC-^?) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Kill the word behind the cursor.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-backward-kill-word (unbound) (unbound) (^W)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Kill the word behind the cursor, without going past the point where insert[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mode was last entered.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       capitalize-word (ESC-C ESC-c) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Capitalize the current word and move past it.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-change (unbound) (c) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Read a movement command from the keyboard, and kill from the cursor  posi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion  to  the  endpoint  of the movement.  Then enter insert mode.  If the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              command is vi-change, change the current line.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              For compatibility with vi, if the command is  vi-forward-word  or  vi-for‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ward-blank-word,  the  whitespace  after  the word is not included. If you[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              prefer the more consistent behaviour with the whitespace included use  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              following key binding:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     bindkey -a -s cw dwi[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-change-eol (unbound) (C) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Kill to the end of the line and enter insert mode.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-change-whole-line (unbound) (S) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Kill the current line and enter insert mode.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       copy-region-as-kill (ESC-W ESC-w) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Copy the area from the cursor to the mark to the kill buffer.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  called from a ZLE widget function in the form `zle copy-region-as-kill[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              string' then string will be taken as the text to copy to the kill  buffer.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The cursor, the mark and the text on the command line are not used in this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              case.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       copy-prev-word (ESC-^_) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Duplicate the word to the left of the cursor.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       copy-prev-shell-word[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Like copy-prev-word, but the word is found by using shell parsing, whereas[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              copy-prev-word  looks for blanks. This makes a difference when the word is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              quoted and contains spaces.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-delete (unbound) (d) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Read a movement command from the keyboard, and kill from the cursor  posi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion  to  the endpoint of the movement.  If the command is vi-delete, kill[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the current line.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       delete-char[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Delete the character under the cursor.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-delete-char (unbound) (x) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Delete the character under the cursor, without going past the end  of  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              line.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       delete-word[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Delete the current word.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       down-case-word (ESC-L ESC-l) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Convert the current word to all lowercase and move past it.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-down-case (unbound) (gu) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Read a movement command from the keyboard, and convert all characters from[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  cursor position to the endpoint of the movement to lowercase.  If the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              movement command is vi-down-case, swap the case of all characters  on  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              current line.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       kill-word (ESC-D ESC-d) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Kill the current word.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       gosmacs-transpose-chars[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Exchange the two characters behind the cursor.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-indent (unbound) (>) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Indent a number of lines.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-insert (unbound) (i) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Enter insert mode.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-insert-bol (unbound) (I) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Move to the first non-blank character on the line and enter insert mode.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-join (^X^J) (J) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Join the current line with the next one.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       kill-line (^K) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Kill from the cursor to the end of the line.  If already on the end of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              line, kill the newline character.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-kill-line (unbound) (unbound) (^U)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Kill from the cursor back to wherever insert mode was last entered.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-kill-eol (unbound) (D) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Kill from the cursor to the end of the line.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       kill-region[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Kill from the cursor to the mark.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       kill-buffer (^X^K) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Kill the entire buffer.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       kill-whole-line (^U) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Kill the current line.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-match-bracket (^X^B) (%) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Move  to  the bracket character (one of {}, () or []) that matches the one[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              under the cursor.  If the cursor is not on a bracket character, move  for‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ward  without  going  past the end of the line to find one, and then go to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the matching bracket.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-open-line-above (unbound) (O) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Open a line above the cursor and enter insert mode.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-open-line-below (unbound) (o) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Open a line below the cursor and enter insert mode.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-oper-swap-case (unbound) (g~) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Read a movement command from the keyboard, and swap the case of all  char‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              acters  from  the cursor position to the endpoint of the movement.  If the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              movement command is vi-oper-swap-case, swap the case of all characters  on[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the current line.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       overwrite-mode (^X^O) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Toggle between overwrite mode and insert mode.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-put-before (unbound) (P) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Insert  the  contents  of  the kill buffer before the cursor.  If the kill[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              buffer contains a sequence of lines (as opposed to characters),  paste  it[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              above the current line.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-put-after (unbound) (p) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Insert  the  contents  of  the  kill buffer after the cursor.  If the kill[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              buffer contains a sequence of lines (as opposed to characters),  paste  it[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              below the current line.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       put-replace-selection (unbound) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Replace  the contents of the current region or selection with the contents[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of the kill buffer. If the kill buffer contains a sequence  of  lines  (as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              opposed  to  characters),  the  current  line  will be split by the pasted[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              lines.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       quoted-insert (^V) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Insert the next character typed into the buffer literally.   An  interrupt[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              character will not be inserted.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-quoted-insert (unbound) (unbound) (^Q ^V)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Display  a `^' at the cursor position, and insert the next character typed[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              into the buffer literally.  An interrupt character will not be inserted.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       quote-line (ESC-') (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Quote the current line; that is, put a `'' character at the beginning  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the end, and convert all `'' characters to `'\'''.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       quote-region (ESC-") (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Quote the region from the cursor to the mark.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-replace (unbound) (R) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Enter overwrite mode.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-repeat-change (unbound) (.) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Repeat  the  last vi mode text modification.  If a count was used with the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              modification, it is remembered.  If a count is given to this  command,  it[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              overrides  the remembered count, and is remembered for future uses of this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              command.  The cut buffer specification is similarly remembered.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-replace-chars (unbound) (r) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Replace the character under the cursor with a character read from the key‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              board.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       self-insert (printable characters) (unbound) (printable characters and some con‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       trol characters)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Insert a character into the buffer at the cursor position.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       self-insert-unmeta (ESC-^I ESC-^J ESC-^M) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Insert a character into the buffer after stripping the meta bit  and  con‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              verting ^M to ^J.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-substitute (unbound) (s) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Substitute the next character(s).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-swap-case (unbound) (~) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Swap the case of the character under the cursor and move past it.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       transpose-chars (^T) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Exchange  the  two characters to the left of the cursor if at end of line,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              else exchange the character under the cursor with  the  character  to  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              left.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       transpose-words (ESC-T ESC-t) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Exchange the current word with the one before it.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              With a positive numeric argument N, the word around the cursor, or follow‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ing  it if the cursor is between words, is transposed with the preceding N[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              words.  The cursor is put at the end of the resulting group of words.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              With a negative numeric argument -N, the effect is the  same  as  using  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              positive  argument N except that the original cursor position is retained,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              regardless of how the words are rearranged.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-unindent (unbound) (<) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Unindent a number of lines.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-up-case (unbound) (gU) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Read a movement command from the keyboard, and convert all characters from[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the cursor position to the endpoint of the movement to lowercase.  If  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              movement  command  is  vi-up-case,  swap the case of all characters on the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              current line.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       up-case-word (ESC-U ESC-u) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Convert the current word to all caps and move past it.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       yank (^Y) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Insert the contents of the kill buffer at the cursor position.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       yank-pop (ESC-y) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Remove the text just yanked, rotate the kill-ring (the history  of  previ‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ously  killed  text)  and  yank  the  new top.  Only works following yank,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              vi-put-before, vi-put-after or yank-pop.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-yank (unbound) (y) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Read a movement command from the keyboard, and copy the  region  from  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cursor  position to the endpoint of the movement into the kill buffer.  If[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the command is vi-yank, copy the current line.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-yank-whole-line (unbound) (Y) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Copy the current line into the kill buffer.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-yank-eol[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Copy the region from the cursor position to the end of the line  into  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              kill  buffer.  Arguably, this is what Y should do in vi, but it isn't what[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              it actually does.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Arguments[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       digit-argument (ESC-0..ESC-9) (1-9) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Start a new numeric argument,  or  add  to  the  current  one.   See  also[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              vi-digit-or-beginning-of-line.  This only works if bound to a key sequence[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ending in a decimal digit.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Inside  a  widget function, a call to this function treats the last key of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the key sequence which called the widget as the digit.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       neg-argument (ESC--) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Changes the sign of the following argument.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       universal-argument[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Multiply the argument of the next command by 4.   Alternatively,  if  this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              command  is followed by an integer (positive or negative), use that as the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              argument for the next command.  Thus digits cannot be repeated using  this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              command.   For example, if this command occurs twice, followed immediately[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              by forward-char, move forward sixteen spaces; if instead it is followed by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -2, then forward-char, move backward two spaces.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Inside a widget function, if passed an argument, i.e. `zle universal-argu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ment num', the numeric argument will be set to num; this is equivalent  to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `NUMERIC=num'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       argument-base[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Use  the existing numeric argument as a numeric base, which must be in the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              range 2 to 36 inclusive.  Subsequent use  of  digit-argument  and  univer‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sal-argument  will  input  a  new numeric argument in the given base.  The[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              usual hexadecimal convention is used: the letter a or A corresponds to 10,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and so on.  Arguments in bases requiring digits from 10 upwards  are  more[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              conveniently  input with universal-argument, since ESC-a etc. are not usu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ally bound to digit-argument.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The function can be used with a command  argument  inside  a  user-defined[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              widget.   The following code sets the base to 16 and lets the user input a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              hexadecimal argument until a key out of the digit range is typed:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     zle argument-base 16[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     zle universal-argument[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Completion[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       accept-and-menu-complete[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              In a menu completion, insert the current completion into the  buffer,  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              advance to the next possible completion.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       complete-word[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Attempt completion on the current word.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       delete-char-or-list (^D) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Delete the character under the cursor.  If the cursor is at the end of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              line, list possible completions for the current word.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       expand-cmd-path[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Expand the current command to its full pathname.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       expand-or-complete (TAB) (unbound) (TAB)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Attempt  shell expansion on the current word.  If that fails, attempt com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pletion.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       expand-or-complete-prefix[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Attempt shell expansion on the current word up to cursor.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       expand-history (ESC-space ESC-!) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Perform history expansion on the edit buffer.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       expand-word (^X*) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Attempt shell expansion on the current word.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       list-choices (ESC-^D) (^D =) (^D)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              List possible completions for the current word.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       list-expand (^Xg ^XG) (^G) (^G)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              List the expansion of the current word.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       magic-space[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Perform history expansion and insert a space into the buffer.  This is in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tended to be bound to space.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       menu-complete[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Like  complete-word,  except  that  menu  completion  is  used.   See  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              MENU_COMPLETE option.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       menu-expand-or-complete[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Like expand-or-complete, except that menu completion is used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       reverse-menu-complete[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Perform menu completion, like menu-complete, except that if a menu comple‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion  is  already in progress, move to the previous completion rather than[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the next.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       end-of-list[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When a previous completion displayed a list below the prompt, this  widget[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              can be used to move the prompt below the list.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Miscellaneous[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       accept-and-hold (ESC-A ESC-a) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Push the contents of the buffer on the buffer stack and execute it.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       accept-and-infer-next-history[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Execute  the  contents  of the buffer.  Then search the history list for a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              line matching the current one and push the event following onto the buffer[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              stack.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       accept-line (^J ^M) (^J ^M) (^J ^M)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Finish editing the buffer.  Normally this causes the buffer to be executed[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              as a shell command.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       accept-line-and-down-history (^O) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Execute the current line, and push the next history event  on  the  buffer[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              stack.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       auto-suffix-remove[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  the previous action added a suffix (space, slash, etc.) to the word on[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the command line, remove it.  Otherwise do nothing.  Removing  the  suffix[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ends any active menu completion or menu selection.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              This  widget is intended to be called from user-defined widgets to enforce[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              a desired suffix-removal behavior.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       auto-suffix-retain[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If the previous action added a suffix (space, slash, etc.) to the word  on[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  command  line,  force it to be preserved.  Otherwise do nothing.  Re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              taining the suffix ends any active menu completion or menu selection.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              This widget is intended to be called from user-defined widgets to  enforce[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              a desired suffix-preservation behavior.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       beep   Beep, unless the BEEP option is unset.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       bracketed-paste (^[[200~) (^[[200~) (^[[200~)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This widget is invoked when text is pasted to the terminal emulator. It is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              not  intended  to  be  bound to actual keys but instead to the special se‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              quence generated by the terminal emulator when text is pasted.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              When invoked interactively, the pasted text is inserted to the buffer  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              placed  in  the  cutbuffer.  If a numeric argument is given, shell quoting[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              will be applied to the pasted text before it is inserted.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              When a named buffer is specified with vi-set-buffer ("x), the pasted  text[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is stored in that named buffer but not inserted.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              When  called  from a widget function as `bracketed-paste name`, the pasted[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              text is assigned to the variable name and no other processing is done.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              See also the zle_bracketed_paste parameter.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-cmd-mode (^X^V) (unbound) (^[)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Enter command mode; that is, select the  `vicmd'  keymap.   Yes,  this  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              bound by default in emacs mode.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-caps-lock-panic[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Hang until any lowercase key is pressed.  This is for vi users without the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mental capacity to keep track of their caps lock key (like the author).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       clear-screen (^L ESC-^L) (^L) (^L)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Clear the screen and redraw the prompt.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       deactivate-region[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Make the current region inactive. This disables vim-style visual selection[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mode if it is active.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       describe-key-briefly[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Reads a key sequence, then prints the function bound to that sequence.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       exchange-point-and-mark (^X^X) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Exchange  the  cursor position (point) with the position of the mark.  Un‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              less a negative numeric argument is given, the region  between  point  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mark  is activated so that it can be highlighted.  If a zero numeric argu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ment is given, the region is activated but point and mark are not swapped.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       execute-named-cmd (ESC-x) (:) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Read the name of an editor command and execute it.  Aliasing  this  widget[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              with  `zle -A' or replacing it with `zle -N' has no effect when interpret‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ing key bindings, but `zle execute-named-cmd' will invoke such an alias or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              replacement.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              A restricted set of editing functions is  available  in  the  mini-buffer.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Keys  are  looked up in the special command keymap, and if not found there[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              in the main keymap.  An interrupt signal, as defined by the stty  setting,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              will  abort the function.  Note that the following always perform the same[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              task within the executed-named-cmd environment and cannot be  replaced  by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              user  defined  widgets, nor can the set of functions be extended.  The al‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              lowed  functions   are:   backward-delete-char,   vi-backward-delete-char,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              clear-screen,    redisplay,    quoted-insert,    vi-quoted-insert,   back‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ward-kill-word,  vi-backward-kill-word,   kill-whole-line,   vi-kill-line,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              backward-kill-line,  list-choices, delete-char-or-list, complete-word, ac‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cept-line, expand-or-complete and expand-or-complete-prefix.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              kill-region kills the last word, and vi-cmd-mode is treated  the  same  as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              accept-line.   The  space and tab characters, if not bound to one of these[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              functions, will complete the name and then list the possibilities  if  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              AUTO_LIST  option  is  set.   Any  other  character  that  is not bound to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              self-insert or self-insert-unmeta will beep and be ignored.  The  bindings[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of the current insert mode will be used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Currently this command may not be redefined or called by name.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       execute-last-named-cmd (ESC-z) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Redo the last function executed with execute-named-cmd.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Like  execute-named-cmd,  this command may not be redefined, but it may be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              called by name.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       get-line (ESC-G ESC-g) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Pop the top line off the buffer stack and insert it at  the  cursor  posi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       pound-insert (unbound) (#) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  there is no # character at the beginning of the buffer, add one to the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              beginning of each line.  If there is one, remove a # from each  line  that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              has  one.   In either case, accept the current line.  The INTERACTIVE_COM‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              MENTS option must be set for this to have any usefulness.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-pound-insert[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If there is no # character at the beginning of the current line, add  one.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  there  is one, remove it.  The INTERACTIVE_COMMENTS option must be set[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for this to have any usefulness.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       push-input[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Push the entire current multiline construct onto the buffer stack and  re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              turn  to  the  top-level (PS1) prompt.  If the current parser construct is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              only a single line, this is exactly like push-line.  Next time the  editor[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              starts up or is popped with get-line, the construct will be popped off the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              top of the buffer stack and loaded into the editing buffer.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       push-line (^Q ESC-Q ESC-q) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Push  the current buffer onto the buffer stack and clear the buffer.  Next[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              time the editor starts up, the buffer will be popped off the  top  of  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              buffer stack and loaded into the editing buffer.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       push-line-or-edit[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              At  the  top-level  (PS1) prompt, equivalent to push-line.  At a secondary[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (PS2) prompt, move the entire current multiline construct into the  editor[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              buffer.  The latter is equivalent to push-input followed by get-line.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       read-command[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Only  useful  from  a user-defined widget.  A keystroke is read just as in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              normal operation, but instead of the command being executed  the  name  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the command that would be executed is stored in the shell parameter REPLY.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  can be used as the argument of a future zle command.  If the key se‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              quence is not bound, status 1 is returned; typically,  however,  REPLY  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              set to undefined-key to indicate a useless key sequence.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       recursive-edit[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Only  useful  from  a user-defined widget.  At this point in the function,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the editor regains control until one of the standard widgets  which  would[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              normally cause zle to exit (typically an accept-line caused by hitting the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              return  key)  is  executed.   Instead, control returns to the user-defined[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              widget.  The status returned is non-zero if the return was  caused  by  an[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              error,  but  the function still continues executing and hence may tidy up.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This makes it safe for the user-defined widget to alter the  command  line[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              or key bindings temporarily.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The following widget, caps-lock, serves as an example.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     self-insert-ucase() {[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                       LBUFFER+=${(U)KEYS[-1]}[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     }[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     integer stat[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     zle -N self-insert self-insert-ucase[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     zle -A caps-lock save-caps-lock[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     zle -A accept-line caps-lock[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     zle recursive-edit[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     stat=$?[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     zle -A .self-insert self-insert[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     zle -A save-caps-lock caps-lock[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     zle -D save-caps-lock[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     (( stat )) && zle send-break[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     return $stat[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              This  causes  typed  letters  to  be inserted capitalised until either ac‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cept-line (i.e. typically the return key) is typed or the caps-lock widget[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is invoked again; the later is handled by saving  the  old  definition  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              caps-lock  as  save-caps-lock and then rebinding it to invoke accept-line.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that an error from the recursive edit is detected as a  non-zero  re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              turn status and propagated by using the send-break widget.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       redisplay (unbound) (^R) (^R)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Redisplays the edit buffer.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       reset-prompt (unbound) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Force  the  prompts  on both the left and right of the screen to be re-ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              panded, then redisplay the edit buffer.  This reflects changes both to the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              prompt variables themselves and changes in the  expansion  of  the  values[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (for  example,  changes  in  time or directory, or changes to the value of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              variables referred to by the prompt).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Otherwise, the prompt is only expanded each time zle starts, and when  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              display  has  been  interrupted  by  output from another part of the shell[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (such as  a  job  notification)  which  causes  the  command  line  to  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              reprinted.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              reset-prompt doesn't alter the special parameter LASTWIDGET.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       send-break (^G ESC-^G) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Abort the current editor function, e.g. execute-named-command, or the edi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tor  itself,  e.g. if you are in vared. Otherwise abort the parsing of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              current line; in this case the aborted line  is  available  in  the  shell[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              variable  ZLE_LINE_ABORTED.   If  the editor is aborted from within vared,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the variable ZLE_VARED_ABORTED is set.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       run-help (ESC-H ESC-h) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Push the buffer onto the buffer stack, and execute the  command  `run-help[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cmd',  where  cmd is the current command.  run-help is normally aliased to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              man.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-set-buffer (unbound) (") (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Specify a buffer to be used  in  the  following  command.   There  are  37[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              buffers that can be specified: the 26 `named' buffers "a to "z, the `yank'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              buffer  "0, the nine `queued' buffers "1 to "9 and the `black hole' buffer[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              "_.  The named buffers can also be specified as "A to "Z.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              When a buffer is specified for a cut, change or  yank  command,  the  text[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              concerned  replaces  the  previous  contents of the specified buffer. If a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              named buffer is specified using a capital, the newly cut text is  appended[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to the buffer instead of overwriting it. When using the "_ buffer, nothing[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              happens.  This  can  be  useful  for  deleting  text without affecting any[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              buffers.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If no buffer is specified for a cut or change command, "1 is used, and the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              contents of "1 to "8 are each shifted along one buffer; the contents of "9[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is lost. If no buffer is specified for a yank command,  "0  is  used.  Fi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              nally, a paste command without a specified buffer will paste the text from[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the most recent command regardless of any buffer that might have been used[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              with that command.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              When  called from a widget function by the zle command, the buffer can op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tionally be specified with an argument. For example,[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     zle vi-set-buffer A[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-set-mark (unbound) (m) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Set the specified mark at the cursor position.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       set-mark-command (^@) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Set the mark at the cursor position.  If called with  a  negative  numeric[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              argument,  do  not set the mark but deactivate the region so that it is no[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              longer highlighted (it is still usable for other purposes).  Otherwise the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              region is marked as active.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       spell-word (ESC-$ ESC-S ESC-s) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Attempt spelling correction on the current word.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       split-undo[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Breaks the undo sequence at the current change.  This is useful in vi mode[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              as changes made in insert mode are coalesced  on  entering  command  mode.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Similarly,  undo  will  normally  revert  as one all the changes made by a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              user-defined widget.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       undefined-key[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This command is executed when a key sequence that is not bound to any com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mand is typed.  By default it beeps.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       undo (^_ ^Xu ^X^U) (u) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Incrementally undo  the  last  text  modification.   When  called  from  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              user-defined  widget,  takes  an  optional  argument indicating a previous[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              state of the undo history as returned by the UNDO_CHANGE_NO variable; mod‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ifications are undone until that state is reached, subject  to  any  limit[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              imposed by the UNDO_LIMIT_NO variable.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that when invoked from vi command mode, the full prior change made in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              insert  mode is reverted, the changes having been merged when command mode[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              was selected.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       redo (unbound) (^R) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Incrementally redo undone text modifications.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-undo-change (unbound) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Undo the last text modification.  If repeated, redo the modification.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       visual-mode (unbound) (v) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Toggle vim-style visual selection mode. If line-wise visual mode  is  cur‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rently enabled then it is changed to being character-wise. If used follow‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ing  an  operator, it forces the subsequent movement command to be treated[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              as a character-wise movement.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       visual-line-mode (unbound) (V) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Toggle vim-style line-wise visual selection mode. If character-wise visual[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mode is currently enabled then it is changed to being line-wise.  If  used[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              following  an  operator,  it  forces the subsequent movement command to be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              treated as a line-wise movement.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       what-cursor-position (^X=) (ga) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Print the character under the cursor, its code as an  octal,  decimal  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              hexadecimal  number, the current cursor position within the buffer and the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              column of the cursor in the current line.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       where-is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Read the name of an editor command and print the listing of key  sequences[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that  invoke the specified command.  A restricted set of editing functions[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is available in the mini-buffer.  Keys are looked up in the  special  com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mand keymap, and if not found there in the main keymap.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       which-command (ESC-?) (unbound) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Push the buffer onto the buffer stack, and execute the command `which-com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mand  cmd'.  where  cmd is the current command.  which-command is normally[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              aliased to whence.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       vi-digit-or-beginning-of-line (unbound) (0) (unbound)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If the last command executed was a digit as part of an argument,  continue[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the argument.  Otherwise, execute vi-beginning-of-line.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Text Objects[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Text objects are commands that can be used to select a block of text according to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       some criteria. They are a feature of the vim text editor and so are primarily in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tended for use with vi operators or from visual selection mode. However, they can[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       also be used from vi-insert or emacs mode. Key bindings listed below apply to the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       viopp and visual keymaps.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       select-a-blank-word (aW)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Select  a word including adjacent blanks, where a word is defined as a se‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ries of non-blank characters. With a numeric argument, multiple words will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              be selected.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       select-a-shell-word (aa)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Select the current command argument applying the normal rules for quoting.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       select-a-word (aw)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Select a word including adjacent blanks, using the  normal  vi-style  word[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              definition. With a numeric argument, multiple words will be selected.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       select-in-blank-word (iW)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Select  a  word,  where a word is defined as a series of non-blank charac‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ters. With a numeric argument, multiple words will be selected.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       select-in-shell-word (ia)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Select the current command argument applying the normal rules for quoting.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If the argument begins and ends with matching quote characters, these  are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              not included in the selection.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       select-in-word (iw)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Select  a  word, using the normal vi-style word definition. With a numeric[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              argument, multiple words will be selected.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mCHARACTER HIGHLIGHTING[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The line editor has the ability to highlight characters or regions  of  the  line[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       that  have  a particular significance.  This is controlled by the array parameter[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zle_highlight, if it has been set by the user.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       If the parameter contains the single entry none all highlighting is  turned  off.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Note the parameter is still expected to be an array.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Otherwise  each  entry of the array should consist of a word indicating a context[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       for highlighting, then a colon, then a comma-separated list of the types of high‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       lighting to apply in that context.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The contexts available for highlighting are the following:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       default[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Any text within the command line not affected by any  other  highlighting.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Text outside the editable area of the command line is not affected.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       isearch[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When  one of the incremental history search widgets is active, the area of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the command line matched by the search string or pattern.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       region The currently selected text. In emacs terminology, this is referred to  as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  region  and is bounded by the cursor (point) and the mark. The region[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is only highlighted if it is active, which is the case after the  mark  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              modified  with  set-mark-command  or  exchange-point-and-mark.   Note that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              whether or not the region is active has no effect on its use within  emacs[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              style widgets, it simply determines whether it is highlighted. In vi mode,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the region corresponds to selected text in visual mode.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       special[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Individual characters that have no direct printable representation but are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              shown  in  a  special manner by the line editor.  These characters are de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              scribed below.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       suffix This context is used in completion for characters that are marked as  suf‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fixes  that will be removed if the completion ends at that point, the most[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              obvious example being a slash (/) after a directory name.  Note that  suf‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fix removal is configurable; the circumstances under which the suffix will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              be removed may differ for different completions.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       paste  Following a command to paste text, the characters that were inserted.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       When region_highlight is set, the contexts that describe a region -- isearch, re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       gion,  suffix,  and paste -- are applied first, then region_highlight is applied,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       then the remaining zle_highlight contexts are applied.  If a particular character[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       is affected by multiple specifications, the last specification wins.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zle_highlight may contain additional fields  for  controlling  how  terminal  se‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       quences  to  change  colours  are output.  Each of the following is followed by a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       colon and a string in the same form as for key bindings.  This will not be neces‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       sary for the vast majority of terminals as the defaults shown in parentheses  are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       widely used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       fg_start_code (\e[3)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The  start of the escape sequence for the foreground colour.  This is fol‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              lowed by one to three ASCII digits representing the colour.  Only used for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              palette colors, i.e. not 24-bit colors specified via a color triplet.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       fg_default_code (9)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The number to use instead of the colour to reset  the  default  foreground[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              colour.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       fg_end_code (m)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The end of the escape sequence for the foreground colour.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       bg_start_code (\e[4)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The  start  of  the  escape  sequence  for  the  background  colour.   See[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fg_start_code above.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       bg_default_code (9)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The number to use instead of the colour to reset  the  default  background[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              colour.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       bg_end_code (m)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The end of the escape sequence for the background colour.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The  available  types of highlighting are the following.  Note that not all types[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       of highlighting are available on all terminals:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       none   No highlighting is applied to the given context.  It  is  not  useful  for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              this  to appear with other types of highlighting; it is used to override a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              default.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       fg=colour[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The foreground colour should be set to colour, a decimal integer, the name[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of one of the eight most widely-supported colours or as a `#' followed  by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              an RGB triplet in hexadecimal format.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Not  all terminals support this and, of those that do, not all provide fa‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cilities to test the support, hence the user should decide  based  on  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              terminal type.  Most terminals support the colours black, red, green, yel‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              low,  blue,  magenta,  cyan and white, which can be set by name.  In addi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion. default may be used to set the terminal's default foreground colour.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Abbreviations are allowed; b or bl selects black.  Some terminals may gen‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              erate additional colours if the bold attribute is also present.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              On recent terminals and on systems with an  up-to-date  terminal  database[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  number of colours supported may be tested by the command `echotc Co';[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              if this succeeds, it indicates a limit on the number of colours which will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              be enforced by the line editor.  The number of colours is in any case lim‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ited to 256 (i.e. the range 0 to 255).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Some modern terminal emulators have support for  24-bit  true  colour  (16[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              million  colours).  In this case, the hex triplet format can be used. This[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              consists of a `#' followed by either a three or six digit hexadecimal num‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ber describing the red, green and  blue  components  of  the  colour.  Hex[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              triplets  can  also  be  used  with  88  and  256 colour terminals via the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zsh/nearcolor module (see zshmodules(1)).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Colour is also known as color.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       bg=colour[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The background colour should be set to colour.  This  works  similarly  to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  foreground  colour,  except the background is not usually affected by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the bold attribute.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       bold   The characters in the given context are shown in a  bold  font.   Not  all[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              terminals distinguish bold fonts.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       standout[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The  characters  in the given context are shown in the terminal's standout[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mode.  The actual effect is specific to the terminal; on many terminals it[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is inverse video.  On some such terminals, where the cursor does not blink[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              it appears with standout mode negated, making it less than clear where the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cursor actually is.  On such terminals one of the  other  effects  may  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              preferable for highlighting the region and matched search string.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       underline[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The  characters in the given context are shown underlined.  Some terminals[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              show the foreground in a different colour instead; in this case whitespace[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              will not be highlighted.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The characters described above as `special' are as follows.  The  formatting  de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       scribed here is used irrespective of whether the characters are highlighted:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ASCII control characters[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Control  characters  in  the  ASCII range are shown as `^' followed by the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              base character.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Unprintable multibyte characters[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This item applies to control characters not in the ASCII range, plus other[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              characters as follows.  If the MULTIBYTE option is  in  effect,  multibyte[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              characters not in the ASCII character set that are reported as having zero[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              width  are treated as combining characters when the option COMBINING_CHARS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is on.  If the option is off, or if a character appears where a  combining[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              character is not valid, the character is treated as unprintable.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Unprintable multibyte characters are shown as a hexadecimal number between[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              angle brackets.  The number is the code point of the character in the wide[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              character  set; this may or may not be Unicode, depending on the operating[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              system.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Invalid multibyte characters[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If the MULTIBYTE option is in effect, any sequence of one  or  more  bytes[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that  does  not  form  a  valid  character in the current character set is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              treated as a series of bytes each shown as a special character.  This case[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              can be distinguished from other unprintable characters as  the  bytes  are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              represented  as two hexadecimal digits between angle brackets, as distinct[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              from the four or eight digits that are  used  for  unprintable  characters[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that are nonetheless valid in the current character set.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Not  all systems support this: for it to work, the system's representation[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of wide characters must be code values from the Universal  Character  Set,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              as defined by IS0 10646 (also known as Unicode).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Wrapped double-width characters[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When a double-width character appears in the final column of a line, it is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              instead shown on the next line. The empty space left in the original posi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion is highlighted as a special character.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       If  zle_highlight is not set or no value applies to a particular context, the de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       faults applied are equivalent to[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              zle_highlight=(region:standout special:standout[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              suffix:bold isearch:underline paste:standout)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       i.e. both the region and special characters are shown in standout mode.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Within widgets, arbitrary regions may be highlighted by setting the special array[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       parameter region_highlight; see above.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mzsh 5.9                               May 14, 2022                             ZSHZLE(1)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────[0m
[38;2;248;248;242mZSHCOMPWID(1)                    General Commands Manual                   ZSHCOMPWID(1)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mNAME[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zshcompwid - zsh completion widgets[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mDESCRIPTION[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The shell's programmable completion mechanism can be  manipulated  in  two  ways;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       here  the  low-level  features supporting the newer, function-based mechanism are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       defined.  A complete set of shell functions based on these features is  described[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       in zshcompsys(1), and users with no interest in adding to that system (or, poten‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tially,  writing  their own -- see dictionary entry for `hubris') should skip the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       current section.  The older system based on the compctl builtin  command  is  de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       scribed in zshcompctl(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Completion  widgets  are defined by the -C option to the zle builtin command pro‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       vided by the zsh/zle module (see zshzle(1)). For example,[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              zle -C complete expand-or-complete completer[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       defines a widget named `complete'.  The second argument is the name of any of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       builtin widgets that handle completions: complete-word,  expand-or-complete,  ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       pand-or-complete-prefix,      menu-complete,     menu-expand-or-complete,     re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       verse-menu-complete, list-choices, or delete-char-or-list.  Note that  this  will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       still work even if the widget in question has been re-bound.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       When  this  newly defined widget is bound to a key using the bindkey builtin com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       mand defined in the zsh/zle module (see zshzle(1)), typing that key will call the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       shell function `completer'. This function is responsible for  generating  comple‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tion  matches using the builtins described below.  As with other ZLE widgets, the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       function is called with its standard input closed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Once the function returns, the completion  code  takes  over  control  again  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       treats  the  matches  in the same manner as the specified builtin widget, in this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       case expand-or-complete.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mCOMPLETION SPECIAL PARAMETERS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The parameters ZLE_REMOVE_SUFFIX_CHARS and ZLE_SPACE_SUFFIX_CHARS are used by the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       completion mechanism, but are not special. See Parameters Used By  The  Shell  in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zshparam(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Inside  completion  widgets,  and any functions called from them, some parameters[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       have special meaning; outside these functions they are not special to  the  shell[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       in any way.  These parameters are used to pass information between the completion[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       code  and  the  completion widget. Some of the builtin commands and the condition[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       codes use or change the current values of these parameters.  Any existing  values[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       will  be hidden during execution of completion widgets; except for compstate, the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       parameters are reset on each function exit (including nested function calls  from[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       within  the  completion  widget) to the values they had when the function was en‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tered.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       CURRENT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This is the number of the current word, i.e. the word the cursor  is  cur‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rently on in the words array.  Note that this value is only correct if the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ksharrays option is not set.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       IPREFIX[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Initially  this will be set to the empty string.  This parameter functions[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              like PREFIX; it contains a string which precedes the one in PREFIX and  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              not considered part of the list of matches.  Typically, a string is trans‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ferred from the beginning of PREFIX to the end of IPREFIX, for example:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     IPREFIX=${PREFIX%%\=*}=[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     PREFIX=${PREFIX#*=}[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              causes the part of the prefix up to and including the first equal sign not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to be treated as part of a matched string.  This can be done automatically[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              by the compset builtin, see below.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ISUFFIX[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              As  IPREFIX,  but  for  a suffix that should not be considered part of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              matches; note that the ISUFFIX string follows the SUFFIX string.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       PREFIX Initially this will be set to the part of the current word from the begin‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ning of the word up to the position of the cursor; it may  be  altered  to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              give a common prefix for all matches.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       QIPREFIX[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  parameter is read-only and contains the quoted string up to the word[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              being completed. E.g. when completing `"foo', this parameter contains  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              double  quote.  If  the  -q option of compset is used (see below), and the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              original string was `"foo bar' with the cursor on the `bar', this  parame‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ter contains `"foo '.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       QISUFFIX[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Like QIPREFIX, but containing the suffix.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       SUFFIX Initially this will be set to the part of the current word from the cursor[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              position  to  the  end;  it may be altered to give a common suffix for all[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              matches.  It is most useful when the option COMPLETE_IN_WORD  is  set,  as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              otherwise the whole word on the command line is treated as a prefix.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       compstate[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This is an associative array with various keys and values that the comple‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion  code  uses  to exchange information with the completion widget.  The[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              keys are:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              all_quotes[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     The -q option of the compset builtin command (see below)  allows  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     quoted string to be broken into separate words; if the cursor is on[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     one  of those words, that word will be completed, possibly invoking[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     `compset -q' recursively.  With this key it is possible to test the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     types of quoted strings which are currently broken  into  parts  in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     this  fashion.   Its  value contains one character for each quoting[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     level.  The characters are a single quote or  a  double  quote  for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     strings  quoted  with  these characters, a dollars sign for strings[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     quoted with $'...' and a backslash for strings not starting with  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     quote  character.   The  first character in the value always corre‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     sponds to the innermost quoting level.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              context[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     This will be set by the completion code to the overall  context  in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     which completion is attempted. Possible values are:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     array_value[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                            when  completing  inside the value of an array parameter as‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                            signment; in this case the words array  contains  the  words[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                            inside the parentheses.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     brace_parameter[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                            when  completing  the name of a parameter in a parameter ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                            pansion beginning with ${.  This context will  also  be  set[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                            when completing parameter flags following ${(; the full com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                            mand  line  argument  is presented and the handler must test[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                            the value to be completed to  ascertain  that  this  is  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                            case.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     assign_parameter[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                            when  completing  the name of a parameter in a parameter as‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                            signment.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     command[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                            when completing for a normal command (either in command  po‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                            sition or for an argument of the command).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     condition[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                            when  completing  inside a `[[...]]' conditional expression;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                            in this case the words array contains only the words  inside[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                            the conditional expression.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     math   when  completing  in  a  mathematical  environment such as a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                            `((...))' construct.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     parameter[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                            when completing the name of a parameter in a  parameter  ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                            pansion beginning with $ but not ${.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     redirect[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                            when completing after a redirection operator.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     subscript[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                            when completing inside a parameter subscript.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     value  when completing the value of a parameter assignment.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              exact  Controls  the  behaviour when the REC_EXACT option is set.  It will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     be set to accept if an exact match would be accepted, and  will  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     unset otherwise.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     If  it  was  set when at least one match equal to the string on the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     line was generated, the match is accepted.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              exact_string[0m
