[38;2;248;248;242m              Next,  _tags  is called repeatedly without arguments from the same comple‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion function.  This successively selects the first, second, etc.  set  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tags  requested by the user.  The return status is zero if at least one of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the tags is requested and non-zero otherwise.  To test if a particular tag[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is to be tried, the _requested function should be called (see above).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If `-C name' is given, name is temporarily stored in  the  argument  field[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (the  fifth) of the context in the curcontext parameter during the call to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              _tags; the field is restored on exit.  This allows _tags  to  use  a  more[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              specific context without having to change and reset the curcontext parame‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ter (which has the same effect).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       _tilde_files[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Like _files, but resolve leading tildes according to the rules of filename[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              expansion, so the suggested completions don't start with a `~' even if the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              filename on the command-line does.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       _values [ -O name ] [ -s sep ] [ -S sep ] [ -wC ] desc spec ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  is used to complete arbitrary keywords (values) and their arguments,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              or lists of such combinations.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If the first argument is the option `-O name', it will be used in the same[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              way as by the _arguments function.  In other words, the  elements  of  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              name array will be passed to compadd when executing an action.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If the first argument (or the first argument after `-O name') is `-s', the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              next  argument  is  used  as the character that separates multiple values.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This character is automatically added after each value in  an  auto-remov‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              able  fashion  (see below); all values completed by `_values -s' appear in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the same word on the command line, unlike completion using _arguments.  If[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              this option is not present, only a single  value  will  be  completed  per[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              word.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Normally, _values will only use the current word to determine which values[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              are  already present on the command line and hence are not to be completed[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              again.  If the -w option is given, other arguments are examined as well.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The first non-option argument, desc, is used as a string to print as a de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              scription before listing the values.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              All other arguments describe the possible values and  their  arguments  in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  same  format  used  for  the description of options by the _arguments[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              function (see above).  The only differences are that no minus or plus sign[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is required at the beginning, values can have only one argument,  and  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              forms of action beginning with an equal sign are not supported.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The  character  separating  a value from its argument can be set using the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              option -S (like -s, followed by the character to use as the  separator  in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  next argument).  By default the equals sign will be used as the sepa‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rator between values and arguments.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Example:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     _values -s , 'description' \[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                             '*foo[bar]' \[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                             '(two)*one[number]:first count:' \[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                             'two[another number]::second count:(1 2 3)'[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              This describes three possible values: `foo', `one', and `two'.  The  first[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is  described  as  `bar', takes no argument and may appear more than once.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The second is described as `number', may appear more than once, and  takes[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              one mandatory argument described as `first count'; no action is specified,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              so  it  will  not be completed.  The `(two)' at the beginning says that if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the value `one' is on the line, the value `two' will no longer be  consid‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ered  a possible completion.  Finally, the last value (`two') is described[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              as `another number' and takes an optional argument  described  as  `second[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              count'  for  which  the completions (to appear after an `=') are `1', `2',[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and `3'.  The _values function will complete lists of these  values  sepa‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rated by commas.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Like  _arguments, this function temporarily adds another context name com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ponent to the arguments element (the fifth) of the current  context  while[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              executing  the  action.   Here this name is just the name of the value for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              which the argument is completed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The style verbose is used to decide if the  descriptions  for  the  values[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (but not those for the arguments) should be printed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The  associative  array  val_args is used to report values and their argu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ments; this works similarly to the opt_args associative array used by _ar‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              guments.  Hence the function calling _values should declare the local  pa‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rameters state, state_descr, line, context and val_args:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     local context state state_descr line[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     typeset -A val_args[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              when  using an action of the form `->string'.  With this function the con‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              text parameter will be set to the name of the value whose argument  is  to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              be  completed.   Note  that  for  _values,  the  state and state_descr are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              scalars rather than arrays.  Only a single matching state is returned.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note also that _values normally adds the character used as  the  separator[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              between  values  as an auto-removable suffix (similar to a `/' after a di‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rectory).  However, this is not possible for a `->string'  action  as  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              matches  for  the  argument are generated by the calling function.  To get[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the usual behaviour, the calling function can add the  separator  x  as  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              suffix  by  passing  the  options `-qS x' either directly or indirectly to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              compadd.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The option -C is treated in the same way as it is by _arguments.  In  that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              case  the parameter curcontext should be made local instead of context (as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              described above).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       _wanted [ -x ] [ -C name ]  [ -12VJ ] tag name descr command [ arg ...][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              In many contexts, completion can  only  generate  one  particular  set  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              matches, usually corresponding to a single tag.  However, it is still nec‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              essary  to  decide  whether  the user requires matches of this type.  This[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              function is useful in such a case.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The arguments to _wanted are the same as those to _requested,  i.e.  argu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ments  to be passed to _description.  However, in this case the command is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              not optional;  all the processing of tags, including the  loop  over  both[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tags and tag labels and the generation of matches, is carried out automat‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ically by _wanted.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Hence  to offer only one tag and immediately add the corresponding matches[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              with the given description:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     local expl[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     _wanted tag expl 'description' \[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                         compadd -- match1 match2...[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              See also the use of _wanted in the example function in the subsection `Dy‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              namic named directories' in zshexpn(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that, as for _requested, the command must be able to  accept  options[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to be passed down to compadd.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Like  _tags  this function supports the -C option to give a different name[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for the argument context field.  The -x option has the same meaning as for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              _description.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       _widgets [ -g pattern ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This function completes names of zle widgets (see the section `Widgets' in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zshzle(1)).  The pattern, if present, is matched  against  values  of  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              $widgets  special parameter, documented in the section `The zsh/zleparame‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ter Module' in zshmodules(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mCOMPLETION SYSTEM VARIABLES[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       There are some standard variables, initialised by the _main_complete function and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       then used from other functions.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The standard variables are:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       _comp_caller_options[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The completion system uses setopt to set a number of options. This  allows[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              functions  to be written without concern for compatibility with every pos‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sible combination of user options. However, sometimes completion needs  to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              know  what  the  user's  option  preferences  are.  These are saved in the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              _comp_caller_options associative array. Option names, spelled in lowercase[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              without underscores, are mapped to one or other of the  strings  `on'  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `off'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       _comp_priv_prefix[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Completion  functions such as _sudo can set the _comp_priv_prefix array to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              a command prefix that may then be used by _call_program to match the priv‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ileges when calling programs to generate matches.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Two more features are offered by the _main_complete function.  The  arrays  comp‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       prefuncs  and  comppostfuncs may contain names of functions that are to be called[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       immediately before or after completion has been tried.  A function will  only  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       called once unless it explicitly reinserts itself into the array.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mCOMPLETION DIRECTORIES[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       In  the source distribution, the files are contained in various subdirectories of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the Completion directory.  They may have been installed in the same structure, or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       into one single function directory.  The following is a description of the  files[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       found  in  the  original  directory structure.  If you wish to alter an installed[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       file, you will need to copy it to some directory which appears  earlier  in  your[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       fpath than the standard directory where it appears.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Base   The  core  functions and special completion widgets automatically bound to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              keys.  You will certainly need most of these,  though  will  probably  not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              need to alter them.  Many of these are documented above.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Zsh    Functions  for  completing arguments of shell builtin commands and utility[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              functions for this.  Some of these are also used  by  functions  from  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Unix directory.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Unix   Functions for completing arguments of external commands and suites of com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mands.   They  may  need modifying for your system, although in many cases[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              some attempt is made to decide which version of a command is present.  For[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              example, completion for the mount command tries to determine the system it[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is running on, while completion for many other  utilities  try  to  decide[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              whether  the  GNU  version of the command is in use, and hence whether the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              --help option is supported.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       X, AIX, BSD, ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Completion and utility function for commands available only on  some  sys‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tems.   These  are  not arranged hierarchically, so, for example, both the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Linux and Debian directories, as well as the X directory, may be useful on[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              your system.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mzsh 5.9                               May 14, 2022                         ZSHCOMPSYS(1)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────[0m
[38;2;248;248;242mZSHCOMPCTL(1)                    General Commands Manual                   ZSHCOMPCTL(1)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mNAME[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zshcompctl - zsh programmable completion[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mDESCRIPTION[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       This version of zsh has two ways of performing completion of words on the command[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       line.  New users of the shell may prefer to use the newer and more powerful  sys‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tem  based  on shell functions; this is described in zshcompsys(1), and the basic[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       shell mechanisms which support it are described in  zshcompwid(1).   This  manual[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       entry describes the older compctl command.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       compctl [ -CDT ] options [ command ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       compctl [ -CDT ] options [ -x pattern options - ... -- ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m               [ + options [ -x ... -- ] ... [+] ] [ command ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       compctl -M match-specs ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       compctl -L [ -CDTM ] [ command ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       compctl + command ...[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Control  the  editor's  completion  behavior according to the supplied set of op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tions.  Various editing commands, notably expand-or-complete-word, usually  bound[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       to  tab, will attempt to complete a word typed by the user, while others, notably[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       delete-char-or-list, usually bound to ^D in EMACS editing mode, list  the  possi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       bilities; compctl controls what those possibilities are.  They may for example be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       filenames  (the  most  common  case,  and hence the default), shell variables, or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       words from a user-specified list.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mCOMMAND FLAGS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Completion of the arguments of a command may be different for each command or may[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       use the default.  The behavior when completing the command word itself  may  also[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       be  separately specified.  These correspond to the following flags and arguments,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       all of which (except for -L) may be combined with any combination of the  options[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       described subsequently in the section `Option Flags':[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       command ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              controls  completion  for the named commands, which must be listed last on[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the command line.  If completion is attempted for a command with  a  path‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              name  containing slashes and no completion definition is found, the search[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is retried with the last pathname component. If the command starts with  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              =, completion is tried with the pathname of the command.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Any  of  the command strings may be patterns of the form normally used for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              filename generation.  These should be quoted to protect them from  immedi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ate  expansion; for example the command string 'foo*' arranges for comple‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion of the words of any command beginning with foo.  When  completion  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              attempted, all pattern completions are tried in the reverse order of their[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              definition  until  one  matches.   By default, completion then proceeds as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              normal, i.e. the shell will try to generate more matches for the  specific[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              command  on  the  command line; this can be overridden by including -tn in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the flags for the pattern completion.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that aliases are expanded before the command name is  determined  un‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              less  the  COMPLETE_ALIASES  option  is set.  Commands may not be combined[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              with the -C, -D or -T flags.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -C     controls completion when the command word itself is being  completed.   If[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              no  compctl  -C command has been issued,  the names of any executable com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mand (whether in the path or specific to the shell,  such  as  aliases  or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              functions) are completed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -D     controls default completion behavior for the arguments of commands not as‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              signed  any  special  behavior.  If no compctl -D command has been issued,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              filenames are completed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -T     supplies completion flags to be used before any other processing is  done,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              even  before  processing for compctls defined for specific commands.  This[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is especially useful when combined with extended completion (the -x  flag,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              see the section `Extended Completion' below).  Using this flag you can de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fine  default behavior which will apply to all commands without exception,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              or you can alter the standard behavior for all commands.  For example,  if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              your  access  to the user database is too slow and/or it contains too many[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              users (so that completion after `~' is too slow to be usable), you can use[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     compctl -T -x 's[~] C[0,[^/]#]' -k friends -S/ -tn[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              to complete the strings in the array friends after a `~'.  The C[...]  ar‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              gument  is  necessary so that this form of ~-completion is not tried after[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the directory name is finished.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -L     lists the existing completion behavior in a manner  suitable  for  putting[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              into  a start-up script; the existing behavior is not changed.  Any combi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              nation of the above forms, or the -M flag (which must follow the -L flag),[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              may be specified, otherwise all defined completions are listed.  Any other[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              flags supplied are ignored.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       no argument[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If no argument is given, compctl lists all defined completions in  an  ab‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              breviated  form;  with a list of options, all completions with those flags[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              set (not counting extended completion) are listed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       If the + flag is alone and followed immediately by the command list, the  comple‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tion behavior for all the commands in the list is reset to the default.  In other[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       words, completion will subsequently use the options specified by the -D flag.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The  form with -M as the first and only option defines global matching specifica‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tions (see zshcompwid). The match specifications given will  be  used  for  every[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       completion  attempt (only when using compctl, not with the new completion system)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       and are tried in the order in which they are defined until one generates at least[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       one match. E.g.:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              compctl -M '' 'm:{a-zA-Z}={A-Za-z}'[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       This will first try completion without any global match specifications (the empty[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       string) and, if that generates no matches, will try case insensitive completion.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mOPTION FLAGS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       [ -fcFBdeaRGovNAIOPZEnbjrzu/12 ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       [ -k array ] [ -g globstring ] [ -s subststring ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       [ -K function ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       [ -Q ] [ -P prefix ] [ -S suffix ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       [ -W file-prefix ] [ -H num pattern ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       [ -q ] [ -X explanation ] [ -Y explanation ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       [ -y func-or-var ] [ -l cmd ] [ -h cmd ] [ -U ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       [ -t continue ] [ -J name ] [ -V name ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       [ -M match-spec ][0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The remaining options specify the type of command arguments to  look  for  during[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       completion.   Any  combination  of  these flags may be specified; the result is a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       sorted list of all the possibilities.  The options are as follows.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Simple Flags[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       These produce completion lists made up by the shell itself:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -f     Filenames and file system paths.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -/     Just file system paths.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -c     Command names, including aliases, shell functions, builtins  and  reserved[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              words.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -F     Function names.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -B     Names of builtin commands.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -m     Names of external commands.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -w     Reserved words.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -a     Alias names.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -R     Names of regular (non-global) aliases.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -G     Names of global aliases.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -d     This  can  be combined with -F, -B, -w, -a, -R and -G to get names of dis‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              abled functions, builtins, reserved words or aliases.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -e     This option (to show enabled commands) is in effect by default, but may be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              combined with -d; -de in combination with -F, -B, -w, -a, -R and  -G  will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              complete  names  of functions, builtins, reserved words or aliases whether[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              or not they are disabled.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -o     Names of shell options (see zshoptions(1)).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -v     Names of any variable defined in the shell.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -N     Names of scalar (non-array) parameters.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -A     Array names.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -I     Names of integer variables.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -O     Names of read-only variables.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -p     Names of parameters used by the shell (including special parameters).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -Z     Names of shell special parameters.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -E     Names of environment variables.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -n     Named directories.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -b     Key binding names.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -j     Job names:  the first word of the job leader's command line.  This is use‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ful with the kill builtin.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -r     Names of running jobs.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -z     Names of suspended jobs.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -u     User names.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Flags with Arguments[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       These have user supplied arguments to determine how the list of completions is to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       be made up:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -k array[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Names taken from the elements of $array (note that the `$' does not appear[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              on the command line).  Alternatively, the argument array itself may  be  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              set  of  space- or comma-separated values in parentheses, in which any de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              limiter may be escaped with a backslash; in this case the argument  should[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              be quoted.  For example,[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     compctl -k "(cputime filesize datasize stacksize[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                                 coredumpsize resident descriptors)" limit[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -g globstring[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The globstring is expanded using filename globbing; it should be quoted to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              protect  it from immediate expansion. The resulting filenames are taken as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the possible completions.  Use `*(/)' instead  of  `*/'  for  directories.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The fignore special parameter is not applied to the resulting files.  More[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              than one pattern may be given separated by blanks. (Note that brace expan‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sion  is  not part of globbing.  Use the syntax `(either|or)' to match al‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ternatives.)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -s subststring[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The subststring is split into words and these words are than expanded  us‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ing  all shell expansion mechanisms (see zshexpn(1)).  The resulting words[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              are taken as possible completions.  The fignore special parameter  is  not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              applied to the resulting files.  Note that -g is faster for filenames.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -K function[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Call  the  given  function to get the completions.  Unless the name starts[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              with an underscore, the function is passed two arguments: the  prefix  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  suffix  of  the word on which completion is to be attempted, in other[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              words those characters before the cursor position, and those from the cur‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sor position onwards.  The whole command line can be accessed with the  -c[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and -l flags of the read builtin. The function should set the variable re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ply  to  an array containing the completions (one completion per element);[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              note that reply should not be made local to the  function.   From  such  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              function  the command line can be accessed with the -c and -l flags to the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              read builtin.  For example,[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     function whoson { reply=(`users`); }[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     compctl -K whoson talk[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              completes only logged-on users after `talk'.  Note that `whoson' must  re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              turn an array, so `reply=`users`' would be incorrect.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -H num pattern[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The  possible completions are taken from the last num history lines.  Only[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              words matching pattern are taken.  If num is zero or  negative  the  whole[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              history is searched and if pattern is the empty string all words are taken[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (as with `*').  A typical use is[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     compctl -D -f + -H 0 ''[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              which  forces completion to look back in the history list for a word if no[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              filename matches.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Control Flags[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       These do not directly specify types of name to be completed, but  manipulate  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       options that do:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -Q     This  instructs  the shell not to quote any metacharacters in the possible[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              completions.  Normally the results of a completion are inserted  into  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              command  line  with any metacharacters quoted so that they are interpreted[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              as normal characters.  This is  appropriate  for  filenames  and  ordinary[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              strings.  However, for special effects, such as inserting a backquoted ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pression  from  a completion array (-k) so that the expression will not be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              evaluated until the complete line is executed, this option must be used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -P prefix[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The prefix is inserted just before the completed string; any initial  part[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              already  typed  will be completed and the whole prefix ignored for comple‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion purposes.  For example,[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     compctl -j -P "%" kill[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              inserts a `%' after the kill command and then completes job names.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -S suffix[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              When a completion is found the suffix  is  inserted  after  the  completed[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              string.   In  the  case  of menu completion the suffix is inserted immedi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ately, but it is still possible to cycle through the list  of  completions[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              by repeatedly hitting the same key.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -W file-prefix[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              With  directory  file-prefix:   for  command, file, directory and globbing[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              completion (options -c, -f, -/, -g), the file prefix is  implicitly  added[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              in front of the completion.  For example,[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     compctl -/ -W ~/Mail maildirs[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              completes  any  subdirectories  to any depth beneath the directory ~/Mail,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              although that prefix does not appear on the command line.  The file-prefix[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              may also be of the form accepted by the -k flag, i.e. the name of an array[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              or a literal list in parenthesis. In this case all the directories in  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              list will be searched for possible completions.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -q     If  used with a suffix as specified by the -S option, this causes the suf‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fix to be removed if the next character typed is a blank or does  not  in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sert anything or if the suffix consists of only one character and the next[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              character  typed  is  the  same character; this the same rule used for the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              AUTO_REMOVE_SLASH option.  The option is most useful for  list  separators[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (comma, colon, etc.).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -l cmd This  option restricts the range of command line words that are considered[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to be arguments.  If combined with one of the extended completion patterns[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `p[...]', `r[...]', or `R[...]'  (see the  section  `Extended  Completion'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              below)  the range is restricted to the range of arguments specified in the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              brackets.  Completion is then performed as if these had been given as  ar‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              guments  to  the  cmd supplied with the option. If the cmd string is empty[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the first word in the range is instead taken as the command name, and com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mand name completion performed on the first word in the range.  For  exam‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ple,[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     compctl -x 'r[-exec,;]' -l '' -- find[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              completes  arguments  between `-exec' and the following `;' (or the end of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the command line if there is no such string) as if they  were  a  separate[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              command line.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -h cmd Normally  zsh  completes quoted strings as a whole. With this option, com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pletion can be done separately on different  parts  of  such  strings.  It[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              works  like  the -l option but makes the completion code work on the parts[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of the current word that are separated by spaces.  These  parts  are  com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pleted  as  if  they  were arguments to the given cmd. If cmd is the empty[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              string, the first part is completed as a command name, as with -l.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -U     Use the whole list of possible completions, whether or not  they  actually[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              match  the  word  on  the  command  line.   The  word typed so far will be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              deleted.  This is most useful with a function (given  by  the  -K  option)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              which  can  examine  the  word  components  passed  to it (or via the read[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              builtin's -c and -l flags)  and  use  its  own  criteria  to  decide  what[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              matches.  If there is no completion, the original word is retained.  Since[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  produced possible completions seldom have interesting common prefixes[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and suffixes, menu completion is started immediately if AUTO_MENU  is  set[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and this flag is used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -y func-or-var[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The  list provided by func-or-var is displayed instead of the list of com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pletions whenever a listing is required; the actual completions to be  in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              serted  are  not  affected.   It  can be provided in two ways. Firstly, if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              func-or-var begins with a $ it defines a variable, or if it begins with  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              left parenthesis a literal array, which contains the list.  A variable may[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              have  been  set by a call to a function using the -K option.  Otherwise it[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              contains the name of a function which will be executed to create the list.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The function will be passed as an argument list all matching  completions,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              including prefixes and suffixes expanded in full, and should set the array[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              reply  to  the  result.   In both cases, the display list will only be re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              trieved after a complete list of matches has been created.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that the returned list does not have to correspond, even  in  length,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to  the  original set of matches, and may be passed as a scalar instead of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              an array.  No special formatting of characters is performed on the  output[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              in  this  case;  in particular, newlines are printed literally and if they[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              appear output in columns is suppressed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -X explanation[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Print explanation when trying completion on the current set of options.  A[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `%n'  in  this string is replaced by the number of matches that were added[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for this explanation string.  The explanation only appears  if  completion[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              was  tried and there was no unique match, or when listing completions. Ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              planation strings will be listed together with the matches  of  the  group[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              specified  together with the -X option (using the -J or -V option). If the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              same explanation string is given to multiple -X options,  the  string  ap‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pears  only  once (for each group) and the number of matches shown for the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `%n' is the total number of all matches for each of  these  uses.  In  any[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              case,  the explanation string will only be shown if there was at least one[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              match added for the explanation string.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The sequences %B, %b, %S, %s, %U, and %u specify output attributes  (bold,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              standout, and underline), %F, %f, %K, %k specify foreground and background[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              colours, and %{...%} can be used to include literal escape sequences as in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              prompts.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -Y explanation[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Identical  to  -X,  except  that the explanation first undergoes expansion[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              following the usual rules for strings in  double  quotes.   The  expansion[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              will  be  carried  out after any functions are called for the -K or -y op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tions, allowing them to set variables.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -t continue[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The continue-string contains a character that specifies which set of  com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pletion flags should be used next.  It is useful:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              (i)  With  -T,  or when trying a list of pattern completions, when compctl[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              would usually continue with ordinary  processing  after  finding  matches;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              this can be suppressed with `-tn'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              (ii)  With  a list of alternatives separated by +, when compctl would nor‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mally stop when one of the alternatives  generates  matches.   It  can  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              forced  to  consider  the  next  set of completions by adding `-t+' to the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              flags of the alternative before the `+'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              (iii) In an extended completion list (see below), when compctl would  nor‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mally  continue until a set of conditions succeeded, then use only the im‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mediately following flags.  With `-t-', compctl will continue  trying  ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tended  completions after the next `-'; with `-tx' it will attempt comple‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion with the default flags, in other words those before the `-x'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -J name[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This gives the name of the group the matches should be placed  in.  Groups[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              are listed and sorted separately; likewise, menu completion will offer the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              matches in the groups in the order in which the groups were defined. If no[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              group  name  is  explicitly given, the matches are stored in a group named[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              default. The first time a group name is encountered,  a  group  with  that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              name  is  created.  After  that  all  matches with the same group name are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              stored in that group.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              This can be useful with non-exclusive alternative completions.  For  exam‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ple, in[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     compctl -f -J files -t+ + -v -J variables foo[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              both  files and variables are possible completions, as the -t+ forces both[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sets of alternatives before and after the + to be considered at once.  Be‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cause of the -J options, however, all files are listed  before  all  vari‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ables.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -V name[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Like  -J,  but matches within the group will not be sorted in listings nor[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              in menu completion. These unsorted groups are in a  different  name  space[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              from  the sorted ones, so groups defined as -J files and -V files are dis‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tinct.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -1     If given together with the -V option, makes only consecutive duplicates in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the group be removed. Note that groups with and without this flag  are  in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              different name spaces.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -2     If  given together with the -J or -V option, makes all duplicates be kept.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Again, groups with and without this flag are in different name spaces.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       -M match-spec[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This defines additional matching control  specifications  that  should  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              used  only  when testing words for the list of flags this flag appears in.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The format of the match-spec string is described in zshcompwid.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mALTERNATIVE COMPLETION[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       compctl [ -CDT ] options + options [ + ... ] [ + ] command ...[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The form with `+' specifies alternative options. Completion is tried with the op‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tions before the first `+'. If this produces no matches completion is tried  with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the flags after the `+' and so on. If there are no flags after the last `+' and a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       match  has  not been found up to that point, default completion is tried.  If the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       list of flags contains a -t with a + character, the next list of  flags  is  used[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       even if the current list produced matches.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Additional  options  are  available  that restrict completion to some part of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       command line; this is referred to as `extended completion'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mEXTENDED COMPLETION[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       compctl [ -CDT ] options -x pattern options - ... --[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m               [ command ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       compctl [ -CDT ] options [ -x pattern options - ... -- ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m               [ + options [ -x ... -- ] ... [+] ] [ command ... ][0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The form with `-x' specifies extended  completion  for  the  commands  given;  as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       shown, it may be combined with alternative completion using `+'.  Each pattern is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       examined  in turn; when a match is found, the corresponding options, as described[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       in the section `Option Flags' above, are used to generate  possible  completions.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       If no pattern matches, the options given before the -x are used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Note  that  each  pattern  should  be supplied as a single argument and should be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       quoted to prevent expansion of metacharacters by the shell.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       A pattern is built of sub-patterns separated by commas; it matches  if  at  least[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       one  of  these  sub-patterns matches (they are `or'ed). These sub-patterns are in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       turn composed of other sub-patterns separated by white spaces which match if  all[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       of  the sub-patterns match (they are `and'ed).  An element of the sub-patterns is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       of the form `c[...][...]', where the pairs of brackets may be repeated  as  often[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       as  necessary,  and  matches if any of the sets of brackets match (an `or').  The[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       example below makes this clearer.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The elements may be any of the following:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       s[string]...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Matches if the current word on the command line starts  with  one  of  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              strings  given  in brackets.  The string is not removed and is not part of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the completion.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       S[string]...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Like s[string] except that the string is part of the completion.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       p[from,to]...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Matches if the number of the current word is between one of the  from  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to pairs inclusive. The comma and to are optional; to defaults to the same[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              value  as  from.   The numbers may be negative: -n refers to the n'th last[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              word on the line.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       c[offset,string]...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Matches if the string matches the word offset by offset from  the  current[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              word position.  Usually offset will be negative.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       C[offset,pattern]...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Like c but using pattern matching instead.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       w[index,string]...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Matches  if  the  word  in  position  index  is equal to the corresponding[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              string.  Note that the word count is made after any alias expansion.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       W[index,pattern]...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Like w but using pattern matching instead.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       n[index,string]...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Matches if the current word contains string.  Anything up to and including[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the indexth occurrence of this string will not be considered part  of  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              completion,  but  the  rest will.  index may be negative to count from the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              end: in most cases, index will be 1 or -1.  For example,[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     compctl -s '`users`' -x 'n[1,@]' -k hosts -- talk[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              will usually complete usernames, but if you insert an @  after  the  name,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              names  from the array hosts (assumed to contain hostnames, though you must[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              make the array yourself) will be completed.  Other commands  such  as  rcp[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              can be handled similarly.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       N[index,string]...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Like  n  except  that the string will be taken as a character class.  Any‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              thing up to and including the indexth occurrence of any of the  characters[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              in string will not be considered part of the completion.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       m[min,max]...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Matches if the total number of words lies between min and max inclusive.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       r[str1,str2]...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Matches  if the cursor is after a word with prefix str1.  If there is also[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              a word with prefix str2 on the command line after the one matched by  str1[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              it  matches  only if the cursor is before this word. If the comma and str2[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              are omitted, it matches if the cursor is after a word with prefix str1.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       R[str1,str2]...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Like r but using pattern matching instead.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       q[str]...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Matches the word currently being completed is in single quotes and the str[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              begins with the letter `s', or if completion is done in double quotes  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              str  starts with the letter `d', or if completion is done in backticks and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              str starts with a `b'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mEXAMPLE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              compctl -u -x 's[+] c[-1,-f],s[-f+]' \[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                -g '~/Mail/*(:t)' - 's[-f],c[-1,-f]' -f -- mail[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       This is to be interpreted as follows:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       If the current command is mail, then[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              if ((the current word begins with + and the previous word is -f)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              or (the current word begins with -f+)), then complete the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              non-directory part (the `:t' glob modifier) of files in the directory[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ~/Mail; else[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              if the current word begins with -f or the previous word was -f, then[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              complete any file; else[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              complete user names.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mzsh 5.9                               May 14, 2022                         ZSHCOMPCTL(1)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────[0m
[38;2;248;248;242mZSHMODULES(1)                    General Commands Manual                   ZSHMODULES(1)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mNAME[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zshmodules - zsh loadable modules[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mDESCRIPTION[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Some optional parts of zsh are in modules, separate from the core of  the  shell.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Each  of these modules may be linked in to the shell at build time, or can be dy‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       namically linked while the shell is running if  the  installation  supports  this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       feature.   Modules  are  linked  at  runtime  with the zmodload command, see zsh‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       builtins(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The modules that are bundled with the zsh distribution are:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/attr[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Builtins for manipulating extended attributes (xattr).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/cap[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Builtins for manipulating POSIX.1e (POSIX.6) capability (privilege) sets.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/clone[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              A builtin that can clone a running shell onto another terminal.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/compctl[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The compctl builtin for controlling completion.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/complete[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The basic completion code.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/complist[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Completion listing extensions.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/computil[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              A module with utility builtins needed for the shell function based comple‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion system.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/curses[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              curses windowing commands[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/datetime[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Some date/time commands and parameters.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/db/gdbm[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Builtins for managing associative array parameters tied to GDBM databases.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/deltochar[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              A ZLE function duplicating EMACS' zap-to-char.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/example[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              An example of how to write a module.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/files[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Some basic file manipulation commands as builtins.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/langinfo[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Interface to locale information.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/mapfile[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Access to external files via a special associative array.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/mathfunc[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Standard scientific functions for use in mathematical evaluations.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/nearcolor[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Map colours to the nearest colour in the available palette.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/newuser[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Arrange for files for new users to be installed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/parameter[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Access to internal hash tables via special associative arrays.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/pcre[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Interface to the PCRE library.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/param/private[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Builtins for managing private-scoped parameters in function context.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/regex[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Interface to the POSIX regex library.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/sched[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              A builtin that provides a timed execution facility within the shell.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/net/socket[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Manipulation of Unix domain sockets[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/stat[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              A builtin command interface to the stat system call.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/system[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              A builtin interface to various low-level system features.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/net/tcp[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Manipulation of TCP sockets[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/termcap[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Interface to the termcap database.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/terminfo[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Interface to the terminfo database.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/watch[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Reporting of login and logout events.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/zftp[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              A builtin FTP client.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/zle[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The Zsh Line Editor, including the bindkey and vared builtins.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/zleparameter[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Access to internals of the Zsh Line Editor via parameters.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/zprof[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              A module allowing profiling for shell functions.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/zpty[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              A builtin for starting a command in a pseudo-terminal.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/zselect[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Block and return when file descriptors are ready.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/zutil[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Some utility builtins, e.g.  the  one  for  supporting  configuration  via[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              styles.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mTHE ZSH/ATTR MODULE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The  zsh/attr module is used for manipulating extended attributes.  The -h option[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       causes all commands to operate on symbolic links instead of their  targets.   The[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       builtins in this module are:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zgetattr [ -h ] filename attribute [ parameter ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Get  the  extended attribute attribute from the specified filename. If the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              optional argument parameter is given, the attribute is set on that parame‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ter instead of being printed to stdout.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zsetattr [ -h ] filename attribute value[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Set the extended attribute attribute on the specified filename to value.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zdelattr [ -h ] filename attribute[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Remove the extended attribute attribute from the specified filename.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zlistattr [ -h ] filename [ parameter ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              List the extended attributes currently set on the specified  filename.  If[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the optional argument parameter is given, the list of attributes is set on[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that parameter instead of being printed to stdout.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zgetattr  and zlistattr allocate memory dynamically.  If the attribute or list of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       attributes grows between the allocation and the call to get them, they return  2.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       On  all  other  errors, 1 is returned.  This allows the calling function to check[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       for this case and retry.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mTHE ZSH/CAP MODULE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The zsh/cap module is used for manipulating POSIX.1e (POSIX.6)  capability  sets.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       If  the operating system does not support this interface, the builtins defined by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       this module will do nothing.  The builtins in this module are:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       cap [ capabilities ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Change the shell's process capability sets to the specified  capabilities,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              otherwise display the shell's current capabilities.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       getcap filename ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  is a built-in implementation of the POSIX standard utility.  It dis‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              plays the capability sets on each specified filename.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       setcap capabilities filename ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This is a built-in implementation of the POSIX standard utility.  It  sets[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  capability sets on each specified filename to the specified capabili‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ties.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mTHE ZSH/CLONE MODULE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The zsh/clone module makes available one builtin command:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       clone tty[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Creates a forked instance of the current shell, attached to the  specified[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tty.   In  the  new  shell,  the  PID, PPID and TTY special parameters are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              changed appropriately.  $! is set to zero in the new shell, and to the new[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              shell's PID in the original shell.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The return status of the builtin is zero in both shells if successful, and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              non-zero on error.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The target of clone should be an unused terminal, such as an  unused  vir‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tual console or a virtual terminal created by[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     xterm -e sh -c 'trap : INT QUIT TSTP; tty;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                             while :; do sleep 100000000; done'[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Some  words  of  explanation  are  warranted about this long xterm command[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              line: when doing clone on a pseudo-terminal, some other session ("session"[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              meant as a unix session group, or SID) is  already  owning  the  terminal.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Hence  the  cloned zsh cannot acquire the pseudo-terminal as a controlling[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tty. That means two things:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              •      the job control signals will go to the sh-started-by-xterm  process[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     group (that's why we disable INT QUIT and TSTP with trap; otherwise[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the while loop could get suspended or killed)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              •      the  cloned  shell will have job control disabled, and the job con‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     trol keys (control-C, control-\ and control-Z) will not work.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              This does not apply when cloning to an unused vc.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Cloning to a used (and unprepared) terminal will result in  two  processes[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              reading simultaneously from the same terminal, with input bytes going ran‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              domly to either process.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              clone is mostly useful as a shell built-in replacement for openvt.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mTHE ZSH/COMPCTL MODULE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The zsh/compctl module makes available two builtin commands. compctl, is the old,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       deprecated  way  to  control  completions for ZLE.  See zshcompctl(1).  The other[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       builtin command, compcall can be used in  user-defined  completion  widgets,  see[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zshcompwid(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mTHE ZSH/COMPLETE MODULE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The  zsh/complete  module  makes  available several builtin commands which can be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       used in user-defined completion widgets, see zshcompwid(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mTHE ZSH/COMPLIST MODULE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The zsh/complist module offers three extensions to completion listings: the abil‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ity to highlight matches in such a list, the ability to scroll through long lists[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       and a different style of menu completion.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Colored completion listings[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Whenever one of the parameters ZLS_COLORS or ZLS_COLOURS is set and the  zsh/com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       plist  module  is  loaded or linked into the shell, completion lists will be col‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ored.  Note, however, that complist will not automatically be loaded if it is not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       linked in:  on systems with dynamic loading, `zmodload zsh/complist' is required.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The parameters ZLS_COLORS and ZLS_COLOURS describe how matches  are  highlighted.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       To  turn  on  highlighting an empty value suffices, in which case all the default[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       values given below will be used.  The format of the value of these parameters  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the  same as used by the GNU version of the ls command: a colon-separated list of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       specifications of the form `name=value'.  The name may be one  of  the  following[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       strings,  most of which specify file types for which the value will be used.  The[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       strings and their default values are:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       no 0   for normal text (i.e. when displaying something other than a matched file)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       fi 0   for regular files[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       di 32  for directories[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ln 36  for symbolic links.  If this has the special value target, symbolic  links[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              are dereferenced and the target file used to determine the display format.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       pi 31  for named pipes (FIFOs)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       so 33  for sockets[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       bd 44;37[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for block devices[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       cd 44;37[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for character devices[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       or none[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for a symlink to nonexistent file (default is the value defined for ln)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       mi none[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for  a  non-existent file (default is the value defined for fi); this code[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is currently not used[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       su 37;41[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for files with setuid bit set[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       sg 30;43[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for files with setgid bit set[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       tw 30;42[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for world writable directories with sticky bit set[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ow 34;43[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for world writable directories without sticky bit set[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       sa none[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for files with an associated suffix alias; this is only tested after  spe‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cific suffixes, as described below[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       st 37;44[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for directories with sticky bit set but not world writable[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ex 35  for executable files[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       lc \e[ for the left code (see below)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       rc m   for the right code[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       tc 0   for the character indicating the file type  printed after filenames if the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              LIST_TYPES option is set[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       sp 0   for the spaces printed after matches to align the next column[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       ec none[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for the end code[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Apart  from these strings, the name may also be an asterisk (`*') followed by any[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       string. The value given for such a string will be used for all files  whose  name[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ends  with  the  string.  The name may also be an equals sign (`=') followed by a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       pattern; the EXTENDED_GLOB option will be turned on for evaluation  of  the  pat‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tern.   The  value  given for this pattern will be used for all matches (not just[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       filenames) whose display string are matched by the pattern.  Definitions for  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       form with the leading equal sign take precedence over the values defined for file[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       types,  which  in  turn  take  precedence over the form with the leading asterisk[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       (file extensions).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The leading-equals form also allows different parts of the displayed  strings  to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       be  colored  differently.   For  this, the pattern has to use the `(#b)' globbing[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       flag and pairs of parentheses surrounding the parts of the strings that are to be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       colored differently.  In this case the value may consist of more than  one  color[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       code  separated  by  equal  signs.  The first code will be used for all parts for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       which no explicit code is specified and the following codes will be used for  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       parts matched by the sub-patterns in parentheses.  For example, the specification[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       `=(#b)(?)*(?)=0=3=7'  will be used for all matches which are at least two charac‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ters long and will use the code `3' for the first character,  `7'  for  the  last[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       character and `0' for the rest.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       All  three forms of name may be preceded by a pattern in parentheses.  If this is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       given, the value will be used only for matches in groups whose names are  matched[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       by the pattern given in the parentheses.  For example, `(g*)m*=43' highlights all[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       matches  beginning with `m' in groups whose names  begin with `g' using the color[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       code `43'.  In case of the `lc', `rc', and `ec' codes, the group pattern  is  ig‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       nored.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Note  also  that  all patterns are tried in the order in which they appear in the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       parameter value until the first one matches which is then used.  Patterns may  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       matched  against  completions,  descriptions  (possibly  with spaces appended for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       padding), or lines consisting of a completion followed  by  a  description.   For[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       consistent coloring it may be necessary to use more than one pattern or a pattern[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       with backreferences.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       When  printing  a  match,  the  code  prints  the  value of lc, the value for the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       file-type or the last matching specification with a `*', the  value  of  rc,  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       string  to  display for the match itself, and then the value of ec if that is de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       fined or the values of lc, no, and rc if ec is not defined.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The default values are ISO 6429 (ANSI) compliant and can be used on vt100 compat‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ible terminals such as xterms.  On monochrome terminals the default  values  will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       have no visible effect.  The colors function from the contribution can be used to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       get  associative  arrays containing the codes for ANSI terminals (see the section[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       `Other Functions' in zshcontrib(1)).  For  example,  after  loading  colors,  one[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       could   use   `$color[red]'  to  get  the  code  for  foreground  color  red  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       `$color[bg-green]' for the code for background color green.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       If the completion system invoked by compinit is used, these parameters should not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       be set directly because the system controls them itself.  Instead, the  list-col‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ors  style  should  be used (see the section `Completion System Configuration' in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zshcompsys(1)).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Scrolling in completion listings[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       To enable scrolling through a completion list, the LISTPROMPT parameter  must  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       set.   Its value will be used as the prompt; if it is the empty string, a default[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       prompt will be used.  The value may contain escapes of the form  `%x'.   It  sup‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ports  the escapes `%B', `%b', `%S', `%s', `%U', `%u', `%F', `%f', `%K', `%k' and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       `%{...%}' used also in shell prompts as well as three  pairs  of  additional  se‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       quences:  a `%l' or `%L' is replaced by the number of the last line shown and the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       total number of lines in the form `number/total'; a `%m' or `%M' is replaced with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the number of the last match shown and the total number of matches; and  `%p'  or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       `%P'  is replaced with `Top', `Bottom' or the position of the first line shown in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       percent of the total number of lines, respectively.  In each of these  cases  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       form  with  the  uppercase  letter will be replaced with a string of fixed width,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       padded to the right with spaces, while the lowercase form will not be padded.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       If the parameter LISTPROMPT is set, the completion code will not ask if the  list[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       should be shown.  Instead it immediately starts displaying the list, stopping af‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ter the first screenful, showing the prompt at the bottom, waiting for a keypress[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       after  temporarily switching to the listscroll keymap.  Some of the zle functions[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       have a special meaning while scrolling lists:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       send-break[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              stops listing discarding the key pressed[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       accept-line, down-history, down-line-or-history[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       down-line-or-search, vi-down-line-or-history[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              scrolls forward one line[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       complete-word, menu-complete, expand-or-complete[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       expand-or-complete-prefix, menu-complete-or-expand[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              scrolls forward one screenful[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       accept-search[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              stop listing but take no other action[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Every other character stops listing and immediately processes the key  as  usual.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Any  key  that  is  not  bound in the listscroll keymap or that is bound to unde‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       fined-key is looked up in the keymap currently selected.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       As for the ZLS_COLORS and ZLS_COLOURS parameters, LISTPROMPT should  not  be  set[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       directly  when  using  the  shell function based completion system.  Instead, the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       list-prompt style should be used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Menu selection[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The zsh/complist module also offers an alternative  style  of  selecting  matches[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       from  a  list, called menu selection, which can be used if the shell is set up to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       return to  the  last  prompt  after  showing  a  completion  list  (see  the  AL‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       WAYS_LAST_PROMPT option in zshoptions(1)).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Menu  selection can be invoked directly by the widget menu-select defined by this[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       module.  This is a standard ZLE widget that can be bound to a key  in  the  usual[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       way as described in zshzle(1).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Alternatively, the parameter MENUSELECT can be set to an integer, which gives the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       minimum number of matches that must be present before menu selection is automati‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       cally  turned  on.   This second method requires that menu completion be started,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       either directly from a widget such as menu-complete, or due to one of the options[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       MENU_COMPLETE or AUTO_MENU being set.  If MENUSELECT is  set,  but  is  0,  1  or[0m
