[38;2;248;248;242m              be spammed, but before any use of it is made.  Hence it is possible to al‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ter the value of $TCP_SESS within this function.  For example, the session[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              arguments  to  tcp_spam could include extra information to be stripped off[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and processed in tcp_on_spam.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If the function sets the parameter $REPLY to `done', the command  line  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              not  executed;  in  addition,  no  prompt  is printed for the -v option to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tcp_spam.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       tcp_on_unalias alias fd[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This is called with the name of an alias and the  corresponding  session's[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              file descriptor after an alias has been deleted.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mTCP UTILITY FUNCTIONS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The  following  functions  are used by the TCP function system but will rarely if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ever need to be called directly.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       tcp_fd_handler[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This is the function installed by tcp_open for handling input from  within[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  line editor, if that is required.  It is in the format documented for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the builtin `zle -F' in zshzle(1) .[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              While active, the function sets the  parameter  TCP_HANDLER_ACTIVE  to  1.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  allows  shell  code  called  internally  (for  example,  by  setting[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tcp_on_read) to tell if is being called when the shell is  otherwise  idle[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              at the editor prompt.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       tcp_output [ -q ] -P prompt -F fd -S sess[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  function  is  used  for both logging and handling output to standard[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              output, from within tcp_read and (if $TCP_OUTPUT is set) tcp_send.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The prompt to use is specified by -P; the default is the empty string.  It[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              can contain:[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              %c     Expands to 1 if the session is the current  session,  otherwise  0.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Used  with ternary expressions such as `%(c.-.+)' to output `+' for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     the current session and `-' otherwise.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              %f     Replaced by the session's file descriptor.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              %s     Replaced by the session name.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              %%     Replaced by a single `%'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The option -q suppresses output to standard output, but  not  to  any  log[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              files which are configured.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The  -S  and  -F options are used to pass in the session name and file de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              scriptor for possible replacement in the prompt.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mTCP USER PARAMETERS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Parameters follow the usual convention that uppercase is used for scalars and in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tegers, while lowercase is used for normal and associative array.  It  is  always[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       safe  for  user  code to read these parameters.  Some parameters may also be set;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       these are noted explicitly.  Others are included in this group as they are set by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the function system for the user's benefit, i.e. setting them  is  typically  not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       useful but is benign.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       It is often also useful to make settable parameters local to a function.  For ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ample,  `local  TCP_SILENT=1'  specifies  that data read during the function call[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       will not be printed to standard output, regardless of  the  setting  outside  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       function.   Likewise,  `local TCP_SESS=sess' sets a session for the duration of a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       function, and `local TCP_PROMPT=' specifies that no prompt is used for input dur‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ing the function.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       tcp_expect_lines[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Array.  The set of lines read during the last call to tcp_expect,  includ‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ing the last ($TCP_LINE).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       tcp_filter[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Array. May be set directly.  A set of extended globbing patterns which, if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              matched  in  tcp_output, will cause the line not to be printed to standard[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              output.  The patterns should be defined as described for the arguments  to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tcp_expect.  Output of line to log files is not affected.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TCP_HANDLER_ACTIVE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Scalar.   Set  to  1 within tcp_fd_handler to indicate to functions called[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              recursively that they have been called during an editor  session.   Other‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              wise unset.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TCP_LINE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The last line read by tcp_read, and hence also tcp_expect.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TCP_LINE_FD[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The     file     descriptor     from    which    $TCP_LINE    was    read.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ${tcp_by_fd[$TCP_LINE_FD]} will give the corresponding session name.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       tcp_lines[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Array. The set of lines read during the last call to  tcp_read,  including[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the last ($TCP_LINE).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TCP_LOG[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              May  be set directly, although it is also controlled by tcp_log.  The name[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of a file to which output from all sessions will be sent.  The  output  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              proceeded  by  the usual $TCP_PROMPT.  If it is not an absolute path name,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              it will follow the user's current directory.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TCP_LOG_SESS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              May be set directly, although it is also controlled by tcp_log.  The  pre‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fix  for  a set of files to which output from each session separately will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              be sent; the full filename is ${TCP_LOG_SESS}.sess.  Output to  each  file[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is  raw;  no prompt is added.  If it is not an absolute path name, it will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              follow the user's current directory.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       tcp_no_spam_list[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Array.  May be set directly.  See tcp_spam for how this is used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TCP_OUTPUT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              May be set directly.  If a non-empty string, any data sent to a session by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tcp_send will be logged.  This parameter gives the prompt to be used in  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              file specified by $TCP_LOG but not in a file generated from $TCP_LOG_SESS.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The prompt string has the same format as TCP_PROMPT and the same rules for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              its use apply.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TCP_PROMPT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              May  be  set directly.  Used as the prefix for data read by tcp_read which[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is printed to standard output or to the log file  given  by  $TCP_LOG,  if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              any.   Any  `%s', `%f' or `%%' occurring in the string will be replaced by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the name of the session, the session's underlying file  descriptor,  or  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              single `%', respectively.  The expression `%c' expands to 1 if the session[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              being  read is the current session, else 0; this is most useful in ternary[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              expressions such as `%(c.-.+)' which outputs `+' if  the  session  is  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              current one, else `-'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  the prompt starts with %P, this is stripped and the complete result of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the previous stage is passed through standard  prompt  %-style  formatting[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              before being output.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TCP_READ_DEBUG[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              May be set directly.  If this has non-zero length, tcp_read will give some[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              limited diagnostics about data being read.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TCP_SECONDS_START[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This value is created and initialised to zero by tcp_open.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The  functions  tcp_read  and tcp_expect use the shell's SECONDS parameter[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for their own timing purposes.  If that parameter is not of floating point[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              type on entry to one of the functions, it will create  a  local  parameter[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              SECONDS which is floating point and set the parameter TCP_SECONDS_START to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  previous  value  of  $SECONDS.   If the parameter is already floating[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              point, it is used without a local copy being created and TCP_SECONDS_START[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is not set.  As the global value is zero, the shell elapsed time is  guar‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              anteed to be the sum of $SECONDS and $TCP_SECONDS_START.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              This  can be avoided by setting SECONDS globally to a floating point value[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              using `typeset -F SECONDS'; then the TCP functions will never make a local[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              copy and never set TCP_SECONDS_START to a non-zero value.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TCP_SESS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              May be set directly.  The current session; must refer to one of  the  ses‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sions established by tcp_open.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TCP_SILENT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              May  be  set  directly,  although it is also controlled by tcp_log.  If of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              non-zero length, data read by tcp_read will not  be  written  to  standard[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              output, though may still be written to a log file.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       tcp_spam_list[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Array.  May be set directly.  See the description of the function tcp_spam[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for how this is used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TCP_TALK_ESCAPE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              May be set directly.  See the description of the function tcp_talk for how[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              this is used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       TCP_TIMEOUT[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              May be set directly.  Currently this is only used by the function tcp_com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mand, see above.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mTCP USER-DEFINED PARAMETERS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The  following  parameters are not set by the function system, but have a special[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       effect if set by the user.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       tcp_on_read[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This should be an associative array; if it is not, the behaviour is  unde‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fined.  Each key is the name of a shell function or other command, and the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              corresponding  value is a shell pattern (using EXTENDED_GLOB).  Every line[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              read from a TCP session directly or indirectly using tcp_read  (which  in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cludes  lines read by tcp_expect) is compared against the pattern.  If the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              line matches, the command given in the key is called with  two  arguments:[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the name of the session from which the line was read, and the line itself.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  any  function  called  to handle a line returns a non-zero status, the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              line is not output.  Thus a tcp_on_read handler containing  only  the  in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              struction  `return  1'  can be used to suppress output of particular lines[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (see, however, tcp_filter above).  However, the line is  still  stored  in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              TCP_LINE and tcp_lines; this occurs after all tcp_on_read processing.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mTCP UTILITY PARAMETERS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       These  parameters  are  controlled  by  the function system; they may be read di‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       rectly, but should not usually be set by user code.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       tcp_aliases[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Associative array.  The keys are the names of  sessions  established  with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tcp_open;  each  value is a space-separated list of aliases which refer to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that session.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       tcp_by_fd[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Associative array.  The keys are session file descriptors; each  value  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the name of that session.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       tcp_by_name[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Associative  array.  The keys are the names of sessions; each value is the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              file descriptor associated with that session.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mTCP EXAMPLES[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Here is a trivial example using a remote calculator.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       To create a calculator server on port 7337 (see the dc manual page for quite  how[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       infuriating the underlying command is):[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              tcp_proxy 7337 dc[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       To connect to this from the same host with a session also named `dc':[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              tcp_open localhost 7337 dc[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       To send a command to the remote session and wait a short while for output (assum‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ing dc is the current session):[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              tcp_command 2 4 + p[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       To close the session:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              tcp_close[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The  tcp_proxy needs to be killed to be stopped.  Note this will not usually kill[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       any connections which have already been accepted, and also that the port  is  not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       immediately available for reuse.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The  following  chunk  of code puts a list of sessions into an xterm header, with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the current session followed by a star.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              print -n "\033]2;TCP:" ${(k)tcp_by_name:/$TCP_SESS/$TCP_SESS\*} "\a"[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mTCP BUGS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The function tcp_read uses the shell's normal read builtin.  As this reads a com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       plete line at once, data arriving without a terminating  newline  can  cause  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       function to block indefinitely.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Though the function suite works well for interactive use and for data arriving in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       small  amounts, the performance when large amounts of data are being exchanged is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       likely to be extremely poor.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mzsh 5.9                               May 14, 2022                          ZSHTCPSYS(1)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────[0m
[38;2;248;248;242mZSHZFTPSYS(1)                    General Commands Manual                   ZSHZFTPSYS(1)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mNAME[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zshzftpsys - zftp function front-end[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mDESCRIPTION[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       This describes the set of shell functions supplied with the  source  distribution[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       as  an  interface to the zftp builtin command, allowing you to perform FTP opera‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tions from the shell command line or within functions or scripts.  The  interface[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       is similar to a traditional FTP client (e.g. the ftp command itself, see ftp(1)),[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       but  as it is entirely done within the shell all the familiar completion, editing[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       and globbing features, and so on, are present, and macros are particularly simple[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       to write as they are just ordinary shell functions.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The prerequisite is that the zftp command, as described in zshmodules(1)  ,  must[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       be  available in the version of zsh installed at your site.  If the shell is con‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       figured to load new commands at  run  time,  it  probably  is:  typing  `zmodload[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zsh/zftp'  will make sure (if that runs silently, it has worked).  If this is not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the case, it is possible zftp was linked into the shell  anyway:  to  test  this,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       type  `which zftp' and if zftp is available you will get the message `zftp: shell[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       built-in command'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Commands given directly with zftp builtin may be interspersed between  the  func‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tions  in  this  suite; in a few cases, using zftp directly may cause some of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       status information stored in shell parameters to become invalid.  Note in partic‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ular the description of the variables $ZFTP_TMOUT, $ZFTP_PREFS and  $ZFTP_VERBOSE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       for zftp.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mINSTALLATION[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       You  should  make sure all the functions from the Functions/Zftp directory of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       source distribution are available; they all begin  with  the  two  letters  `zf'.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       They  may already have been installed on your system; otherwise, you will need to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       find them and copy them.  The directory should appear as one of the  elements  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the $fpath array (this should already be the case if they were installed), and at[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       least  the  function zfinit should be autoloaded; it will autoload the rest.  Fi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       nally, to initialize the use of the system you need to call the zfinit  function.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The following code in your .zshrc will arrange for this; assume the functions are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       stored in the directory ~/myfns:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              fpath=(~/myfns $fpath)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              autoload -U zfinit[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zfinit[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Note  that zfinit assumes you are using the zmodload method to load the zftp com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       mand.  If it is already built into the shell, change zfinit to zfinit -n.  It  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       helpful  (though  not  essential) if the call to zfinit appears after any code to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       initialize the new completion system, else unnecessary compctl commands  will  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       given.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mFUNCTIONS[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The  sequence of operations in performing a file transfer is essentially the same[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       as that in a standard FTP client.  Note that, due  to  a  quirk  of  the  shell's[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       getopts builtin, for those functions that handle options you must use `--' rather[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       than `-' to ensure the remaining arguments are treated literally (a single `-' is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       treated as an argument).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Opening a connection[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zfparams [ host [ user [ password ... ] ] ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Set  or  show the parameters for a future zfopen with no arguments.  If no[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              arguments are given, the current parameters are  displayed  (the  password[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              will be shown as a line of asterisks).  If a host is given, and either the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              user  or  password  is not, they will be prompted for; also, any parameter[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              given as `?' will be prompted for, and if the `?' is followed by a string,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              that will be used as the prompt.  As zfopen calls zfparams  to  store  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              parameters, this usually need not be called directly.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              A  single  argument `-' will delete the stored parameters.  This will also[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cause the memory of the last directory (and so on) on the other host to be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              deleted.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zfopen [ -1 ] [ host [ user [ password [ account ] ] ] ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If host is present, open a connection to that  host  under  username  user[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              with  password  password (and, on the rare occasions when it is necessary,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              account account).  If a necessary parameter is missing or given as `?'  it[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              will be prompted for.  If host is not present, use a previously stored set[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of parameters.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  the  command was successful, and the terminal is compatible with xterm[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              or is sun-cmd, a summary will appear in the title bar,  giving  the  local[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              host:directory and the remote host:directory; this is handled by the func‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion zftp_chpwd, described below.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Normally,  the  host,  user and password are internally recorded for later[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              re-opening, either by a zfopen with no arguments,  or  automatically  (see[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              below).  With the option `-1', no information is stored.  Also, if an open[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              command  with  arguments  failed, the parameters will not be retained (and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              any previous parameters will also be deleted).  A zfopen on its own, or  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zfopen -1, never alters the stored parameters.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Both  zfopen  and  zfanon  (but  not zfparams) understand URLs of the form[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ftp://host/path... as meaning to connect to the host, then  change  direc‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tory to path (which must be a directory, not a file).  The `ftp://' can be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              omitted;  the  trailing  `/' is enough to trigger recognition of the path.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Note prefixes other than `ftp:' are not recognized, and that  all  charac‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ters after the first slash beyond host are significant in path.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zfanon [ -1 ] host[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Open  a  connection  host for anonymous FTP.  The username used is `anony‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mous'.  The password (which will be reported the first time) is  generated[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              as user@host; this is then stored in the shell parameter $EMAIL_ADDR which[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              can alternatively be set manually to a suitable string.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Directory management[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zfcd [ dir ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zfcd -[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zfcd old new[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Change the current directory on the remote server:  this is implemented to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              have many of the features of the shell builtin cd.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              In the first form with dir present, change to the directory dir.  The com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mand  `zfcd ..' is treated specially, so is guaranteed to work on non-UNIX[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              servers (note this is handled internally by zftp).  If dir is omitted, has[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the effect of `zfcd ~'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The second form changes to the directory previously current.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The third form attempts to change the current directory by  replacing  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              first  occurrence of the string old with the string new in the current di‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rectory.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that in this command, and indeed anywhere a remote  filename  is  ex‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pected, the string which on the local host corresponds to `~' is converted[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              back  to  a `~' before being passed to the remote machine.  This is conve‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              nient because of the way expansion is performed on the command line before[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zfcd receives a string.  For example, suppose the command is `zfcd ~/foo'.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The  shell  will  expand   this   to   a   full   path   such   as   `zfcd[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              /home/user2/pws/foo'.   At this stage, zfcd recognises the initial path as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              corresponding to `~' and will send the directory to  the  remote  host  as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ~/foo,  so  that the `~' will be expanded by the server to the correct re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mote host directory.  Other named directories of the form `~name' are  not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              treated in this fashion.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zfhere Change directory on the remote server to the one corresponding to the cur‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rent  local directory, with special handling of `~' as in zfcd.  For exam‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ple, if the current local directory is ~/foo/bar, then zfhere performs the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              effect of `zfcd ~/foo/bar'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zfdir [ -rfd ] [ - ] [ dir-options ] [ dir ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Produce a long directory listing.  The arguments dir-options and  dir  are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              passed  directly  to  the server and their effect is implementation depen‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              dent, but specifying a particular remote directory dir is  usually  possi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ble.   The output is passed through a pager given by the environment vari‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              able $PAGER, or `more' if that is not set.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The directory is usually cached for re-use.  In fact, two caches are main‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tained.  One is for use when there is no dir-options or dir, i.e.  a  full[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              listing  of  the  current remote directory; it is flushed when the current[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              remote directory changes.  The other is kept for  repeated  use  of  zfdir[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              with  the  same  arguments;  for example, repeated use of `zfdir /pub/gnu'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              will only require the directory to be retrieved on the first call.  Alter‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              natively, this cache can be re-viewed with the -r option.  As relative di‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              rectories will confuse zfdir, the -f option can be used to force the cache[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to be flushed before the directory is listed.  The option -d  will  delete[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              both  caches  without showing a directory listing; it will also delete the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cache of file names in the current remote directory, if any.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zfls [ ls-options ] [ dir ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              List files on the remote server.  With no arguments, this will  produce  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              simple list of file names for the current remote directory.  Any arguments[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              are passed directly to the server.  No pager and no caching is used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Status commands[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zftype [ type ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              With  no arguments, show the type of data to be transferred, usually ASCII[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              or binary.  With an argument, change the type: the types  `A'  or  `ASCII'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for ASCII data and `B' or `BINARY', `I' or `IMAGE' for binary data are un‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              derstood case-insensitively.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zfstat [ -v ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Show  the  status of the current or last connection, as well as the status[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of some of zftp's status variables.  With the -v option,  a  more  verbose[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              listing is produced by querying the server for its version of events, too.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Retrieving files[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The  commands  for  retrieving files all take at least two options. -G suppresses[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       remote filename expansion which would otherwise be performed  (see  below  for  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       more  detailed description of that).  -t attempts to set the modification time of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the local file to that of the remote file: see the description  of  the  function[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zfrtime below for more information.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zfget [ -Gtc ] file1 ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Retrieve  all  the  listed  files  file1 ... one at a time from the remote[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              server.  If a file contains a `/', the full name is passed to  the  remote[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              server,  but  the  file is stored locally under the name given by the part[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              after the final `/'.  The option -c (cat) forces all files to be sent as a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              single stream to standard output; in this case the -t option  has  no  ef‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              fect.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zfuget [ -Gvst ] file1 ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              As  zfget,  but only retrieve files where the version on the remote server[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              is newer (has a later modification time), or where the local file does not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              exist.  If the remote file is older but the files have different sizes, or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              if the sizes are the same but the remote file is newer, the user will usu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ally be queried.  With the option -s, the command runs silently  and  will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              always  retrieve  the  file in either of those two cases.  With the option[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              -v, the command prints more information about the files while it is  work‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ing out whether or not to transfer them.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zfcget [ -Gt ] file1 ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              As  zfget,  but  if any of the local files exists, and is shorter than the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              corresponding remote file, the command assumes that it is the result of  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              partially  completed  transfer  and  attempts  to transfer the rest of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              file.  This is useful on a poor connection which keeps failing.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Note that this requires a commonly implemented, but non-standard,  version[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of the FTP protocol, so is not guaranteed to work on all servers.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zfgcp [ -Gt ] remote-file local-file[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zfgcp [ -Gt ] rfile1 ... ldir[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  retrieves files from the remote server with arguments behaving simi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              larly to the cp command.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              In the first form, copy remote-file from the server to the local file  lo‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cal-file.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              In  the  second  form, copy all the remote files rfile1 ... into the local[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              directory ldir retaining the same basenames.  This assumes UNIX  directory[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              semantics.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Sending files[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zfput [ -r ] file1 ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Send  all the file1 ... given separately to the remote server.  If a file‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              name contains a `/', the full filename is used locally to find  the  file,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              but only the basename is used for the remote file name.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              With  the option -r, if any of the files are directories they are sent re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cursively with all their subdirectories, including  files  beginning  with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `.'.   This  requires  that the remote machine understand UNIX file seman‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tics, since `/' is used as a directory separator.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zfuput [ -vs ] file1 ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              As zfput, but only send files which are newer than  their  remote  equiva‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              lents, or if the remote file does not exist.  The logic is the same as for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zfuget, but reversed between local and remote files.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zfcput file1 ...[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              As  zfput,  but  if any remote file already exists and is shorter than the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              local equivalent, assume it is the result of an  incomplete  transfer  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              send  the rest of the file to append to the existing part.  As the FTP ap‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pend command is part of the standard set, this is in principle more likely[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to work than zfcget.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zfpcp local-file remote-file[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zfpcp lfile1 ... rdir[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This sends files to the remote server with arguments behaving similarly to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the cp command.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              With two arguments, copy local-file to the server as remote-file.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              With more than two arguments, copy all the local files lfile1 ... into the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              existing remote directory rdir retaining the same basenames.  This assumes[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              UNIX directory semantics.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              A problem arises if you attempt to use zfpcp lfile1 rdir, i.e. the  second[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              form  of  copying but with two arguments, as the command has no simple way[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of knowing if rdir corresponds to a directory or a filename.  It  attempts[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              to  resolve  this  in various ways.  First, if the rdir argument is `.' or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              `..' or ends in a slash, it is assumed to be a  directory.   Secondly,  if[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  operation  of  copying to a remote file in the first form failed, and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the remote server sends back the expected failure code 553 and a reply in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              cluding the string `Is a directory', then zfpcp will retry using the  sec‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ond form.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Closing the connection[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zfclose[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Close the connection.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Session management[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zfsession [ -lvod ] [ sessname ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Allows  you  to manage multiple FTP sessions at once.  By default, connec‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tions take place in a session called `default'; by giving the command `zf‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              session sessname' you can change to a new or existing session with a  name[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of  your choice.  The new session remembers its own connection, as well as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              associated shell parameters, and also the host/user parameters set by  zf‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              params.   Hence  you can have different sessions set up to connect to dif‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ferent hosts, each remembering the appropriate host, user and password.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              With no arguments, zfsession prints the name of the current session;  with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  option  -l  it lists all sessions which currently exist, and with the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              option -v it gives a verbose list showing the host and directory for  each[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              session,  where  the current session is marked with an asterisk.  With -o,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              it will switch to the most recent previous session.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              With -d, the given session (or else the current one)  is  removed;  every‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              thing  to do with it is completely forgotten.  If it was the only session,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              a new session called `default' is created and made current.  It is  safest[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              not to delete sessions while background commands using zftp are active.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zftransfer sess1:file1 sess2:file2[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Transfer  files  between two sessions; no local copy is made.  The file is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              read from the session sess1 as file1 and written to session sess2 as  file[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              file2;  file1  and file2 may be relative to the current directories of the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              session.  Either sess1 or sess2 may be omitted (though the colon should be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              retained if there is a possibility of a colon appearing in the file  name)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and  defaults to the current session; file2 may be omitted or may end with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              a slash, in which case the basename of file1 will be added.  The  sessions[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sess1 and sess2 must be distinct.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The operation is performed using pipes, so it is required that the connec‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tions  still  be valid in a subshell, which is not the case under versions[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of some operating systems, presumably due to a system bug.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Bookmarks[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The two functions zfmark and zfgoto allow you to `bookmark' the present  location[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       (host, user and directory) of the current FTP connection for later use.  The file[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       to  be  used  for  storing  and  retrieving  bookmarks  is given by the parameter[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       $ZFTP_BMFILE; if not set when one of the two functions is called, it will be  set[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       to  the  file .zfbkmarks in the directory where your zsh startup files live (usu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ally ~).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zfmark [ bookmark ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If given an argument, mark the current host, user and directory under  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              name  bookmark  for  later use by zfgoto.  If there is no connection open,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              use the values for the last connection immediately before it  was  closed;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              it  is  an  error if there was none.  Any existing bookmark under the same[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              name will be silently replaced.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If not given an argument, list the existing bookmarks and  the  points  to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              which  they  refer  in the form user@host:directory; this is the format in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              which they are stored, and the file may be edited directly.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zfgoto [ -n ] bookmark[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Return to the location given by bookmark, as previously set by zfmark.  If[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the location has user `ftp'  or  `anonymous',  open  the  connection  with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zfanon,  so that no password is required.  If the user and host parameters[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              match those stored for the current session, if any, those  will  be  used,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              and  again no password is required.  Otherwise a password will be prompted[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              With the option -n, the bookmark is taken to be a nickname stored  by  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ncftp  program in its bookmark file, which is assumed to be ~/.ncftp/book‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              marks.  The function works identically in other ways.  Note that there  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              no  mechanism  for adding or modifying ncftp bookmarks from the zftp func‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tions.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Other functions[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Mostly, these functions will not be called directly (apart from zfinit), but  are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       described   here  for  completeness.   You  may  wish  to  alter  zftp_chpwd  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zftp_progress, in particular.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zfinit [ -n ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              As described above, this is used to initialize the zftp  function  system.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The -n option should be used if the zftp command is already built into the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              shell.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zfautocheck [ -dn ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This function is called to implement automatic reopening behaviour, as de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              scribed  in more detail below.  The options must appear in the first argu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ment; -n prevents the command from changing to the old directory, while -d[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              prevents it from setting the variable do_close, which it otherwise does as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              a flag for automatically closing the connection  after  a  transfer.   The[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              host  and  directory  for  the  last  session  are  stored in the variable[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              $zflastsession, but the internal host/user/password parameters  must  also[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              be correctly set.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zfcd_match prefix suffix[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  performs  matching for completion of remote directory names.  If the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              remote server is UNIX, it will attempt to persuade the server to list  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              remote  directory  with  subdirectories marked, which usually works but is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              not guaranteed.  On other hosts it simply calls zfget_match and hence com‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              pletes all files, not just directories.  On some systems, directories  may[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              not even look like filenames.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zfget_match prefix suffix[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  performs  matching  for  completion  of remote filenames.  It caches[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              files  for  the  current  directory  (only)   in   the   shell   parameter[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              $zftp_fcache.  It is in the form to be called by the -K option of compctl,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              but  also  works  when called from a widget-style completion function with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              prefix and suffix set appropriately.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zfrglob varname[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Perform remote globbing, as describes in more detail  below.   varname  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the  name  of  a  variable containing the pattern to be expanded; if there[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              were any matches, the same variable will be set to  the  expanded  set  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              filenames on return.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zfrtime lfile rfile [ time ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Set  the local file lfile to have the same modification time as the remote[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              file rfile, or the explicit time time in FTP format CCYYMMDDhhmmSS for the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              GMT timezone.  This uses the shell's zsh/datetime module  to  perform  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              conversion from GMT to local time.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zftp_chpwd[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  function  is called every time a connection is opened, or closed, or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the remote directory changes.  This version alters the  title  bar  of  an[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              xterm-compatible or sun-cmd terminal emulator to reflect the local and re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              mote  hostnames and current directories.  It works best when combined with[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the function chpwd.  In particular, a function of the form[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     chpwd() {[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                       if [[ -n $ZFTP_USER ]]; then[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                         zftp_chpwd[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                       else[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                         # usual chpwd e.g put host:directory in title bar[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                       fi[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     }[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              fits in well.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zftp_progress[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This function shows the status of the transfer.  It will  not  write  any‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              thing  unless  the output is going to a terminal; however, if you transfer[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              files in the background, you should turn off progress reports by hand  us‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ing `zstyle ':zftp:*' progress none'.  Note also that if you alter it, any[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              output  must  be to standard error, as standard output may be a file being[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              received.  The form of the progress meter, or whether it is used  at  all,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              can  be configured without altering the function, as described in the next[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              section.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zffcache[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This is used to implement caching of files in the  current  directory  for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              each session separately.  It is used by zfget_match and zfrglob.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mMISCELLANEOUS FEATURES[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m   Configuration[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Various  styles are available using the standard shell style mechanism, described[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       in zshmodules(1). Briefly, the command `zstyle ':zftp:*' style value  ...'.   de‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       fines  the  style to have value value; more than one value may be given, although[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       that is not useful in the cases described here.  These values will then  be  used[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       throughout  the  zftp function system.  For more precise control, the first argu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ment, which gives a pattern that matches contexts in which the style applies, can[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       be modified to include a particular function, as for example  `:zftp:zfget':  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       style  will  then have the given value only in the zfget function, and will over‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ride styles set under `:zftp:*'.  Note that only the top level function name,  as[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       called  by  the user, is used; calling of lower level functions is transparent to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the user.  Hence modifications to the title bar in zftp_chpwd  use  the  contexts[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       :zftp:zfopen,  :zftp:zfcd, etc., depending where it was called from.  The follow‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ing styles are understood:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       progress[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Controls the way that zftp_progress reports on the progress of a transfer.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If empty, unset, or `none', no progress report is made; if `bar' a growing[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              bar of inverse video is shown; if `percent' (or any other  string,  though[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              this  may  change  in  future),  the percentage of the file transferred is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              shown.  The bar meter requires that the width of the terminal be available[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              via the $COLUMNS parameter (normally this is set automatically).   If  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              size  of  the file being transferred is not available, bar and percent me‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ters will simply show the number of bytes transferred so far.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              When zfinit is run, if this style is not defined for the context  :zftp:*,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              it will be set to `bar'.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       update Specifies  the minimum time interval between updates of the progress meter[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              in seconds.  No update is made unless new data has been received,  so  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              actual time interval is limited only by $ZFTP_TIMEOUT.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              As described for progress, zfinit will force this to default to 1.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       remote-glob[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If  set  to  `1',  `yes' or `true', filename generation (globbing) is per‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              formed on the remote machine instead of by zsh itself; see below.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       titlebar[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              If set to `1', `yes' or `true', zftp_chpwd will put the  remote  host  and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              remote  directory into the titlebar of terminal emulators such as xterm or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              sun-cmd that allow this.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              As described for progress, zfinit will force this to default to 1.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       chpwd  If set to `1' `yes' or `true', zftp_chpwd will  call  the  function  chpwd[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              when  a  connection  is closed.  This is useful if the remote host details[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              were put into the terminal title bar by zftp_chpwd and  your  usual  chpwd[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              also modifies the title bar.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              When  zfinit  is  run, it will determine whether chpwd exists and if so it[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              will set the default value for the style to 1 if none exists already.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Note that there is also an associative array zfconfig which contains values  used[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       by the function system.  This should not be modified or overwritten.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Remote globbing[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The  commands for retrieving files usually perform filename generation (globbing)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       on their arguments; this can be turned off by passing the option -G  to  each  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the  commands.  Normally this operates by retrieving a complete list of files for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the directory in question, then matching these locally against the  pattern  sup‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       plied.   This  has  the advantage that the full range of zsh patterns (respecting[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the setting of the option EXTENDED_GLOB) can be used.  However, it means that the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       directory part of a filename will not be expanded and must be given exactly.   If[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the  remote  server does not support the UNIX directory semantics, directory han‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       dling is problematic and it is recommended that globbing only be used within  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       current  directory.   The  list  of files in the current directory, if retrieved,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       will be cached, so that subsequent globs in the same directory without an  inter‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       vening zfcd are much faster.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       If the remote-glob style (see above) is set, globbing is instead performed on the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       remote  host:  the  server is asked for a list of matching files.  This is highly[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       dependent on how the server is implemented, though typically  UNIX  servers  will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       provide support for basic glob patterns.  This may in some cases be faster, as it[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       avoids retrieving the entire list of directory contents.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Automatic and temporary reopening[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       As  described for the zfopen command, a subsequent zfopen with no parameters will[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       reopen the connection to the last host (this includes connections made  with  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zfanon  command).   Opened  in this fashion, the connection starts in the default[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       remote directory and will remain open until explicitly closed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Automatic re-opening is also available.  If a connection is  not  currently  open[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       and  a command requiring a connection is given, the last connection is implicitly[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       reopened.  In this case the directory which was current when the  connection  was[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       closed  again becomes the current directory (unless, of course, the command given[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       changes it).  Automatic reopening will also take  place  if  the  connection  was[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       close  by  the  remote  server  for  whatever reason (e.g. a timeout).  It is not[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       available if the -1 option to zfopen or zfanon was used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Furthermore, if the command issued is a file transfer,  the  connection  will  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       closed after the transfer is finished, hence providing a one-shot mode for trans‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       fers.  This does not apply to directory changing or listing commands; for example[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       a  zfdir may reopen a connection but will leave it open.  Also, automatic closure[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       will only ever happen in the same command as automatic opening, i.e a  zfdir  di‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       rectly followed by a zfget will never close the connection automatically.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Information  about  the previous connection is given by the zfstat function.  So,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       for example, if that reports:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Session:        default[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Not connected.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Last session:   ftp.bar.com:/pub/textfiles[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       then  the  command  zfget  file.txt  will  attempt  to  reopen  a  connection  to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ftp.bar.com, retrieve the file /pub/textfiles/file.txt, and immediately close the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       connection  again.   On  the other hand, zfcd ..  will open the connection in the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       directory /pub and leave it open.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Note that all the above is local to each session; if you  return  to  a  previous[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       session, the connection for that session is the one which will be reopened.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Completion[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Completion of local and remote files, directories, sessions and bookmarks is sup‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ported.   The  older,  compctl-style completion is defined when zfinit is called;[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       support for the new widget-based completion system is provided  in  the  function[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Completion/Zsh/Command/_zftp,  which should be installed with the other functions[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       of the completion system and hence should automatically be available.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mzsh 5.9                               May 14, 2022                         ZSHZFTPSYS(1)[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────[0m
[38;2;248;248;242mZSHCONTRIB(1)                    General Commands Manual                   ZSHCONTRIB(1)[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mNAME[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zshcontrib - user contributions to zsh[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mDESCRIPTION[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The Zsh source distribution includes a number of items contributed  by  the  user[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       community.   These  are  not  inherently a part of the shell, and some may not be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       available in every zsh installation.  The most significant  of  these  are  docu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       mented  here.   For  documentation on other contributed items such as shell func‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tions, look for comments in the function source files.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242mUTILITIES[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m   Accessing On-Line Help[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The key sequence ESC h is normally bound by ZLE to execute  the  run-help  widget[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       (see  zshzle(1)).   This  invokes the run-help command with the command word from[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the current input line as its argument.  By default, run-help is an alias for the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       man command, so this often fails when the command word is a shell  builtin  or  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       user-defined  function.   By  redefining  the run-help alias, one can improve the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       on-line help provided by the shell.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The helpfiles utility, found in the Util directory of the distribution, is a Perl[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       program that can be used to process the zsh manual to  produce  a  separate  help[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       file  for  each shell builtin and for many other shell features as well.  The au‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       toloadable run-help function, found in Functions/Misc, searches for  these  help‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       files and performs several other tests to produce the most complete help possible[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       for the command.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Help  files  are  installed  by  default  to  a subdirectory of /usr/share/zsh or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       /usr/local/share/zsh.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       To create your own help files with helpfiles, choose or create a directory  where[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the  individual  command  help  files will reside.  For example, you might choose[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ~/zsh_help.  If you unpacked the zsh distribution in  your  home  directory,  you[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       would use the commands:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              mkdir ~/zsh_help[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              perl ~/zsh-5.9/Util/helpfiles ~/zsh_help[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The  HELPDIR  parameter tells run-help where to look for the help files. When un‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       set, it uses the default installation path.  To use your own set of  help  files,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       set this to the appropriate path in one of your startup files:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              HELPDIR=~/zsh_help[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       To  use the run-help function, you need to add lines something like the following[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       to your .zshrc or equivalent startup file:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              unalias run-help[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              autoload run-help[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Note that in order for `autoload run-help' to work, the run-help file must be  in[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       one  of the directories named in your fpath array (see zshparam(1)).  This should[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       already be the case if you have a standard zsh installation; if it is  not,  copy[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Functions/Misc/run-help to an appropriate directory.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Recompiling Functions[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       If  you  frequently  edit your zsh functions, or periodically update your zsh in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       stallation to track the latest developments, you may find that  function  digests[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       compiled with the zcompile builtin are frequently out of date with respect to the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       function  source  files.  This is not usually a problem, because zsh always looks[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       for the newest file when loading a  function,  but  it  may  cause  slower  shell[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       startup  and  function  loading.  Also, if a digest file is explicitly used as an[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       element of fpath, zsh won't check whether any of its source files has changed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The zrecompile autoloadable function, found in Functions/Misc,  can  be  used  to[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       keep function digests up to date.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       zrecompile [ -qt ] [ name ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       zrecompile [ -qt ] -p arg ... [ -- arg ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              This  tries  to  find  *.zwc files and automatically re-compile them if at[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              least one of the original files is newer than  the  compiled  file.   This[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              works only if the names stored in the compiled files are full paths or are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              relative to the directory that contains the .zwc file.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              In the first form, each name is the name of a compiled file or a directory[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              containing *.zwc files that should be checked.  If no arguments are given,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              the directories and *.zwc files in fpath are used.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              When -t is given, no compilation is performed, but a return status of zero[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (true)  is set if there are files that need to be re-compiled and non-zero[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              (false) otherwise.  The -q option quiets the chatty output that  describes[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              what zrecompile is doing.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Without  the -t option, the return status is zero if all files that needed[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              re-compilation could be compiled and non-zero if compilation for at  least[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              one of the files failed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If the -p option is given, the args are interpreted as one or more sets of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              arguments for zcompile, separated by `--'.  For example:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m                     zrecompile -p \[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                                -R ~/.zshrc -- \[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                                -M ~/.zcompdump -- \[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                                ~/zsh/comp.zwc ~/zsh/Completion/*/_*[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              This compiles ~/.zshrc into ~/.zshrc.zwc if that doesn't exist or if it is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              older  than ~/.zshrc. The compiled file will be marked for reading instead[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              of mapping. The same is done for ~/.zcompdump  and  ~/.zcompdump.zwc,  but[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              this  compiled  file  is  marked for mapping. The last line re-creates the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              file ~/zsh/comp.zwc if any of the files  matching  the  given  pattern  is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              newer than it.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              Without the -p option, zrecompile does not create function digests that do[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              not already exist, nor does it add new functions to the digest.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The  following shell loop is an example of a method for creating function digests[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       for all functions in your fpath, assuming that you have write permission  to  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       directories:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              for ((i=1; i <= $#fpath; ++i)); do[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                dir=$fpath[i][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                zwc=${dir:t}.zwc[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                if [[ $dir == (.|..) || $dir == (.|..)/* ]]; then[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                  continue[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                fi[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                files=($dir/*(N-.))[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                if [[ -w $dir:h && -n $files ]]; then[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                  files=(${${(M)files%/*/*}#/})[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                  if ( cd $dir:h &&[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                       zrecompile -p -U -z $zwc $files ); then[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                    fpath[i]=$fpath[i].zwc[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                  fi[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                fi[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              done[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The  -U and -z options are appropriate for functions in the default zsh installa‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tion fpath; you may need to use different options for your personal function  di‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       rectories.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Once  the digests have been created and your fpath modified to refer to them, you[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       can keep them up to date by running zrecompile with no arguments.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Keyboard Definition[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       The large number of possible combinations of keyboards, workstations,  terminals,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       emulators,  and  window  systems makes it impossible for zsh to have built-in key[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       bindings for every situation.  The zkbd utility,  found  in  Functions/Misc,  can[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       help you quickly create key bindings for your configuration.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Run zkbd either as an autoloaded function, or as a shell script:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              zsh -f ~/zsh-5.9/Functions/Misc/zkbd[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       When  you run zkbd, it first asks you to enter your terminal type; if the default[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       it offers is correct, just press return.  It then asks you to press a  number  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       different  keys  to determine characteristics of your keyboard and terminal; zkbd[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       warns you if it finds anything out of the ordinary, such as  a  Delete  key  that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       sends neither ^H nor ^?.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The keystrokes read by zkbd are recorded as a definition for an associative array[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       named key, written to a file in the subdirectory .zkbd within either your HOME or[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ZDOTDIR  directory.   The  name of the file is composed from the TERM, VENDOR and[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       OSTYPE parameters, joined by hyphens.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       You may read this file into your .zshrc or another startup file with the `source'[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       or `.' commands, then reference the key parameter in bindkey commands, like this:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              source ${ZDOTDIR:-$HOME}/.zkbd/$TERM-$VENDOR-$OSTYPE[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              [[ -n ${key[Left]} ]] && bindkey "${key[Left]}" backward-char[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              [[ -n ${key[Right]} ]] && bindkey "${key[Right]}" forward-char[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              # etc.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       Note that in order for `autoload zkbd' to work, the zkdb file must be in  one  of[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       the directories named in your fpath array (see zshparam(1)).  This should already[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       be  the  case  if  you have a standard zsh installation; if it is not, copy Func‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       tions/Misc/zkbd to an appropriate directory.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Dumping Shell State[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       Occasionally you may encounter what appears to be a bug in  the  shell,  particu‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       larly  if  you are using a beta version of zsh or a development release.  Usually[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       it is sufficient to send a description of the problem to one of the  zsh  mailing[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       lists (see zsh(1)), but sometimes one of the zsh developers will need to recreate[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       your environment in order to track the problem down.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       The  script  named  reporter, found in the Util directory of the distribution, is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       provided for this purpose.  (It is also possible to autoload  reporter,  but  re‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       porter  is  not  installed  in fpath by default.)  This script outputs a detailed[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       dump of the shell state, in the form of another script that can be read with `zsh[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       -f' to recreate that state.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       To use reporter, read the script into your shell with the `.' command  and  redi‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       rect the output into a file:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              . ~/zsh-5.9/Util/reporter > zsh.report[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       You  should check the zsh.report file for any sensitive information such as pass‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       words and delete them by hand before sending the script to the developers.  Also,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       as the output can be voluminous, it's best to wait for the developers to ask  for[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       this information before sending it.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       You  can  also  use  reporter  to dump only a subset of the shell state.  This is[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       sometimes useful for creating startup files for the first time.  Most of the out‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       put from reporter is far more detailed than usually is necessary  for  a  startup[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       file, but the aliases, options, and zstyles states may be useful because they in‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       clude  only  changes  from the defaults.  The bindings state may be useful if you[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       have created any of your own keymaps,  because  reporter  arranges  to  dump  the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       keymap creation commands as well as the bindings for every keymap.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       As is usual with automated tools, if you create a startup file with reporter, you[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       should  edit the results to remove unnecessary commands.  Note that if you're us‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       ing the new completion system, you should not dump the functions  state  to  your[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       startup  files  with  reporter;  use  the compdump function instead (see zshcomp‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       sys(1)).[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       reporter [ state ... ][0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Print to standard output the indicated subset of the current shell  state.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              The state arguments may be one or more of:[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              all    Output everything listed below.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              aliases[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Output alias definitions.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              bindings[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Output ZLE key maps and bindings.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              completion[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Output  old-style  compctl  commands.  New completion is covered by[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     functions and zstyles.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              functions[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Output autoloads and function definitions.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              limits Output limit commands.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              options[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Output setopt commands.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              styles Same as zstyles.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              variables[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Output shell parameter assignments, plus export  commands  for  any[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     environment variables.[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zstyles[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m                     Output zstyle commands.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If the state is omitted, all is assumed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       With the exception of `all', every state can be abbreviated by any prefix, even a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       single letter; thus a is the same as aliases, z is the same as zstyles, etc.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m   Manipulating Hook Functions[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m       add-zsh-hook [ -L | -dD ] [ -Uzk ] hook function[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Several  functions  are  special to the shell, as described in the section[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              SPECIAL FUNCTIONS, see zshmisc(1), in that they are  automatically  called[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              at  specific  points during shell execution.  Each has an associated array[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              consisting of names of functions to be called at the same point; these are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              so-called `hook functions'.  The shell function  add-zsh-hook  provides  a[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              simple way of adding or removing functions from the array.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              hook  is  one of chpwd, periodic, precmd, preexec, zshaddhistory, zshexit,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              or zsh_directory_name, the  special  functions  in  question.   Note  that[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              zsh_directory_name  is called in a different way from the other functions,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              but may still be manipulated as a hook.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              function is name of an ordinary shell function.  If no options  are  given[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              this  will  be added to the array of functions to be executed in the given[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              context.  Functions are invoked in the order they were added.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If the option -L is given, the current values  for  the  hook  arrays  are[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              listed with typeset.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If the option -d is given, the function is removed from the array of func‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tions to be executed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              If  the  option  -D is given, the function is treated as a pattern and any[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              matching names of functions are removed from the array of functions to  be[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              executed.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              The  options  -U,  -z and -k are passed as arguments to autoload for func‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              tion.  For functions contributed with zsh, the options -Uz  are  appropri‐[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              ate.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m       add-zle-hook-widget [ -L | -dD ] [ -Uzk ] hook widgetname[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              Several  widget  names are special to the line editor, as described in the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              section Special Widgets, see zshzle(1), in  that  they  are  automatically[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              called at specific points during editing.  Unlike function hooks, these do[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              not  use  a predefined array of other names to call at the same point; the[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              shell function add-zle-hook-widget maintains a similar array and  arranges[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              for the special widget to invoke those additional widgets.[0m

[38;2;248;248;242m              hook  is  one of isearch-exit, isearch-update, line-pre-redraw, line-init,[0m
[38;2;248;248;242m              line-finish, history-line-set, or keymap-select, corresponding to each  of[0m
